Emotional Awareness Prompts Optional reminders such as: «“This track is currently placed in your Emotional Boundary playlist.”» Or «“You previously chose to avoid this song during regular listening sessions.”» The prompts should remain subtle and respectful. 5. Recovery-Oriented Redirection Instead of replaying emotionally harmful loops, Spotify could gently redirect users toward: - Calmer music - Recovery playlists - Grounding sounds - Confidence-building tracks - New music discovery - Emotionally stabilizing recommendations Why This Matters Music strongly affects memory, emotional conditioning, and mental state. Many people unintentionally replay songs that reinforce: - Emotional dependency - Rumination - Depression - Heartbreak cycles - Substance cravings - Isolation A system that helps separate emotionally harmful patterns from daily listening could improve digital wellbeing while still respecting artistic freedom. Important Considerations - All emotional data should remain private and encrypted. - Users should always maintain full control over their music. - Songs should never be globally labeled as “negative.” - Emotional responses are personal and contextual. - The feature should support self-awareness, not censorship. Possible Feature Names - Emotional Boundaries - Recovery Mode - Mindful Listening - Safe Listening - Reset Playlist - Emotional Filter - Reflection Mode Closing Thought Sometimes healing is not about deleting memories, but preventing them from constantly reappearing when you’re trying to move forward. Music platforms already shape emotions through algorithms — this feature would simply give users more control over that emotional influence. Additional Feature Clarification: “Blocked Playlist” System The feature could work as a dedicated private “Blocked Playlist” where users can place songs they no longer want appearing in their regular listening experience. Instead of deleting or permanently hiding the music, Spotify would: - Compile these songs into a separate reviewable playlist - Prevent them from appearing in autoplay - Exclude them from Smart Shuffle - Remove them from recommendations and mood mixes - Avoid resurfacing them during casual listening sessions Users would still retain full access to the songs and could revisit the playlist anytime intentionally. This creates a healthier balance between: - preserving meaningful music memories - and preventing unwanted emotional triggering during everyday listening Key Difference From Existing Features Unlike simply hiding a song, this system would function more like: - an emotional boundary list - a controlled archive - or a personal “do not auto-surface” collection The important part is that Spotify’s algorithm would actively understand: «“Do not casually reintroduce these songs into my listening experience.”» Suggested User Controls Users could: - Add or remove songs anytime - Review blocked songs privately - Restore songs back into normal recommendations - Set temporary or permanent blocking - Block entire artists, albums, or playlists if desired Why It Matters Many users do not necessarily want to erase certain songs permanently. Some songs carry emotional significance, but users may not want them unexpectedly appearing during vulnerable moments, commutes, late nights, workouts, or random shuffle sessions. This feature would give users healthier emotional control over algorithmic resurfacing while still respecting personal choice and music ownership. Thank you for your time and consideration. I appreciate the work Spotify continues to do in creating deeply personal listening experiences for millions of users worldwide.

records.text: Music-taste test to enhance personalisation Spotify should offer a dedicated “ music taste test ” to improve recommendation accuracy , since the current algorithm often feels inconsistent. The feature could live in its own tab and begin by showing users a stream of completely random songs . Instead of a simple like/dislike system, users would rate each song across five categories : “Horrible,” “Bad,” “Neutral,” “Good,” and “Great.” The “ Great ” rating should carry especially high weight , because that is what users actually want to hear more of. The other ratings would help the algorithm learn the boundaries between levels of preference : what makes a song “Good” rather than “Great,” what separates “Neutral” from “Bad,” and which elements consistently trigger strong rejection or strong approval . To make this genuinely effective, Spotify should not rely mainly on broad genres or categories . It should analyze finer details such as instrumentation, dynamics, rhythmic patterns, harmonic structure, complexity, tempo, and mood . The goal is for the system to understand taste at a deeper level, not just match surface-level labels . What this does? The algorithm takes into consideration the songs actual character, not their popularity, genre, trendiness etc. In short: What actually matters. The test should also never stop completely . From time to time, Spotify should introduce new and slightly unfamiliar songs to broaden the user profile and prevent the algorithm from becoming too narrow. It could measure how long users listen, whether they skip quickly, and how they rate these more experimental recommendations. That would help the system learn not only what users already like, but also what they are open to discovering . The format could be inspired by Instagram Reels : an endless, swipe-based flow of suggestions that becomes more personalized over time. The difference is that, instead of passive scrolling, it would be an active taste evaluation tool . In other words: continuous suggestions, more precise feedback, and deeper pattern recognition, combined with occasional novelty to keep the model from stagnating . Why? Because then, Spotify can suggest Playlists by other users that fit the personalised taste profile best and in general suggest new songs more accurately. This would have huge effects, because accurate recommendations are THE key to a more satisfying experience. A big flaw right now , is the exaggerated repetition . People want NEW songs. It makes no sense to recommend what they know already, unless the last time they listened to them is years ago. Spotify should furthermore distinguish between active and passive listening, because background playback currently distorts what the algorithm interprets as actual preference. That’s why my suggestion actually matters: the active rating design ensures representation of the users actual preferences . The Core of my suggestions in summary: Spotify should learn what users truly love — and why they love it. 1.Spotify should introduce a dedicated music taste test that collects explicit, high-quality feedback instead of relying mostly on passive listening data. 2.Users could rate random songs on a detailed scale from “Horrible” to “Great,” giving the algorithm a much clearer picture of real preference. 3.That matters because background listening can distort the data: a song played while studying, talking, or falling asleep is not the same as a song actively chosen and enjoyed. 4.Spotify should therefore distinguish between passive playback and genuine engagement, and weight the latter much more heavily. 5. At the same time, the system should go beyond broad genres and surface labels. It should analyze the actual musical structure of songs — instrumentation, dynamics, rhythm, harmony, tempo, complexity, and mood — so it understands taste at a deeper level rather than just matching categories or popularity. 6.The test should remain ongoing, with occasional unfamiliar songs to keep broadening the profile and reduce repetition. Spoiler (Highlight to read) In the end, the goal is simple: Spotify should stop guessing from noisy signals and instead learn true user preference with precision, depth, and variety. In the end, the goal is simple: Spotify should stop guessing from noisy signals and instead learn true user preference with precision, depth, and variety.

records.text: Fast Account Switching in the Spotify App 1. Context Many households currently use a single Spotify Premium account across multiple devices. While this allows centralized control through Spotify Connect, it also creates limitations: only one stream can play at a time, and all users share the same recommendations, playlists, and history. To improve the experience, I plan to move to a Family Plan with separate accounts: fatherAccount motherAccount child1Account child2Account carAccount homeAccount (admin) Each account would be assigned to a specific device (phones, tablets, Google Home speakers, Android Auto, etc.). However, managing these accounts becomes cumbersome because switching between them requires logging out and logging back in manually. 2. Problem Spotify currently does not support quick account switching. This creates friction in several everyday scenarios: A. At home Google Home speakers use homeAccount (admin) . If music is playing in a room, other family members cannot pause, change volume, or modify playback from their own devices unless they log out and log in with the homeAccount. B. In the car Android Auto restricts interaction for safety reasons. With a dedicated carAccount , passengers could safely search and control playback from their own phones — but only if switching accounts were fast and seamless. C. Multi‑speaker households Modern homes often include multiple smart speakers and even speaker groups . With separate accounts, each room could play different music without interference. For example: In a bedroom: A Google Home speaker using homeAccount (admin) can start playback via voice (“Ok Google, play Spotify”) without affecting other rooms. In the living room: Using the Spotify app on a phone linked to fatherAccount , a user could send a specific song to a particular speaker or speaker group without logging out or interrupting playback elsewhere. Today, this flexibility is limited by the need to constantly switch accounts manually. 3. Requested Feature: Fast Account Switch I propose that Spotify introduce: a) Multi‑account registration within the app Allow users to store several Spotify accounts on the same device. b) A fast, one‑tap account switcher Similar to Instagram, YouTube, or Twitter, enabling instant switching without re‑authentication. c) Device control per active account Once an account is selected, the user can control all devices linked to that account via Spotify Connect. 4. Benefits A. For individual users Seamless control of shared devices without logging in and out. Personalized recommendations preserved for each account. Safer and more convenient music control in the car. Full flexibility to manage smart speakers and speaker groups. Significant time savings in daily use. B. For families and shared households Smooth management of multiple speakers and rooms. Adults can supervise or stop playback without accessing children’s accounts. Easier adoption of separate profiles for each family member. True simultaneous playback across different rooms and devices. C. For Spotify (strategic advantages) A strong incentive for users to migrate to the Family Plan. Many households currently share a single account because switching is inconvenient. A fast account switcher removes this barrier and encourages users to upgrade. Reduced support issues related to account conflicts and device takeover. Increased usage of Spotify Connect across smart homes and vehicles. Competitive advantage over platforms that already support multi‑account switching. Better integration with the growing smart‑home ecosystem. 5. Conclusion A Fast Account Switch feature would significantly enhance the user experience, especially in multi‑device and multi‑speaker households. It would streamline control of shared devices, improve safety and convenience in vehicles, and unlock the full potential of smart‑home audio setups. Most importantly, it would serve as a powerful incentive for users to upgrade to the Spotify Family Plan , benefiting both customers and Spotify.
records.author: rmompo

records.text: Re: Ai generated content should be labeled. https://community.spotify.com/t5/Live-Ideas/Mark-Disable-AI-Generated-Songs/idi-p/6641329/page/5#comments Like the Main Case. There shouldnt be an Issue to Label those songs. And if you have an Label of those Songs, then there should be an Option to turn it off fully. Checked the Main Request .. at start of 2025 we had maybe 5-6 Sites of Users who complains ( again .. already way to much), now at Start of 2025 we are on Site 115? 116? Not Everyone wants AI Music. For me it ruins Core Spotify Features like the "made yor you" Playlists. "Your Weekly Songs" - the 30 Songs 1x per Week, was my main source of new Music, as the Algorythm was so good. I also used Release Radar. Now they changed the "Your Weekly Songs" to 5x Genres * 30 songs, and the "normal" Weekly. We now have 180 Songs in Weekly, like written in the other Thread .. at least 30 are AI Songs. instead of Joy hearing new Music, you get old Music with 1000 Plays and doesnt match any of your "Music" and many AI Songs which Topics are Religion, Political Stuff of the US ( Kirks Death, Orange Man and so on, while im not intrested in that, nor am i American). Shared Songs, liked Songs, Songs used in Playlists wont show up in Weekly Recommendations, but AI Songs spawn .. and that increases when you do block other AI Artist. I dont complain about the Funny Songs where Michael Jackson is singing Barbie Girl. But when AI is using Artists Names same as an Original Artist, or very close to it, using Pictures to make everyone believe " yeah its just like the Original" then i have an Problem with it .. Deeps an Artist with around 1 Million monthly Listeners, i have like 4-5 liked songs. Had "Deeps" Songs in my Weekly, but it wasnt the Original, it was an AI Clone. Same with "linkon Mark" - Name looks similar to an very known Band. The Pictures used look like an AI drawing of that famous Band and Front Man who isnt alive anymore. ( dont even listen to that music). Others with the same Artists name show up in Weekly, while the Original Artist has new Songs and isnt showing up anywhere, which he should .. when i literally listen to that Artist. Its not Joy to listen Spotify Playlists, its more like Work.. You cant enjoy to just start the Playlist and listen, when you now the next Songs are AI, sound the same, and you cant do anything about it. Like written on OG Post, if you can Label it ( Deezer has no issue to label that) .. then there should also be an Option for EVERYONE to turn that stuff off.
records.author: Malic90

records.text: Re: How do I stop spotify adding songs to my playlist Hi there, @Journeyjackson , This behavior might be stemming from Smart Shuffle - the feature adds song recommendations to the queue which, if you like, can save directly in your playlist or Liked Songs. Recommended songs are indicated with a special green icon next to the artist's name (left) . This article goes in-depth on the topic + how to potentially disable Smart Shuffle, I'd recommend checking it out. Below are a few other common examples where you may encounter the app playing songs you haven't personally added to your playlists. Click the arrow to expand the list: Spoiler (Highlight to read) Leftover songs in the queue - Manually added, most likely during a past listening session. Premium users can remove those with the 'Clear queue' button. Autoplay - When the current queue ends, Autoplay automatically generates a new one with similar content so that the playback doesn't stop. You can toggle it off in the app's 'Settings' > 'Playback'. Check 3rd-party apps - Third-party services linked to your Spotify account may be adding songs to your playlists. It's recommended you remove any apps you don't recognize to keep your account secure. Active Jam session - You might have an active Jam session running. You can end it either from the Spotify Connect menu or from the Queue . Collaborative playlists - Someone else whom you shared the playlist with could've added the song. You can double-check this by opening the playlist's contents - you'll find the collaborator person who added the song next to its title. Leftover songs in the queue - Manually added, most likely during a past listening session. Premium users can remove those with the 'Clear queue' button. Autoplay - When the current queue ends, Autoplay automatically generates a new one with similar content so that the playback doesn't stop. You can toggle it off in the app's 'Settings' > 'Playback'. Check 3rd-party apps - Third-party services linked to your Spotify account may be adding songs to your playlists. It's recommended you remove any apps you don't recognize to keep your account secure. Active Jam session - You might have an active Jam session running. You can end it either from the Spotify Connect menu or from the Queue. Collaborative playlists - Someone else whom you shared the playlist with could've added the song. You can double-check this by opening the playlist's contents - you'll find the collaborator person who added the song next to its title. Hope this clears things up. In case you need additional assistance, feel free to share a screenshot / short screen recording which captures the described behavior in action so we can take a direct look at what's happening on your end. Cheers 🤘

records.text: Expand Radio & Mix Playlists to 100 Songs I would like to suggest improving Spotify’s official Radio and Mix playlists by increasing their size from 50 songs to 100 songs. At the moment, many Radio and Mix playlists feel too short and repetitive. In my experience, playlists are often built from approximately 80–90% songs that are already in my liked songs, favorites, or listening history, while only 10–15% are truly new recommendations. Because of this, Radio and Mix playlists often do not feel like discovery tools anymore. Instead, they feel like a shorter repeated version of my own library. Current Problems 1. Too much repetition When playlists only contain 50 tracks, Spotify quickly fills them with songs I already know well. This makes the experience predictable. After listening to several radios or mixes, I often see the same songs repeated again and again. 2. Not enough music discovery One of the main reasons people use Radio is to discover similar artists, genres, and tracks. But with only a small number of songs and a high percentage of already liked tracks, there is very little room for new recommendations. This reduces the value of Radio playlists. 3. Short listening session A 50-song playlist can finish relatively quickly, especially for users who listen for many hours. Longer playlists would allow users to listen longer without repetition, avoid manually switching playlists, and stay in one mood or genre for more time. Suggested Solution Increase official Spotify Radio and Mix playlists from 50 songs to 100 songs. A suggested playlist structure could be: 50–60% familiar songs from liked songs, favorites, and listening history. 40–50% new recommendations based on listening taste, genre, artists, mood, and audio similarity. This balance would keep playlists comfortable but also exciting. Benefits for Users With 100-song playlists, users would get more variety, less repetition, more new discoveries, longer uninterrupted listening sessions, and a stronger feeling that Radio is personalized but still exploratory. Radio should feel like an intelligent music discovery engine, not a repeated copy of my existing library. Additional Optional Features Spotify could also let users customize Radio behavior: More Familiar mode — more favorite songs. Balanced mode — equal familiar and new songs. More Discovery mode — mostly new recommendations. This would give users more control over their listening experience. Final Suggestion Please consider increasing official Radio and Mix playlists from 50 to 100 songs. This simple change would significantly improve playlist quality, reduce repetition, and make Spotify much better for discovering new music while still keeping personalization.
records.author: OBIDJON_ABDIEV

support the bands that you love Bandcamp is the best place Not only do they pay the majority of the purchase to the band members but you actually also own the music you can download the mp3 file and records.text: I'm fed up with these glitchy playlists Plan Free Country US Device Moto G Power 25, Cricket Debut s2, Samsung Galaxy S25 Operating System Android 16 My Question or Issue I have so, so many. I wrote a whole @ss essay about this earlier and it got deleted when I tried to send it. Damn, you guys can't even run an online website. Anyways, I don't want to write the whole thing again, so I'm just gonna summarize the issues: 80% of the time, my main playlist only plays one artist, typically Juice WRLD but sometimes other artists. I have over 1,200 songs on the playlist and tons of artists, there is no reason for this to happen. Sometimes it will also only play songs from one album. It also adds songs I have never added or even listened to. (And no, it's not recommendations or in the "We added" section, they were smack dab in the middle of my playlist like I added them myself. When I unadd them, they get re-added as soon as I leave. The worst part is they aren't even songs I would listen to. I listen to 75% rap, and the rest is a mix of R&B, (American) Pop, Rock, and alternative. I don't listen to any K-Pop, and those are the songs they always add. I'm so sick of having to use all my skips on K-Pop songs I've never heard, and that's only on the rare occasions my playlist actually works and doesn't just play Juice WRLD. It's gotten to the point where I had to make a backup playlist that doesn't have Juice in it. Viewing albums is completely wack, it shuffles the order, adds songs that aren't on the album, then removes songs that are on the album. The library is also annoying, as it lists random artists I'm following before any of my playlists or albums. I'm finally just fed up with this f**k @ss playlist glitches. With all the money you make from overpriced subscriptions and giving out 4-5 ads for every 2 songs, you should be able to fix these issues easily. But they've been around for months. Don't try to tell me that it's just me, because I've tried logging out, restarting the app, deleting the app, trying a different phone, everything. Fix this app soon or I'm switching to Apple Music.
records.author: oj_mills

records.text: Give users more control over podcast recommendations and reduce clickbait-style content Feedback: One of the things I value about Spotify is the possibility of discovering high-quality music, podcasts, and long-form audio. But I’m increasingly concerned that the recommendation experience, especially around podcasts and video podcasts, is moving toward the same attention-maximizing pattern as YouTube, Instagram, or TikTok. I do not want Spotify to become another addictive feed optimized for whatever is most clickable, sensational, or emotionally engaging. I want recommendations that respect my intent, my time, and my intellectual standards. A recurring issue is that low-quality, clickbait-style podcasts or videos are recommended repeatedly. By “low quality,” I mean content that feels sensationalized, shallow, or not intellectually rigorous, even if it is highly engaging or popular. I would genuinely appreciate strong recommendations for thoughtful, well-produced, rigorous podcasts — but that is often not what I am seeing. The recommendations can feel like they are drifting toward the lowest common denominator. What I would like: - More control over the types of podcast channels and topics that are recommended. - A way to say “show me less content like this,” not just hide one individual item. - Controls for excluding certain formats, channels, topics, creators, or recommendation styles. - An option to prioritize higher-quality, more thoughtful, less clickbait-driven recommendations. - Better feedback tools that let users actively shape the recommendation system around their own values and listening goals. The underlying issue is not that I dislike recommendations. I actually want good recommendations. But I want recommendations that feel aligned with my values and intentions, rather than recommendations that seem optimized for compulsive engagement. I want to emphasize that I broadly love Spotify and would much rather see this improved than leave the product. But this issue matters enough to me that I have already had to aggressively delete apps or install extensions for other platforms to protect my attention. If Spotify moves further toward an addictive recommendation-feed model without giving users strong in-app controls, I may eventually be forced to cancel my subscription, even though I would prefer not to. A good outcome would be: When I open Spotify, I should feel like the product is helping me listen intentionally — not nudging me into the same kind of addictive browsing loop that exists on YouTube, Instagram, and TikTok.
records.author: hugomoran159

records.text: Re: How can you report fake AI artists? Sadly I don't believe spotify cares for the forseeable future. Noticed this a year ago, created a post somehwere here and on reddit i think. Nothing changed at all. Did a slightly more frustrtated one... they deleted it almost instantly. Seemingly got un-deleted afterwards. Still, bot or not, I don't care anymore. It "broke the camels back for me". I had spotify for pretty much 10 years at this point and still really like the service. The message I received 1 Minute after posting. The big response above "Histrocore...". Most likely automated. But doesn't help in this case if Spotify permits AI songs, then uses AI to clean up the reception of it. Surely accidental, but why should I honestly care. But it is pretty evident, that they don't care about customers at all. Let the customer choose and require the artist to specify stuff. I understand it's not an easy feat, but it seems there is not even any attempt about doing something. I'd gladly come back once there is some kind of option to filter out songs that have been mostly generated by AI (for purposes of non-artists making cheap songs quickly, a proper artist using it as a tool is not a problem imo). Ever since a year, I very often had this paranoia "Is the song I'm listening to from some human or did an AI generate it". Especially since I heard some songs of friends that made quick meme songs, I started to notice the familiar voice hard. I understand that spotify may not want to out-right ban something that could prove a valuable opportunity for them. After all a companies goal is still to make money and become sustainable/profitable. But not giving users any choice about this and praying it doesn't get noticed is the wrong way imo. So sorry, not sorry, but for me this ruined what I loved most about spotify for me: Discovering cool music and just being able to listen for hours to recommendations care-free while finding some awesome gems. If this ever gets adressed I might come back, but by then I might also have settled with my alternative too much already...
records.author: LinusCDE98

records.text: Re: Spotify - The Worst App in the World EVER The problem with opinions... is that everyone has one 😉 While I can definitely agree that the app isn't perfect I also don't recognize myself in your issues either. First things first: when you mention the app I'm assuming you're talking about the phone app, and not the Windows (or Mac (if that exists)) app? But second... very important: all those apps use the same so called 'engine'. Your recommendations are not necessarily provided by the app, but rather the "Spotify engine" (as I like to call it). Here's the problem: Spotify recommendations are solely based on what you checked off as stuff you like (and I'm also assuming they sometimes take listening time into consideration as well). The good news though is that you can (re)train Spotify if needed, let me show you: If you check the lower right corner of either the Spotify website (or the desktop (!) app) then you'll notice the controls shown above (at the bottom): see the control I highlighted? That's the queue button, and it will show you what songs Spotify has picked for you to play next, based on your selections / preferences. So if there are songs here which you really don't appreciate then you can actually mark them as such. Just open their context menu (the three dots which will pop up when you hover your mouse over an entry) and click on that. This allows you to perform tasks on that song. Stuff like adding it to your favorites, but also allowing you to exclude those songs from your taste profile; thus telling Spotify not to recommend those anymore. Sure, this can become a drag: having to do this a few times but trust me when I say that things will get better over time. And those changes will also reflect on your apps as well. Hope this can help. PS: I dunno about you but my moods and thus taste in music also often changes. When I'm doing an evening walk I prefer softer, more casual, music then during a powerwalk during the day. SO songs which you may dislike now... may be soothing sometimes later. Or maybe that's just me 😉
records.author: ShelLuser

records.text: Just make it make sense... Plan Premium Country N/A Device Yes. Operating System Definitely. My Question or Issue I've recently actively been removing a specific genre of music from my Liked Songs, my Playlists, etc. and Smart Shuffle is now exclusively recommending me that same genre ... The genre I'm actively removing from my profile . It wasn't doing this before I started removing it. In fact, my taste profile wasn't in too bad of a shape before I started deciding to refine my Liked Songs literally for Smart Shuffle , so it would have an easier time with recommendations for me. However, it has somehow had the exact opposite effect , and now a genre I am actively removing and have not listened to in about 8 years is dominating my taste profile. Let's be real here: Spotify's algorithm with things such as Discover Weekly, For You, and Smart Shuffle in general has historically, and continues to be, as effective at curating diverse music discovery as a quadriplegic is at pole vaulting. However, even despite the service feeling like it's actively trying to rage-bait me into cancelling my Premium, my taste profile had recently managed to be okay-ish and I had my hopes. Now those hopes have been shoved into a urinal and given a swirlie... I don't even want a solution at this point, as it's pretty clear from over my many years of Spotify use that for some wild reasoning, the company refuses to improve their algorithm at all and instead decides to force tone-deaf features down the consumer's throat. Things such as removing Disliking from Discover Weekly and replacing with the completely benign "Hide Song" button for example. This is just so incredibly wack. Like, at first it was frustrating, then disappointing, and eventually saddening. Now it's like watching a paper school bus full of infants slowly drifting down a snow bank into a pit of starving feral hogs. It's just depressing. All I want, all I genuinely want, someone to try and make it make sense to me. Please just try... How does it make sense that while actively removing a genre from my taste profile, the algorithm then feeds me ONLY that genre?
records.author: JoeCeezy

records.text: Why I canceled my subscription due to "algorithmic pollution" and lack of in-app profile switching Dear Spotify Product & Development Team, I am writing to provide constructive feedback regarding a fundamental UX and algorithmic issue that ultimately led me to cancel my Spotify subscription after being a loyal user for a long time. I hope this insight is helpful for your future product roadmap. The Context: For several months, I regularly visited a nursing home and played music for the residents using my smartphone. The Core Issues: Lack of In-App Profile Switching: Even with a Premium Family plan, switching to a different profile on a single device is highly impractical, as it requires a complete manual logout and login. Because of this friction, I had to use my primary personal profile to play music during these visits. Irreversible "Algorithmic Pollution": The music played during these visits completely altered my personalized recommendations. Even after the nursing home visits ended, I waited several months for the algorithm to normalize and relearn my actual taste. Unfortunately, it never recovered. No Algorithm Reset Option: There is no dedicated tool to manually correct the algorithm or completely reset the taste profile. The frequently suggested workaround in the community—deleting the account and starting fresh—means losing all curated libraries and playlists. From a user's perspective, being told to "start over from scratch" is functionally equivalent to being prompted to cancel the subscription and move to a competitor. Conclusion: I genuinely enjoyed Spotify's features. However, the inability to shield my algorithm (e.g., via a seamless profile switch on a single device, similar to video streaming platforms) or easily correct it once it was altered made the service practically unusable for my personal music discovery. I hope you will consider implementing a true "Reset Algorithm" button or a frictionless multi-profile switch within the app in the future. Best regards, Robert Jaeckel
records.author: rjackmuc

records.text: Last.fm Integration for AI DJ — Better Recommendations from Your Full Listening History The AI DJ is a great concept, but its recommendations are only as good as the data it has access to. Right now, there are two significant limitations: It only considers the last 2 years of your Spotify listening history It completely ignores playlists you've created or saved , which often reflect your music taste better than recent streams It has no awareness of music you listen to outside of Spotify , whether that's local files, other streaming services, or offline libraries This means the AI DJ can end up in a bubble — constantly recycling familiar recent listens while missing entire genres, artists, or eras you love but just don't happen to stream on Spotify. The fix: Last.fm integration. Last.fm is a free, widely used service that "scrobbles" (tracks) your listening activity from virtually any music source — Spotify, Apple Music, YouTube Music, Tidal, Winamp, local media players, and more. Millions of users already have years, even decades, of listening data logged there. By allowing users to optionally link their Last.fm account to the AI DJ, Spotify could: Draw on a complete, long-term picture of your music taste — not just 2 years Include listening activity from other platforms , so no genre goes unrecognized Serve genuinely personalized recommendations instead of safe, recent-only picks This would be an opt-in feature , so it doesn't affect anyone who doesn't want it. But for the many listeners who use Spotify alongside other services, or who have deep listening histories that predate their Spotify account, it could transform the AI DJ from "decent shuffle" to a truly intelligent music companion. Last.fm already has an established API and an active user base. The integration path is well-defined. All it takes is the will to build it.
records.author: KazuyaYuza

records.text: Feedback on Spotify DJ voice and personality I want to give feedback on the current Spotify DJ voice and personality. In terms of music selection, it usually works quite well. The recommendations are often relevant, and the feature itself has clear value. My issue is not with the concept of DJ, and not with having a distinct style in itself. My issue is that the current style does not fit a large number of genres very well. The DJ has a very pronounced and distinctly American radio-style personality. This is not necessarily a problem in itself. In some contexts, this strong style can work. But with many genres, it becomes distracting rather than immersive. With genres such as folk, for example, the transitions feel tonally out of place because the spoken presentation clashes with the atmosphere of the music. This is made even more noticeable by the fact that many listeners in the countries where Spotify operates do not have the same cultural familiarity with this kind of highly expressive American host style. Because of that, the DJ can feel less like a natural companion to the music and more like an interruption. That stands out even more because Spotify is not an American company. I would expect either a more neutral default voice, or a wider range of presentation styles that fit different genres and audiences better. I would strongly suggest adding one or more of the following: a neutral DJ voice option, multiple voice/personality options, genre-sensitive presentation styles, a setting to reduce or disable spoken transitions. Again, the music selection is often good. The main problem is not that the DJ has a distinct personality, but that the current personality and delivery style are too culturally specific and too mismatched with many genres to work well as a universal default.
records.author: SpaceAlot

metadata.title: Feedback on Spotify DJ voice and personality
metadata.search_snippet: I want to give feedback on the current Spotify DJ voice and personality. In terms of music selection, it usually works quite well. The recommendations are often relevant, and the feature itself has clear value. My issue is not with the concept of DJ, and not with having a distinct style in itself. My issue is that the current style does not fit a large number of genres very well. The DJ has a very pronounced and distinctly American radio-style personality. This is not necessarily a problem in itself. In some contexts, this strong style can work. But with many genres, it becomes distracting rather than immersive. With genres such as folk, for example, the transitions feel tonally out of place because the spoken presentation clashes with the atmosphere of the music. This is made even more noticeable by the fact that many listeners in the countries where Spotify operates do not have the same cultural familiarity with this kind of highly expressive American host style. Because of that, the DJ can feel less like a natural companion to the music and more like an interruption. That stands out even more because Spotify is not an American company. I would expect either a more neutral default voice, or a wider range of presentation styles that fit different genres and audiences better. I would strongly suggest adding one or more of the following: a neutral DJ voice option, multiple voice/personality options, genre-sensitive presentation styles, a setting to reduce or disable spoken transitions. Again, the music selection is often good. The main problem is not that the DJ has a distinct personality, but that the current personality and delivery style are too culturally specific and too mismatched with many genres to work well as a universal default.

records.text: Re: Remove the DJ AI or at least give the option to turn it off The DJ is very annoying. I've tried it a few times and he's constantly talking instead of just playing music. It's feels like the same exact annoyance as an advertisement playing between songs. I just tested it again, he starts talking as soon as you click play, 5 skips in, he babbling again. Just play the music and shut up, sheesh. I wish they would actually implement some better features or good AI features. Like describing a song or song video and being able to find that song based on your description. Google/YouTube do this for me and guess the song accurately most of the times. I try that on Spotify and it's just a waste of time. So I'm forced off the platform to go search and just end of watching/listening somewhere else. Or the terrible ability with finding specific songs from artists. Want to find an old popular song you can't remember from an artist? Have fun clicking through 20 albums trying to hunt it down. The artist page gives you what, 10 popular tracks only from the latest album? Other platforms give you 20-30 song list of their most popular songs all-time. Much easier to find an old song you were looking for. There's some other annoying little things they do, like dimming the video preview on the sidebar. Why? You leave album covers undimmed, but then dim video snippets? What logical sense does it make forcing users hover over the video to see it at full brightness? One of the most annoying things, constantly playing the same songs over and over in discovery/recommendations. Crazy they don't have a "I don't like this song" to stop playing it in 2026.

records.text: Stop Payola and paid placement in Recommendations I'm not sure how long this has been going on but it's gross and makes me not want to use the radio feature anymore. Paying for placement on radio without announcement is payola (2). There's a disclaimer on playlists notifying that now the recommendation engine can be manipulated with money, and spotify isn't subject to FCC, but the concept is the same. I don't care about the reassurances that my tastes will be taken into account. Why do I pay monthly? I pay monthly so I don't get advertised to. This is another form of advertisement. Please stop. (1) https://www.spotify.com/us/safetyandprivacy/understanding-recommendations/plain (2) https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/47/317 How do commercial considerations impact recommendations? Spotify prioritizes listener satisfaction when recommending content. In some cases, commercial considerations, such as the cost of content or whether we can monetize it, may influence our recommendations. For example, Discovery Mode gives artists and labels the opportunity to identify songs that are a priority for them, and our system will add that signal to the algorithms that determine the content of personalized listening sessions. When an artist or label turns on Discovery Mode for a song, Spotify charges a commission on streams of that song in areas of the platform where Discovery Mode is active (Discovery Mode is not active in our editorial playlists). This signal increases the likelihood of the selected songs being recommended, but does not guarantee it. We only recommend songs there is a high probability listeners will enjoy. As with all recommendations, we take note when a listener isn't engaging with a song — including those in Discovery Mode — and factor this in when determining what to recommend in the future.

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs Spotify - it’s time to address what’s becoming an obvious problem: the platform is being flooded with AI-generated filler music, and users have no way to filter it out. I’m a paying listener. I curate my playlists. I rely on Discover Weekly, Release Radar, and radio recommendations to find human-made music (i.e real artists, real creativity, real performances). But lately, it’s getting harder to trust that what I’m hearing is even written, performed, or produced by an actual person. Let me be clear: I’m not anti-technology. AI has its place as an efficiency tool. But there’s a massive difference between people using technology and algorithms churning out fully generated tracks with no human authorship or even musicianship. And right now, Spotify treats them as if they’re the same. They are not. What users need is simple: A filter that excludes AI-generated music from recommendations, playlists, searches and feeds, clear metadata that shows when AI was used in lyrics, composition, performance, production, or all of the above, Transparency. Choice. Control over the listening experience we’re paying for. If Spotify can filter explicit content, live versions, remasters, and audio quality, it can absolutely let users filter out AI-generated content. There’s no technical limitation, only a lack of prioritization. And yes, this matters. AI music is already diluting the platform, overwhelming algorithms, and burying real artists who put genuine human creativity into their work. Spotify: If AI music is here to stay, so is the need for transparency - Give users the option to hear human-made music. This is a reasonable, necessary step for the future of the platform. Please make it happen.
records.author: AutomaticEight

records.text: Avoid Duplicate Tracks in Radio Stations When Identical Versions Already Exist in the Library The “Radio” feature from a song or artist is one of the best parts of Spotify and something I use almost every day. It’s a great way to discover music and keep a vibe going. However, there is a frustrating issue that happens quite often. Radio stations frequently recommend songs without checking if an identical version of that track already exists in the listener’s library or listening history. Because of this, the same song can appear repeatedly, just from different releases or publications. For example, I really like the rock band Audioslave. For some reason, their label has released many duplicate versions of the same songs across different albums, compilations, and editions. I assume this might be related to catalog or monetization strategies, but the result is that Spotify treats them as different tracks. Because of this, whenever I start almost any rock radio station, I often hear “Like a Stone” again, but from a different release. Over time, I ended up liking multiple identical versions of the same song because they appear separately. I currently have several copies of the exact same track in my library, with the same length and recording, just linked to different releases. The bigger problem is repetition! Since the platform treats these versions as separate tracks, the radio algorithm can recommend them multiple times, which makes the listening experience feel repetitive and eventually reduces the enjoyment of songs I actually like. A possible improvement would be to detect identical recordings across different releases and treat them as a single track for radio recommendations and library management. This could help reduce repetition and create a much smoother listening experience.
records.author: btz1

records.text: How do I clean up the app and remove recommendations? Device Samsung S23U/android My Question or Issue Over the last year or so, the app has become so cluttered with excessive suggestions/recommendations and it's really ruining what used to be a nice clean experience. In the past my home page would immediately give me access to the most recent, and most commonly played songs/shows Now, the grid at the top is taken up mostly by things I listened to once, or ages ago and have no interest in. An extra search bar because why not and after that I'm bombarded with complete nonsense, from audio books I don't want, prompts I never asked for and why am I constantly being told to listen to conspiracy theory and engineering podcasts?? And then double it all because videos now. Sorry Spotify but you'll never be YouTube or Audible, and the more aggressively you try the more you're just making the experience worse of what you do best, music and podcasts. 80% of the usable space in the all page has now become "try this..." "explore this..." "recommend..." "suggested.." instead of the things I actually want, the things I actually like and know I want to listen to. It feels like that is being behind recommendation spam fueld by an algorithm that died two years ago, recommending artists and songs I've not bothered with for ages, and "new" videos from creators I watched on YouTube a month ago. And I have to click through 5 pages and a filter just to get to my own selection. Is there any way to disable this? To put things back the way they were a year or so ago? Thanks
records.author: Minska

records.text: Comprehensive AI Policy on Songs, Album Artwork, Music Videos, and Spotify UI I've seen a lot of complaints about various AI-related issues so I wanted to write my thoughts covering pretty much all of the ones I'm aware of in one post. Ideally, AI generated content would be banned entirely, but that probably won't happen. That being said, Artists should be required to disclose if a song was written, composed, "sung," or any combination of the three by AI, if the album artwork was generated by AI, and if the music video was generated by AI before they upload songs/albums. People who are caught lying should be subject to warnings and have the song, album artwork, and/or music video in question marked as AI and people who lie three or more times should be banned. Any song that was written, composed, or "sung" by AI should be marked as such not just in the credits but next to the title in the same manner that songs are labeled as explicit. Any album artwork or music video generated by AI should have a label in the corner of the image/video saying so. Artists should be required to disclose if they are AI "artists" - i.e. "musicians" who exclusively upload songs that are written, composed, and/or "sung" by AI. Users should have the right to block songs and artists that have been labeled as AI from playing and showing up in their algorithms in forms such as Daily Mixes, genre mixes, Discover Weekly, and home page recommendations. Any Spotify feature powered by AI should be clearly labeled as such. Users should have the right to disable individual AI powered Spotify features in their settings.
records.author: leukos

records.text: Re: Issues with using "Search" - keyboard does not appear automatically "This is how it works today. To open keyboard when hitting the search button: 1. Tap search -> moves to page for search 2. Tap search field at top of search page. Old searches and recommendations can be viewed here-> field transforms to a writeable field in grey 3. Tap the grey field to type in it -> keyboard upens up." It works, thanks. But it's ridiculous. First of all, it was possible to tap the lens twice: the first tap would show the search box, the second tap would show the keyboard. It was perfect, why change it? Now, the second tap brings you back. But not only it brings you back: it stacks tabs! So, if you really want to go back, you have to swipe left multiple times to go back and forth multiple times through the Search tab until you get back to the home. This is already a nuisance. But, even if we accept the fact that it's not possible to tap the lens twice, the first tap on the "What do you want to listen" (or whatever it's called in English, I'm translating literally from the Italian version) box should open the keyboard already. There's no way an expert UX Designer would accept a UX where the use case is "User wants to search for an item" and you have to tap the same element twice to open the keyboard. And the fact that so many people couldn't find a way to show the keyboard at all (including me, and I am a software engineer...) and thought this was a bug shows how counterintuitive this solution is.
records.author: tompabes

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs A mod said that AI disclosure will not do anything for song recommendations but it NEEDS to. Discover Weekly is my favorite feature on Spotify. It has been since it existed and it’s where I have found my favorite songs. I am maybe 10 songs into my Discover Weekly today at so far 3 artists were AI. All of these took music created from real people and make them “reimagined.” Many of them had covers featuring AI generated Black Artists. Only 1 disclosed that it was AI. You have robots pretending to be Black Artists stealing songs from real people. Remember how it felt to learn about Milli Vanilli? This is worse. I have been Ride or Die for Spotify since middle school. It was the first subscription services I ever paid for and it’s the one I’ve always said I’d never give up. But this is insane. This is I miss my Discover Weekly giving me fresh, new artists and songs. Spotify, you MUST: 1. Put a button in the settings that can opt out of AI artists (I know you can do this, it will basically serve the same function as opting out of explicit songs) 2. All AI songs need to have a sticker or some flag to designate its AI-generated status 3. Any artists that does not disclose they are an AI artist should have the ability to be reported and investigated. 4. At the very least, allow us to have a button to take AI songs off of our Discover Weekly, Release Radars, and other mixes and recommendations.
records.author: coniejo

records.text: Seamless Multi-User Profile Support for tvOS App (Like Apple Music) (Time is precious, so I asked a generative AI to write this using the prompt: "You're a Spotify Community expert. Help me write the best idea suggestion making the Spotify multiple user experience seemless on the current version of tvOS. Maybe comparing to other tvOS music apps that already have this feature.") Idea: Implement native multi-user profile switching in the Spotify tvOS app using Apple's "Runs as Current User" APIs, for a household-friendly experience matching Apple Music and other tvOS apps. In households sharing Apple TV 4K (tvOS 18+), multiple user profiles provide personalized Up Next queues, recommendations, and settings across apps. Spotify's tvOS app lags behind: no profile picker, no automatic login per system user—forcing manual logouts/logins or clunky Spotify Connect workarounds that don't sync playlists or history. Why it matters: Families on Premium Family plans can't easily access individual libraries, breaking the shared living room vibe. Competitors nail this: - Apple Music: Auto-detects active TV profile, loads user-specific music, radio, and recommendations seamlessly. - YouTube Music/Premium: Quick profile switcher respects TV users. Apple's APIs make it straightforward—tvOS 16+ handles profile mapping automatically via UserDefaults and Keychain for secure, per-user data (no more interstitial prompts). A simple checkbox in app settings could opt-in: "Support Multiple Users. Requested features: - Profile picker on launch/switch, storing preferences per TV user. - Per-profile: Playlists, listening history, recommendations, login credentials. - Backward-compatible for single-user setups. This would boost retention on Apple TV—please prioritize! Upvote if your household needs it.
records.author: djdare9

Spotify's tvOS app lags behind: no profile picker, no automatic login per system user—forcing manual logouts/logins or clunky Spotify Connect workarounds that don't sync playlists or history. Why it matters: Families on Premium Family plans can't easily access individual libraries, breaking the shared living room vibe. Competitors nail this: - Apple Music: Auto-detects active TV profile, loads user-specific music, radio, and recommendations seamlessly. - YouTube Music/Premium: Quick profile switcher respects TV users. Apple's APIs make it straightforward—tvOS 16+ handles profile mapping automatically via UserDefaults and Keychain for secure, per-user data (no more interstitial prompts). A simple checkbox in app settings could opt-in: "Support Multiple Users. Requested features: - Profile picker on launch/switch, storing preferences per TV user. - Per-profile: Playlists, listening history, recommendations, login credentials. - Backward-compatible for single-user setups. This would boost retention on Apple TV—please prioritize! Upvote if your household needs it.

records.text: Re: Autoplay/ Radio not working properly I can confirm a similar issue on the same Spotify desktop version. Plan: Premium Device: PC Operating System: Windows 11 64-bit Spotify version: 1.2.90.451.gb094aab0 The issue seems related to playback context handling after the recent Autoplay / Radio problems. Scenario: Artist discography playback. Spotify recently improved the desktop experience by adding better Discography sorting, including the ability to sort albums from oldest to newest. This finally allowed users to listen through an artist's catalog chronologically. Steps to reproduce: Open an artist page in the desktop app. Go to Discography. Sort albums from oldest to newest. Start playing the first album in the sorted discography. Wait until the album finishes. Expected behavior: Spotify should preserve the selected Discography playback context and continue through the artist's albums according to the chosen sorting. Actual behavior: Spotify leaves the Discography context and switches to recommendation / Artist Radio behavior instead. This makes the recently added Discography sorting feature much less useful. The sorting works visually, but the playback queue no longer follows the user's selected context. This is not a request to remove Autoplay or recommendations. They are useful features when intentionally selected. The problem is that playback started from a sorted Discography view should behave differently from starting a radio or recommendation session. Please check if the recent Autoplay / Radio changes affected Discography playback context handling. https://community.spotify.com/t5/Your-Library/Can-t-play-an-artist-s-discography-on-the-desktop-app/m-p/7453206#M70011
records.author: mikcio2

records.text: Missed recommendation recap The idea is about implementing in Spotify short audio previews of songs suggested by Autoplay / Radio / Smart Shuffle Idea Sometimes Spotify recommends great songs during everyday listening (work, driving, running), but users cannot save them in time. Currently, once the session ends, these recommendations are effectively lost. I propose a weekly recap containing short previews (10–30 seconds) of tracks that Spotify recommended to the user but were not liked or saved. This recap would appear on the Home screen as a temporary playlist or story-style card: “Missed Recommendations This Week” --- How it works Spotify already tracks recommended tracks during listening sessions (Autoplay, Radio, Smart Shuffle, Search suggestions). Once per week Spotify would: 1. Collect recommended tracks played but not saved 2. Select the most relevant ones 3. Generate short preview snippets (10–30 seconds) 4. Present them as a swipeable recap For each track the user could: - ❤️ Like - ➕ Add to playlist - ❌ Dismiss --- Why this matters Many users listen while busy (sports, commuting, work). Discovery is passive — saving is active. Currently discovery quality is high, but retention of discovered music is low because users cannot react in the moment. This feature: - prevents losing good recommendations - increases saves and engagement - improves trust in the recommendation system - creates a lightweight weekly ritual similar to Wrapped, but frequent --- User value Spotify becomes not only a player but a memory of discovery. Users rediscover what they almost liked. --- Optional extension Daily short recap or auto-generated “discovery mashup” shareable as a story.
records.author: Czyzyk06

records.text: Removal of “Clear Queue” on mobile breaks intent-based listening and creates driving distractions I’m a long-time Premium subscriber and multi-genre listener. Recent mobile app updates removed the ability to clear the playback queue, which has significantly degraded the listening experience. Previously, users could intentionally reset the queue and start a new listening session. Now, Spotify auto-injects “Recommended tracks” that cannot be cleared in bulk. This creates several problems: • Breaks intent-based listening — I often switch genres based on mood or context (e.g., classical in the morning, hip-hop later, jazz in the evening). Having prior genres forced into the queue makes listening chaotic rather than curated. • Forces algorithmic recommendations over user control — even when Autoplay is disabled, the queue remains polluted unless overridden by starting new playback. • Creates a driving safety issue — when listening in the car (including CarPlay), unexpected tracks from past listening sessions begin playing after selections end. This is distracting and requires additional interaction while driving to correct. • This is a regression — the functionality to clear the queue existed and was removed without an equivalent replacement. Autoplay recommendations can be a useful feature when they are optional and context-aware, but users should retain full control over their playback queue. Requested fixes (any would resolve this): • Restore a “Clear Queue” option on mobile • Allow full disabling of recommendation injection into the queue • Separate user-queued content from algorithmic recommendations Spotify works best when it supports intentional listening, not when it overrides it.
records.author: Kastrothfl

records.text: Re: "not interested" button has disappeared Hi there! Thank you for bringing up the concern. You’re not doing anything wrong. The “Not interested” option has been removed or hidden for many users as part of recent Home feed changes and testing. That’s why it suddenly disappeared after the update. It’s not device-specific and not something you can turn back on locally. A few things that might reduce this content, even though none fully replace that button: Tap the three dots on the show and choose Hide this show if that option still appears. It’s inconsistently available, but it does sometimes work. Go to Settings > Content preferences and turn off Video podcasts where available. This doesn’t always apply retroactively, but it can reduce future recommendations. Make sure you’re not following or auto-following any shows tied to those categories. Unfollow them explicitly if needed. That said, your main point is valid. Removing user-level negative feedback while increasing algorithmic recommendations leaves people stuck with content they’ve clearly signaled they don’t want. Reporting shows doesn’t solve that, especially when the issue is recommendation logic, not just policy violations. This is something Spotify really needs to address: users should be able to say “don’t show me this” without jumping through hoops. You’re far from the only person raising this concern. If nothing else, upvoting similar threads helps keep it visible to Spotify staff.
records.author: Koyubi

records.text: AI Generated Music Recommendations I was listening to 60s music and was recommended an AI generated song of the 60s surf rock genre, which I found problematic. Spotify Support suggested that I was recommended AI music based on my listening style of 40s-60s music... which is an absurdity considering AI didn't exist in the 40s-60s, but they also suggested I block the artist to train my algorithm. The issue is that if I block the artist, this affects my listening algorithm of the genuinely produced genre. If I turn off Autoplay (as recommended by Support), all other recommended songs won't automatically play... The fact that AI generated music exists is one thing, the fact that it's a Spotify recommendation is another, but the added salt of it affecting the algorithm searching for music I'd genuinely like to hear is insulting. But there's a simple solution. There exists software that can detect the likelihood of AI generated content. I argue that uploaded media should be screened upon submission and AI generated music should be tagged as such. With this tag, there should be a filter in settings "Do not recommend me AI content". This would preserve your algorithm while avoiding AI generated music. This is a serious issue as AI generated music is not seen as separate from human made music, such that you cannot target AI content against your listening algorithm which severely impacts user experience.
records.author: ZacL

records.text: Re: Wrapped 2025 MY 2025 WRAPPED SONGS ARE ALMOST ALL FROM PLAYLISTS I HAD MARKED AS EXCLUDED FROM TASTE PROFILE I very often play chill, repetitive, low rhythmic songs like some LoFi, synthwave and such, only for study or work sessions. I don't actually love or hate them, obviously I like them enough to have them in a playlist but MAINLY I just use them as a tool so that I can focus. The thing is, I deliberately have such songs in separate playlists not just for the convenience but also because I don't want these to be included in the recommendation algorithm, so I got them marked as 'exclude from taste profile'. It was working fine, they weren't included in my weekly recommendations and such but then the 2025 wrapped content came out and what I was fearing happened: almost all my 'favorite/most played songs' from 2025 were not the ones I actually like the most but the 'tool' songs I thought (or wanted to think) were being excluded. In my opinion, these songs that exists in this excluded playlist and nowhere else for my profile, should be excluded not only for taste profile but also for any content you guys create for us. Does this behavior make sense to you guys? Please share some feedback on why this is working as expected, otherwise I'd much appreciate it gets fixed for my Wrapped 2026. Thanks! Luis.

records.text: Discovery weekly hasn't worked for months First of all, the buttons for genres in the discovery weekly do nothing at all, they just result in a spinning animation around the button and ultimately do nothing. This is on both the windows app (not website) and on android. Website doesn't even have the buttons. Secondly, the content is not like anything I listen to. It used to be full of songs that were similar to my tastes but new. Like it should be. Now discovery weekly has been ONLY the most generic Finnish songs that you'd hear from a "classics" FM radio for months now. The recommendations have nothing to do with what I listen to, I am absolutely sure of that. I've listened to mostly jazz/doom metal/progressive rock for the past few months, without any lyrics whatsoever. So how could every song in the discovery have lyrics in Finnish? None of the songs even match the genres I listen to. It's like the algorithm just gives the most common recommendations based on what a Finnish man in his 40's-50's would listen to. I have tried to unfollow the playlist and refollow it to no avail. Plan Premium Country Finland Device Samsung galaxy S25 ultra, and Windows PC Operating System Android, Windows 11 My Question or Issue

records.text: Re: When listening to Release Radar or Discover Weekly and hiding a song, the wrong song is played n This issue with Release Radar or Discover Weekly usually happens due to how the app refreshes its queue after you hide a track. When you hide a song, the system removes it from your personalized playlist, but sometimes the playback queue doesn’t update instantly. As a result, it may skip incorrectly or play a different (seemingly wrong) next track. Here are a few things you can try to fix it: Refresh the playlist after hiding a song (pull down or reopen the app) Clear the queue and restart playback from the playlist Log out and log back into your account Make sure your app is updated to the latest version Clear cache (especially on mobile devices) This is mostly a temporary glitch rather than a major issue, and updates usually fix it over time. Also, just like how a helps organize and refine your design choices, music algorithms are constantly learning and adjusting based on your preferences. So hiding songs will eventually improve your recommendations, even if small playback bugs happen in the short term. If the issue keeps happening, it’s best to report it through the app’s support so it can be fixed in future updates.
records.author: freecolortool

records.text: Custom radio or playlist based on personal playlist I’d like to propose a feature that allows users to generate a radio station based on an entire playlist, not just a single track. Right now, Spotify only offers the “Go to radio” option for individual songs, and while helpful, it doesn’t capture the full range of a listener’s taste when that taste is represented through a curated playlist. The suggested songs section beneath playlists also falls short, as it often repeats the same recommendations or misses the overall mood and direction of the playlist entirely. A playlist-based radio feature would take the combined data from all songs within a user’s playlist and create a dynamic radio station or auto-generated playlist that reflects the broader style, tone, and preferences the user has built over time. This would be especially beneficial for users with large or thoughtfully curated playlists, as it would open the door to discovering new music that aligns much more accurately with their actual listening patterns. It differentiates itself from current features by using the playlist as a whole, resulting in recommendations that are more precise, varied, and relevant. This enhancement would deepen personalization and make music discovery far more engaging for users who invest time into shaping their playlists.
records.author: Michael3857

I don't know about you guys, but I don't 'Follow' that many artists, because I listen to way too much music by way too many different people. I don't need 60000 updates or recommendations for the music I'm already listening to. That being said, my release radar is **bleep** because of this fact. Constantly getting 10, 15, 20 year old songs put in there like "hey bet you didn't know about this SECRET INDIE NEW RELEASE called "I Write Sins Not Tragedies". Like honey, Spotify baby, I'm much older than you. I was listening to these "releases" before you were born. It happens in my Discover Weekly as well, but I'm more lenient with it. It's still completely absurd and annoying, that the music it wants me to discover, are some of the biggest hits from [insert genre here] from [insert year here], that I have heard 100s of times before. Like fair enough, I haven't listened to Seether on this spotify account - that doesn't mean that I want to 'discover' that song from 20 years ago... Then all the "new music for you" is filled with **bleep** I don't even listen to! Spotify Enshitification 2025.

records.text: Revert to old model for recommended tracks Hi there, I have been wanting to give feedback for months. Something very bad is happening with your recommendation systems. Discovery and serendipity have dropped dramatically and it is horrible. It means I am in an echo chamber of music. Not only that, but another weird thing I have noticed is that when I put on my 60's/70's rock playlist, when it ends it moves onto recommended tracks. This used to continue on with the same taste profile of the playlist itself which often would surface music along the same profile and was a great outcome. In the last 3-6 months I have noticed when a playlist ends, it recommended tracks reverts to my recent listening history and regurgitates what I already listen to. So in the case of this 60's/70's rock playlist, when it ends, it plays maybe 1-2 songs of 60's/70's rock and then dives into my overall music taste of deep house and it just plays deep house tracks from my 2026 Summer playlist. This is such a jarring and glaring issue. Your recommendation engines are not doing well and I urge you to revert to an older model. The new one IS NOT GOOD.
records.author: ritchles

records.text: Volume-Based Recommendations: The Missing Signal User behavior on mobile devices shows a distinct "High-Affinity" signal: The Volume Spike. When a user discovers a track they love, their first physical interaction is often a significant increase in volume (30%–50%+ increase). Conversely, a decrease in volume or a "mute" during a track is a subtle negative signal that current skip-logic misses. The Proposed Feature: "Volume-Weighted Affinity" I suggest integrating Volume Delta (the change in volume level) as a third-layer feedback signal to refine the "Taste Profile." Positive Reinforcement: A volume increase of >X% within the first 60 seconds of a track correlates to high emotional resonance. The algorithm should weight the metadata of this track more heavily for immediate next-track selection. The "Environmental" Filter: To avoid noise (e.g., turning up volume because the bus got loud), the signal can be normalized using the device's microphone (Ambient Noise Floor) or Accelerometer (detecting movement). If the environment is stable but the volume goes up, the signal is "Active Interest." Negative Refinement: A "Fade Out" (slowly lowering volume) during a track provides a more nuanced negative signal than a "Hard Skip," suggesting the track doesn't fit the current mood even if it fits the genre .
records.author: georgelightning

records.text: Idea: optional filter to exclude AI-generated music from recommendations Hi everyone, I’ve been a Spotify user for years and music has always meant something deeply personal to me. Not background noise — real human connection. Recently I found out that an artist I genuinely enjoyed, Morgan Luna, doesn’t exist. There’s no real person behind the voice. It’s a fully AI-generated project designed to look and feel like a real singer, with a profile, album covers, and millions of streams. I had no idea. Spotify gave me close to zero signals — I had to figure it out myself outside the platform. I’m not against AI music existing. But I am against not being able to choose. My idea is simple: give users an optional filter in Settings to exclude AI-generated music from recommendations, radio, and search results. Think of it like Waze letting you avoid toll roads. Nobody bans the tolls — they just give you the choice. I want to listen to music made by humans, for humans. I just want Spotify to let me choose that. Please vote if you feel the same way. The more of us ask for this, the harder it is to ignore. Thanks for reading.
records.author: Alberto_MG

records.text: What if Spotify just knew — the moment you opened it? What if Spotify had AI? Not just recommendations. Something that actually understands you. I commute to office every day. And every morning it's the same thing open Spotify, the music is there, the mood is there, I even have playlists I made myself. But in that moment I can never find the song that matches exactly how I feel. So I built a concept around this problem. A feature where you tell it once whether you're commuting, working from home, at the gym, or just relaxing. It learns your environment and combines it with your time of day and weather to build your perfect soundtrack automatically. No input needed every single day. It also has an AI Search tab. Not keyword search. Emotion search. I typed "I had a tiring day" and it gave me songs to lift me up. There's also a Rediscovered section. Because we all have songs we deeply loved and completely forgot about. Songs sitting unplayed in our library for months. The AI brings them back at the right moment. Got tired of not always finding the right song for the right moment and wondered if AI could just do it.

records.text: Re: LABEL AI MUSIC Adding a comment here to increase visibility of this issue. I really enjoy finding new music from the discover weekly playlist but after listening to the first few tracks I noticed that none of the artists that I have listened to are real people. To me, this is an affront to all Spotify users and feels incredibly soulless. After investigating I found that two of the AI generated artists in my discover weekly were created by the same entrepreneur named Lawrence Vavra, who owns a label of several different AI "artists". This has completely broken my trust because I used to feel like Spotify recommendations were made for me and my tastes but now I feel like I'm being hand fed literal slop. I now Google every single artist name to make sure they exist before I add them to my Library. I will likely cancel my service if this does not change soon. Let me remove AI garbage from my experience that I pay for, please. This will snowball into a huge issue as AI takes up more and more of our digital space, it would be great to be able to, at the very least, filter out this swill.
records.author: isaiahiswicked

records.text: Find someone with nearly identical taste in music, then show what else they listen to Look at my top 100 songs of 2025 (Wrapped) and give me a list of ten people who have the most overlap in their top 100. Eg this anonymous person from Finland has 90.3% overlap, this person from Wisconsin has 85% overlap, and so on. Then let me look at their top 100 and sort it by what the overlap is AND what songs they listened to that I haven't. In other words, if this person, who has very similar taste in music as I do, has repeatedly listened to some song, there's a high likelihood that I'll dig it. "Find your Taste Match". Many people are averse to AI and AI recommendations. Discover Weekly is notoriously bad for people with unique taste. You can really please music aficionados if you brand this human-centric. These are real people, with really good taste. Now you get to discover music through other people. If it's impractical to do this on a personal level, why not aggregate? "These are the songs you would really like, based on people who share your taste in music (+90% overlap in Wrapped)".
records.author: jaakkoj

records.text: A button to remove recently played songs and playlist from suggested on the home page Spotify should let users long-press any suggested playlist, song, album or recommendation on the homepage to bring up a simple menu that includes Remove from Homepage or Hide This Suggestion. Selecting it would instantly take that item out of the feed so it no longer appears as a suggestion. It could also have an option for Hide similar content to reduce recommendations in the same genre or from the same artists. This would give direct and immediate control over the home screen. The algorithm would receive clear feedback that something does not fit and update much quicker than relying only on ongoing listening to gradually override old data. The homepage would remain clean, relevant and truly personalized every time the app is opened, with no waiting or extra work required. In cases where someone used my account and listened to Spanish or Mexican music and no matter how much music I listen to afterward it doesn't disappear, this feature would help in such cases by letting you remove those stubborn suggestions right away instead of dealing with them lingering for weeks.
records.author: Thor895

records.text: Stop recommending the Bill Simmons podcast. The Bill Simmons sports show keeps getting recommended, shoved up to the front of my home screen and even auto starts if I let podcasts play after one another. I don't like sports. I'm not interested in sports. I actively dislike sports. I've clicked the not interested button multiple times over the years so I guess it's broken. Or maybe you don't care and just push whatever sponsored content you want to make a profit. When you force a sponsored sports podcast like Bill Simmons in my face and down my throat, wedged into my ear canals, I actively want to delete the app and cancel my subscription I'm so upset. There might be something more going on in my life but this lack of control violates something deep inside me. This is my personal audio listening experience. I like my music. I like my podcasts about topics I'm actually interested in. I DON'T WANT TO LISTEN TO A DUDE BLATHER ON ABOUT PUTTING A SPORTS BALL IN A HOOP OR AN ENDZONE. Please stop promoting that show to me or add ways to block content permanently or something comparable.
