records.text: #MusicMonday Review - April 2026 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check March's review for more music. In this issue, we tackle on the subject of human conflict, including those times when you battle with your own self. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 Morgendust – King of Lonely Souls She went astray With demons haunting her Holding on to every bit of rope Faith is hope and we’re running out You want the world that you can’t control You want to touch what you can not hold Come on and get it, come on and get it I’m pretty sure you know me I’m the king of lonely souls We begin this musical journey in Zwolle, Netherlands, with a Cinematic Indie Rock track about being trapped in a toxic world of sadness, where grief is love with no place to go. Band member Marco explains: " I have a religious background and I wanted to write about the devil… but what/who is the devil? Everyone faces their own ‘devil’. In this song it’s a woman who suffers depression. Something I was a witness of in my circle of friends. 😉 " spotify:track:74WuO3lpYnPUcFzyhAGkta:small Icarus Phoenix – Boiled Peanuts, misheard (feat. Chasing Mice) I could be brutal instead feeding you poison sweetened Desiccate all you once were whatever you’re saying misheard Honey it’s easy to squeeze a trigger that’s pointing toward me A storm of resentment in you Robotic and silent, a ruse Let's fly now to Baltimore, MD. for an Indie Rock track with traces of Americana about a relationship where every conversation becomes the same, without truly listening to what's being said: "Lyrically, it's essentially about my ex-wife. I'm dealing with the difficulty of communication. Musically, [band member] Leena wrote the opening guitar line and then we collaborated in the structure and songwriting as a band for the rest." spotify:track:6oWw2zCT2ceALxQPRQpIcM:small Warm Coat – Expert Every subject I find You're so quick to remind us You're always right It's a skill that you honed Not to leave you exposed in the limelight Quick to claim solutions Though you never know Talk over the rest Talking about broken communication, let's now go to Rhondda Valley, South Wales, in the UK for a shoegazy Indie Rock track about how hard is to teach someone who thinks that knows everything, even when they actually don't: " I think everyone knows someone who can’t be advised because they think they already have the answers. It could be someone in the family or at work. In my case it was based on several people I’ve come into contact with in my life who talk over the room and cannot take advice. " spotify:track:15hCHMygbvR2sJL6Sh8h3Q:small Mike Chek & Grant Griffin – Saki Shots The hazmat kimono was half ass on My whole body was exposed to the acid rap zone I was molded To be a murderous ronin My reflex’s so fast in one second you folded Let's travel now to Detroit, MI for an Alt-Rap collaboration about fighting alone against the world, just like a japanese anime can do: " I met Grant on Discord, and we started building a boom bap album. Theme wise the approach was to alternate who starts each song and this specific one came about after watching an episode of samurai champloo ." spotify:track:0K3uD821Uk7SAlqlrvxUV8:small In the Variant – Anything You Want Are you listening To the sound of my voice Can you tell it’s me Stuck inside you head On repeat Even when you close your eyes You’ll see me all the time But I’m not really there you’re dreaming If there’s one thing That I know for sure Is you can’t have me anymore Our trip ends in Denver, CO. for a Hard Rock song about taking steps away form all that we've ever known, looking for something you may not even know yet: " It was shaped by a universal experience I think a lot of people grapple with. Anything you want is a song of struggle between security and risk. The feeling of knowing what is right for you in the long run but fighting the uncertainty of it. We often search for comfort and find it in places that don’t provide passion or fulfillment in any other areas of our life and the need for these things haunts us. Anything you want is about running from your dreams and your happiness only to find out that happiness and passion will always be where your heart leads you in the end. " spotify:track:5E6BASrrCqn4AaSH2iJO56:small Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P See you next time!
records.author: osornios

records.text: #MusicMonday Review - December 2025 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check November's review for more music. To finish the year, we bring you an all UK lineup, covering a whole range of genres, themes, and moods. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 Delves In Time – Big in Berlin Grey blocks rise in the freezing night The City life fades in the fading light Sound club calls and the lights go low High heels click where the young ones go I’m big in Berlin Under the shadow of the wall I hear David Bowie call Bahnhof Zoo is where we ran Losing more than we began We begin with an energetic Post Punk track, featuring an 80s inspired synth bass line that takes you to the 'Sound', a sonic evocation of german nightclubbing: "Big in Berlin was inspired by the cult 1981 film Christiane F. — I was struck by how young the kids were when they fell into that world. A trip to Berlin and visiting the film’s locations gave me a deeper connection to the story, and that atmosphere shaped the song." spotify:track:1KK9IR9hwtJGMWMro719cM:small Cheap Escape – Man Of The People Man of the people name your price You're only messing with people's lives We now travel to Kirkcaldy, Scotland for a high energy Punk Rock song featuring a catchy baseline every elected official could dance to: "Man of the People was written in the run up to the UK General Election in 2024. The song takes aim at populists from all political persuasions, in the age of social media, culture wars and disinformation. We were all just a bit annoyed at the state of things in the world, and it came out in the form of the song! The whole EP was inspired by the hopelessness brought about by the people in power and it carries a lot of that in the lyrics." spotify:track:6CboAX9rZMCNE5yYmHUmFi:small Midnight Housework – Just to be Enough It felt like love When she came around But it hurt like hell When she ran away We went different ways Down the road of life On the lonely days I still see you in my mind Do you fear the past Will taint your future life Even weary minds Will one day find the light Next stop, an bittersweet Alt Ambient Rock track from Belfast, Ireland that knows that there's no right way to live your life, just hold on tight, and enjoy the ride: "Instrumentally the song came from a guitar part that was meant to be in another song, but the band felt like the idea was strong enough to stand on its own feet and we went from there. So there wasn’t anyone or anything we were directly wanting to take influence from, it was a more organic, almost improvised process." spotify:track:4O5SBAdGF5AIuBdOOeGGFN:small Future Theory – The Haze Get out the way I need you Leave me to settle Think I'm a fool Thoughts are like whispers Dreams are like ghosts Maybe I'm young maybe I'm old We now move to Lincolnshire, England, for an atmospheric, psychedelic Alternative track about being a complicated self half the time, but positive in our minds. Are we wasting our lives? Band member Max explains: "The guitar part and vocal melody came first then [band member] Jacob added some bass. I got the lyric inspiration from things I see around me and how I've felt and how many people have felt. Something that can connect with people and that's being lost in life, not knowing your purpose, so many people seem lost. Like you feel like you're stuck in a haze that you can't get out of. Sounds a little depressing but then the song has much hope and the end really makes use of that. A sense of working your purpose out." spotify:track:6JtgtGwxUeeSg2tbvZh3sd:small The Blooms – Reminisce See my soul being changed my way I think I'm getting weaker day by day We finish in Derby, England, for an Indie Rock track whose distorted guitar sound takes us to an Indie scene gone by. Reminisce: "As simple as it sounds it just came from a writing session of taking a step back and thinking about things in the past and how times have changed within the band, all of our personal lives and within the city of Derby where we’re from, it’s mainly just a song about looking back on the past and how you can interpret it with many different moods." spotify:track:4fYjRF2l5vGAxZVAz62BnI:small Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:71fadol0hgsdSjLpM828HZ See you next time!
records.author: osornios

records.text: #MusicMonday Review - February 2026 Part 1 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check January's Part 2 review for more music. Music convey emotions. It can be by expressing love for someone, the excitement of going out, the peace of being by yourself, or even the (apparent) lack of creativity. Feel them all by giving a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 PAO ROVAL – Bucle con tan solo un segundo tu mirada me hipnotiza un abismo sin salida donde no hay final el poder de tu sonrisa es lo que me paraliza o tu simple cercania me pone a temblar Our travels begin in Torreon, Mexico, for a Latin Pop track about the pain of falling in love with someone you think is out of your league. A feeling you just can't control: " The song came from the curiosity of what it feels like to be in love. There isn’t a specific person behind it; it was simply the mindset I had at that time. I knew that if I ever found that person, I would probably resist falling in love, because I’m very selective when it comes to love and, by nature, a free soul. " spotify:track:0EdYTS0PPZxfpseBByTJGe:small Andrew Johnson – Exercise In Style I see it all as an exercise in style My integrity is whatever makes me smile I fell from grace so long ago It's all I've ever really known Let's go now to Stockton-on-Tees, England for a New Wave influenced Pop Rock track that punches straight into emotional intensity from the very beginning: " A lot of the album was written with titles and phrases as a stimulus. Exercise In Style suggests somebody “painting by numbers”, creatively. The song itself is a little bit about my experience and knowledge of the music industry but also about being defiant in the face of either criticism or, let’s face it for the most part, apathy. " spotify:track:4aQy2iB9H4MRa7C1wr8M4S:small first class rebel – Shadows Fade Watching from windows With envious intent A bank full of time Unable to be spent Dreams were measured in the past A time cloaked in fear Let's travel now to Dublin, Ireland for a track that mixes Shoegaze with a touch of Psychedelia that will make you hide in the shadows as you stare into yourself: " The guitar chords came first and I just added to them by layering different guitar lines on them. I like the sound of the guitars and that creates the mood which for me is the most important thing. Simplicity is key for me! " spotify:track:04MTLiOWtrDby0HTDlPhq3:small Abbey Moon – Snooze Put the world on snooze Don't need anyone else just you Sky was clear, everything was frozen We were suspended in a moment Any door we want we can open I know you will get me through We now travel to London, England for an Acoustic Indie track about experiencing deserted streets in company of your loved one: " So the track was inspired by a late night walk with my girlfriend in Portobello road in London. It was very quiet as it was after everything had shut on a Sunday. It felt like a special moment and it made it seem like the world was on Snooze. The track was written acoustically so I intentionally kept it quite sparse with some added lead guitar and touches of piano. I hope it sounds like a bit of a lullaby. " spotify:track:2VsNU0YYiVzy4quIOkF0JB:small Tano Mora – Aquí en Madrid Yo se poco de todo y tu muy poco de mi Tengo el tiempo del mundo que quieras compartir Pégame tu risa y después el carmín Todas estas calles se abrirán para ti Ya se acaba la noche ya se ve amanecer Me miras a los ojos y no sabes que hacer Todo lo he soñado o es realidad? Pero estas a mi lado y no quiero escapar Abre tu corazón y ahora déjame entrar Que Madrid amanece y no puedo esperar From the quiet streets of London, we finish our trip with a busy night out in Madrid, Spain, thanks to this Latin Pop track about leaving everything behind, and get ready to enjoy your evening: " Well, I wanted to reflect how special Madrid nights are. And how special it is to fall in love there. " spotify:track:1TnvOTYlC8bltGElFV7dYm:small #MusicMonday 2025 It's time to recap 2025 with this year's compilation. 76 titles from original artists covering all types of genres, represent the multiple trends independent artists were proud to establish. Have a listen: spotify:playlist:71fadol0hgsdSjLpM828HZ Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P See you next time!
records.author: osornios

metadata.title: #MusicMonday Review - February 2026 Part 1
metadata.search_snippet: #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check January's Part 2 review for more music. Music convey emotions. It can be by expressing love for someone, the excitement of going out, the peace of being by yourself, or even the (apparent) lack of creativity. Feel them all by giving a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 PAO ROVAL – Bucle con tan solo un segundo tu mirada me hipnotiza un abismo sin salida donde no hay final el poder de tu sonrisa es lo que me paraliza o tu simple cercania me pone a temblar Our travels begin in Torreon, Mexico, for a Latin Pop track about the pain of falling in love with someone you think is out of your league. A feeling you just can't control: " The song came from the curiosity of what it feels like to be in love. There isn’t a specific person behind it; it was simply the mindset I had at that time. I knew that if I ever found that person, I would probably resist falling in love, because I’m very selective when it comes to love and, by nature, a free soul. " spotify:track:0EdYTS0PPZxfpseBByTJGe:small Andrew Johnson – Exercise In Style I see it all as an exercise in style My integrity is whatever makes me smile I fell from grace so long ago It's all I've ever really known Let's go now to Stockton-on-Tees, England for a New Wave influenced Pop Rock track that punches straight into emotional intensity from the very beginning: " A lot of the album was written with titles and phrases as a stimulus. Exercise In Style suggests somebody “painting by numbers”, creatively. The song itself is a little bit about my experience and knowledge of the music industry but also about being defiant in the face of either criticism or, let’s face it for the most part, apathy. " spotify:track:4aQy2iB9H4MRa7C1wr8M4S:small first class rebel – Shadows Fade Watching from windows With envious intent A bank full of time Unable to be spent Dreams were measured in the past A time cloaked in fear Let's travel now to Dublin, Ireland for a track that mixes Shoegaze with a touch of Psychedelia that will make you hide in the shadows as you stare into yourself: " The guitar chords came first and I just added to them by layering different guitar lines on them. I like the sound of the guitars and that creates the mood which for me is the most important thing. Simplicity is key for me! " spotify:track:04MTLiOWtrDby0HTDlPhq3:small Abbey Moon – Snooze Put the world on snooze Don't need anyone else just you Sky was clear, everything was frozen We were suspended in a moment Any door we want we can open I know you will get me through We now travel to London, England for an Acoustic Indie track about experiencing deserted streets in company of your loved one: " So the track was inspired by a late night walk with my girlfriend in Portobello road in London. records.text: AI Music Transparency — A Subscribers Demand for Honesty I am writing this as a loyal, paying subscriber — someone who genuinely loves music and who has chosen to invest in your platform because I believed it was a place where real, human artistry could be discovered, celebrated, and supported. That trust is being eroded. And I want you to know why. I love soul music. I love the rawness of it, the pain in it, the joy in it — the fact that a real human being poured something of themselves into every note. Music, for me, has always been about connection. When I listen to an artist, I am connecting with another person's experience, their struggles, their love, their humanity. That relationship between listener and artist is sacred, and it is the very foundation of what makes music matter. AI-generated music is now flooding your platform — and you are letting it happen without a single word of warning to your subscribers. I have found myself listening to tracks, feeling moved, and later discovering they were produced entirely by AI via text prompts. No musician. No vocalist. No human experience behind the sound. Just an algorithm mimicking the appearance of feeling. I want to be absolutely clear: I am not here to tell people they cannot create or enjoy AI music. That is not my argument. But I have a fundamental right — as a paying customer — to know what I am listening to. Right now, that right is being denied to me. When I stream what appears to be a soul track, I deserve to know whether a real person bled into that recording or whether it was generated in seconds by a machine. The fact that I cannot tell the difference is not a testament to how good AI has become — it is a testament to how dishonest the current situation is. AI soul music might sound convincing, but it is, by its very nature, soulless. It is a imitation of human emotion without the human behind it. And discovering that I have been unknowingly listening to it feels like a betrayal. My subscription money is meant to support artists. Real artists. Human beings who have dedicated their lives to their craft. When AI-generated content is mixed indiscriminately into my feed, my recommendations, and my playlists with no labelling whatsoever, I have no way of ensuring that my money is going where I intend it to go. That is not acceptable. Here is what I am asking for, and I do not think any of it is unreasonable: — Every AI-generated track must be clearly and prominently labelled as such, before I click play. — AI music should exist in its own dedicated section of the platform, clearly signposted, so that listeners who wish to explore it can do so knowingly and willingly. — I should have a clear, accessible setting that allows me to exclude AI-generated content from my feed, my recommendations, and my playlists entirely. — AI-generated content should never be mixed into genre categories alongside human-made music without explicit disclosure. These are not radical demands. They are the bare minimum of honesty that any platform owes its users. If Spotify is unwilling or unable to implement these changes, then I must sincerely question whether my subscription is something I can continue in good conscience. I am paying for access to music — real music — and if I cannot trust that what I am hearing has been made by a human being unless I am specifically told otherwise, then I am not getting what I am paying for. In that case, I would expect a refund for the period during which I have been unknowingly exposed to unlabelled AI content. I urge you to take this seriously. I am almost certainly not alone in feeling this way. Music lovers everywhere are beginning to ask the same questions, and the platform that steps up and leads on transparency will earn enormous loyalty as a result. The platform that ignores this issue risks losing the trust of the very people who made it great. Please do right by your subscribers. Please do right by artists. And please do right by real music. DJ Chris Anthony
records.author: DJChrisAnthony

records.text: Search. - Live Events - MacOS/iOS Dear Spotify Support, The Live Events feature was a great idea, central place to consolidate a lot of music events, etc. Ever since this feature was introduced, I find myself constantly looking for new events. Previously, searching through new events, was no issue, as I kept getting recommendations relatively close to my music taste and/or based on categories I play ed . However, lately I noticed that a TON OF new events have been uploaded, and trying to find an event for an artist I know or would recognise, has become extremely difficult, I find myself now spending a lot more time just going through the HUGE LISTS of events trying to find something, than actually listening to music on Spotify. I also found the user experience for Live Events on MacOS as well as iOS is not as "cool" as it could be and I will point out some of the things that perhaps could be improved. 1. on MacOS, I get 3 Sections for Live Events : A> Discover Near You. B> Recommended for You. C> All. However, when I click on A> "Show All", there is no option to Sort by "Date". Additionally, m aybe interchange the Title and Artist Columns . On B> There is no option to View as “Compact List”, and on C> Since the List so HUGE, here the View as “Compact List” would be very helpful. But actually, I don’t know why I get the whole list, this section could be better synchronized to only show artists based on one’s music taste, what one plays or has played on Spotify, perhaps that’s what B> was meant to be, but even there, I also get some artists I would never play. 2. on iOS, similar to the above, except I get a few more sections, but ideally the same issues. A> Just for You>Show all> View as “Compact List” and Sort by “Date” would be helpful. B> Popular in [Your City]>Show all> Here the Events are not even sorted by Date. Sorting by Date and Compact List would really make people’s lives easier. C> same as in MacOS. Artists maybe pay more to be on top of the list “similar to GoogleAds”, and that’s why some artists show up at the very top more than others, but I just thought I share this with Developers, perhaps one day they find time to modify the Queries. I myself am a SysAdmin and work with DevOps, so I know how difficult it is to implement even small App changes. Thanks anyways for the support! BG
records.author: kathringrün

records.text: unusual account activity and listening history Yesterday, I was unexpectedly asked to log back into my account. At the time, I didn’t think much of it. However, today I noticed that an artist I have never listened to — Fuerza Regida — suddenly appeared in my top listening artists. I immediately checked my “Recently Played” and “Top Songs of the Month,” and found a large number of tracks that I have never listened to. The playback patterns are extremely abnormal and clearly resemble automated bot behavior — for example: ABABABAB, CDCDCDCD, EFEFEFEF loops. (the tracks are Past Struggles- Nune Aka Mr. Propane ICE CREAM - ZR Southern Lights - BigDaddyRai LES MECS - Eva Sintiendo - Castillo La Cone EXPLICIT 69 - Timar) This is very distressing because I have used this account for many years, and I value my personalized music library. Seeing these fraudulent records contaminated my history is quite frustrating. What concerns me further: I received no email notification about any suspicious login or new device access. This is not the first time this has happened — I experienced the exact same issue around 2018–2019, after which I logged out of all devices and set a strong password. Despite that, this has happened again years later, which raises serious concerns about platform security, cause I've never had a similar issue an ANY other content consuming platforms. It’s so stressful knowing this could happen again at any time. I would appreciate your help with the following: Is it possible for your technical team to help me remove or reset these specific bot-generated records? I really don't want these tracks to ruin my future recommendations or my annual summary. I didn't receive any notification about a new login. Could you please help me check if there are any suspicious authorized apps or unrecognized devices that I should be aware of? I want to make sure this doesn't happen again. I am concerned that the system might mistake me for a "bot" because of this activity. Can you confirm that my account status is safe and won't be penalized? If this cannot be resolved properly, I may have to reconsider continuing to use Spotify. Thank you for your attention to this matter. I look forward to your response. Best Regards Plan Premium Country Japan Device Macbook Air, M3, 2024 Operating System Mac OS Sonoma 14.3
records.author: Sky2D

records.text: Re: February 2026 Spotify for Developers update: thread @jsneij wrote: Request for endpoint access review — single-user personal case (Client ID eb476afb75a04275babd1ebfaec1a1b8) Hi Spotify team, Posting here following the February 2026 update. I'd like to request a case-by-case review of endpoint access for my Development Mode app "Claude Code ProjectSpotify". My case in one paragraph: I'm a single individual using the API exclusively against my own Premium account (jsneij, Premium since 2014) to manage my own library — dedupe playlists by ISRC, merge themed collections, batch-rename, archive old ones. The Spotify client supports all of this manually, but at my scale (142 playlists, ~2,400 saved tracks) it becomes prohibitive. The app runs locally on my laptop. No third-party users, no public service, no monetisation, no commercial path. User Management is 0/5 because there's no one else to add. Why I'm posting: The February 2026 post explicitly states Development Mode is meant to support "learning, experimentation, and personal projects for non-commercial use by individual developers." That's exactly my profile. However, the restricted endpoint set for newly-created Client IDs returns 403 on the core read/write playlist endpoints I need, which makes basic personal-account hygiene impossible. The March 9 update postponed these restrictions for existing integrations after community feedback — I'd appreciate that same spirit being applied to a single-user personal case. Specific endpoints requested: GET /v1/playlists/{id} (with tracks.items) GET /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks POST/PUT/DELETE on /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks Not asking for audio-features, audio-analysis, recommendations, or any November 2024 deprecated endpoints. Constraints I'd accept: Lock User Management to my single account, signed attestation, periodic review, read-only access only — whatever scoping works for Spotify. Even read-only on my own playlists' tracks would cover most of the use case. Happy to provide any additional context. Thanks for considering this! Jorge I agree with you. We also believe the personal use-cases should get a lot more freedom. There are security and legal hoops to jump through - but our ambition is to fully support your ability to manage your Spotify.
records.author: LambertSpot

records.text: Re: Fire TV now playing does not match song playing or lyrics Thanks for the response @plushkrazee and it's great to hear that you've managed to find a workaround for the playlist order issue. To help clear up any confusion, here are some answers to your other questions: The pinning option within Spotify lets you keep your most-used content at the top of your library for quick access and you can pin up to 4 items (playlists, albums, or artists). The reason why you're seeing songs in your playlists that are not in Your Library could be because of the Smart Shuffle function that keeps your listening sessions fresh by mixing in recommendations that match the vibe. This button - indicates it's turned On and by clicking on it you can disable it. More info you can also find in this article . Spotify typically displays the Top 5 Popular songs on an artist's profile on desktop and mobile, but on the desktop app, there should be a "See more" button below these songs in order to expand the list to see the Top 10. If you were referring to something else, let us know and we'll try to assist in the best possible way. When it comes to the search engine for your library and playlists not working well, consider performing a clean reinstall with the steps here and if you're still experiencing issues, it'd be very helpful if you could share as many details as possible of the exact behavior. Since the workaround with Sirius temporarily fixes the playback issue within Spotify, it could indicate an issue with how your device handles audio focus between apps. This is also something that could be fixed with a clean reinstall as suggested above, because it also contains the steps of clearing the cache (when corrupted it can cause different performance issues). Lastly, whenever it's possible try with restarting the router as suggested and let us know if that makes a difference. Hopefully your questions have been answered, but we remain a post away if there's anything else you're wondering about. Keep us posted 🙂
records.author: Ivelina

records.text: Proposal for a New Mood-Based Music Discovery IDEA Hello, My name is Jin-young Choi, and I majored in Mechanical Engineering in Korea. From a young age, I have been closely connected to music, playing the piano and clarinet as a hobby. Later, I also played the keyboard in a band with my friends. I am currently developing an application based on a new music discovery idea that is not provided by existing music platforms. I designed the UI using Figma and am developing the application with Flutter, enabling detailed feature implementation and modifications. The problem I have identified in existing music platforms is that it is difficult for users to select music that matches their specific mood and preferences. Personally, I enjoy listening to hip-hop and fast-tempo music with melodies, and music has always been a part of my daily life. However, while using various platforms, I often found it inconvenient that songs matching the mood I wanted and songs that did not match were recommended together within the same hip-hop genre. To address this limitation, I am developing a music platform. The goal of this platform is to help users find the music they want more accurately, not by simply classifying music by genre, but by categorizing songs based on mood and detailed musical characteristics. By applying the classification method I have developed, users would be able to adjust mood-based recommendations, such as those currently provided by platforms like Spotify, according to the specific range they want to listen to. This would allow users to explore music more precisely. In addition, the diverse music preference data accumulated through this platform could be expanded into a future-oriented revenue model connected to the music industry. Furthermore, by using user preference data, this platform could help predict directions for music production and support more proactive music planning and creation. Based on this idea, I would like to propose new possibilities to music production professionals and gain an opportunity to develop my concept into a real service and industry application.
records.author: jjjinyoung0511

metadata.title: Proposal for a New Mood-Based Music Discovery IDEA
metadata.search_snippet: Hello, My name is Jin-young Choi, and I majored in Mechanical Engineering in Korea. From a young age, I have been closely connected to music, playing the piano and clarinet as a hobby. Later, I also played the keyboard in a band with my friends. I am currently developing an application based on a new music discovery idea that is not provided by existing music platforms. I designed the UI using Figma and am developing the application with Flutter, enabling detailed feature implementation and modifications. The problem I have identified in existing music platforms is that it is difficult for users to select music that matches their specific mood and preferences. Personally, I enjoy listening to hip-hop and fast-tempo music with melodies, and music has always been a part of my daily life. However, while using various platforms, I often found it inconvenient that songs matching the mood I wanted and songs that did not match were recommended together within the same hip-hop genre. To address this limitation, I am developing a music platform. The goal of this platform is to help users find the music they want more accurately, not by simply classifying music by genre, but by categorizing songs based on mood and detailed musical characteristics. By applying the classification method I have developed, users would be able to adjust mood-based recommendations, such as those currently provided by platforms like Spotify, according to the specific range they want to listen to. This would allow users to explore music more precisely. In addition, the diverse music preference data accumulated through this platform could be expanded into a future-oriented revenue model connected to the music industry. Furthermore, by using user preference data, this platform could help predict directions for music production and support more proactive music planning and creation. Based on this idea, I would like to propose new possibilities to music production professionals and gain an opportunity to develop my concept into a real service and industry application.

records.text: Re: February 2026 Spotify for Developers update: thread Request for endpoint access review — single-user personal case (Client ID eb476afb75a04275babd1ebfaec1a1b8) Hi Spotify team, Posting here following the February 2026 update. I'd like to request a case-by-case review of endpoint access for my Development Mode app "Claude Code ProjectSpotify". My case in one paragraph: I'm a single individual using the API exclusively against my own Premium account (jsneij, Premium since 2014) to manage my own library — dedupe playlists by ISRC, merge themed collections, batch-rename, archive old ones. The Spotify client supports all of this manually, but at my scale (142 playlists, ~2,400 saved tracks) it becomes prohibitive. The app runs locally on my laptop. No third-party users, no public service, no monetisation, no commercial path. User Management is 0/5 because there's no one else to add. Why I'm posting: The February 2026 post explicitly states Development Mode is meant to support "learning, experimentation, and personal projects for non-commercial use by individual developers." That's exactly my profile. However, the restricted endpoint set for newly-created Client IDs returns 403 on the core read/write playlist endpoints I need, which makes basic personal-account hygiene impossible. The March 9 update postponed these restrictions for existing integrations after community feedback — I'd appreciate that same spirit being applied to a single-user personal case. Specific endpoints requested: GET /v1/playlists/{id} (with tracks.items) GET /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks POST/PUT/DELETE on /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks Not asking for audio-features, audio-analysis, recommendations, or any November 2024 deprecated endpoints. Constraints I'd accept: Lock User Management to my single account, signed attestation, periodic review, read-only access only — whatever scoping works for Spotify. Even read-only on my own playlists' tracks would cover most of the use case. Happy to provide any additional context. Thanks for considering this! Jorge
records.author: jsneij

records.text: Artist and Genre Algorithm needs improvement Dear Spotify Team, I have been a Spotify Premium subscriber for many years and have always appreciated the platform's ability to help me discover new music and artists. Unfortunately, over the past several months, I've noticed a significant decline in that experience. Many of the playlists and recommendations being presented to me now feel repetitive and stale. Different playlists often contain many of the same songs, resulting in very little actual music discovery. Instead of being introduced to new artists or tracks, I find myself hearing the same selections over and over again. Additionally, the genres and artists being recommended increasingly do not align with my listening habits. It feels as though the algorithm has become less accurate and less personalized than it once was. Rather than helping me explore new music within the genres I enjoy, it often suggests music that has little relevance to my tastes. One of the primary reasons I subscribe to a music streaming service is to discover new music. Lately, that aspect of Spotify feels largely absent, and the overall listening experience has become predictable and, frankly, boring. At the same time, subscription prices continue to increase. As a paying customer, I expect ongoing improvements to the service, particularly in areas like personalization and music discovery. Instead, I feel the experience has moved in the opposite direction. I wanted to provide this feedback because I genuinely enjoy Spotify and would prefer to remain a customer. However, if the recommendation and discovery experience continues to decline, I will seriously consider exploring alternative streaming platforms that do a better job of introducing listeners to new music. Thank you for taking the time to read my feedback. I hope it can be shared with the teams responsible for recommendations, playlist curation, and user experience.
records.author: mbotic1

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs This situation of AI-generated content flooding spotify, playlists and infecting the recommendation algorithms is getting ridiculous and completely out of control. We pay spotify premium to listen to real human‘s music, not AI slop that’s shuffled and sneaked into our (once great) recommendations or Daily-Mixes, that’s not even labeled as such. Thus is poses a major and user-unfriendly extra effort to manually filter and block out those sloppy ghostbands, which creators are just trying to profit off of it while overshadowing and driving away real music content. To be clear, I don’t consider ALL AI content as bad „music“ - some is actually good and impressive, but it MUST be transparent and be a choice to opt-in. The thing is: you expect to listen to real bands’ music and in many cases you can’t even recognize it anymore as AI, creating a feeling of betrayal when you realize it and a general distrust. There‘s no label for AI content and even typical intuitive indicators don’t work reliably anymore, as many of these ghostband creators try hard to conceal the truth it being AI slop by impersonating bands/interprets and adding plausible/believable band descriptions, images etc. This also damages the reputation and reduces the chances of newer or lesser known real interprets, as they all are now viewed under critical general suspicion with the flood of AI slop. I used to love Spotifys recommendation features to experience new similar music that fits my taste and to broaden my music/genre diversity. But the inclusion and integration of AI slop into Spotify has completely ruined this key feature for me. This situation needs to be addressed asap by Spotify. Act now or lose your customers and reputation.
records.author: Musicify

records.text: Re: Shuffle mode playing the same songs all the time The issue with Spotify's random shuffle seems to be related to it's recommendations and listening history. As long as it uses this, it could never be truly random. Using the Like system and liking any songs will absolutely ruin random shuffle for instance. The fewer repeat feature accomplishes nothing as it still cycles through the same songs on reset. You can try training the algorithm by clicking individual songs, but it is very slow at adapting. That is not all because it prioritizes songs based on their own recommendation algorithm. Some newly added songs to the playlist will mysteriously play right away when some others never seem to show up. The issue isn't fewer repeats and whatever "more human" is supposed to mean here. You need to simply not weigh in listening history and your own recommendation algorithm in random shuffle and offer "true random". I created a new small playlist and hitting play resulted in playing the exact same song as the very first. That is obviously not random when the first song is the exact same one. How about a simple playtest where the first song being played is actually random? Fewer repeats only after the first set of the same 100 songs is not what we want. Allow the option to not use listening history and your own damn recommendations. It has nothing to do with device limits and smaller chunks of data. It has to do with you forcing and weighing certain songs over others. Just like your limited selection of recommended tracks offering the same group of artists. Let us opt-out of your tracking years of our listening history. I think you know exactly what the problem is, but refuse to offer a true solution.
records.author: px7c0e9q-f_m

records.text: Switching between Family accounts (in browser, macOS, etc.) Sorry, incoming rant: On our Family Premium account, I have created a new account for my son. I can switch back and forth between these accounts on my iPhone in the Spotify app by clicking on my profile and selecting his account. Switching back is secured by a PIN. This feature does not seem to be available on ANY other platform. It's not available in the browser, in the macOS app, or in my car's Android-based app. This effectively means, that I can use my son's "account" only on my iPhone . (I cannot "log out and back in" on other devices because my son's account is not a separate account with email/pw, it's just been created from within the iPhone app as another Family account) I am not sure if Spotify understands their Family users: my playlist is currently a mix of my favourite music and children's songs. I would like to keep this separated so Spotify can give me good recommendations (which is of course messed up by the children stuff). Please don't recommend the Spotify Kids app - it has well-deserved 2 out of 5 stars on the App Store, it's basically useless. By when will it be possible to switch easily between accounts on all devices? I am not sure why Spotify thinks the current way is a good solution. All (!) other streaming services (music, video, etc.) make this possible. If you are afraid that you will make less money (wouldn't know why, but I am sure that's the reason), then at least consider introducing different "profiles" so separate listeners can create their own listening profile. I am considering cancelling Spotify, as are many of my friends, due to the completely messed up account management in Spotify.
records.author: qntmz

records.text: Allow exact track selection when creating playlists via AI / third-party integrations Spotify AI Playlist via Claude integration ignores explicit track requests. When using the Spotify MCP integration through Claude (claude.ai), the playlist creation tool does not honour specific track requests, even when tracks are listed by exact title and artist, numbered in order, with explicit instructions to make no substitutions. I tested this multiple times with increasingly specific prompts. Each time, Spotify's AI generated a thematically related but entirely different playlist, ignoring the requested tracks completely. Expected behaviour: When a user provides an explicit, ordered tracklist, the resulting playlist should contain those tracks in that order. Actual behaviour: The AI interprets the prompt thematically and substitutes its own track selection regardless of explicit instructions. Steps to reproduce: 1. Open Claude (claude.ai) 2. Ask it to create a Spotify playlist and provide a specific numbered tracklist 3. The generated playlist will not contain the requested tracks This makes the tool useful only for vague mood-based requests, not for users who want to build a specific playlist with AI assistance. A prompt override or "exact tracks" mode would fix this entirely. Additional frustration: The reason I come to Claude for playlist help is precisely to get specific, curated recommendations, not the same generic AI-generated suggestions Spotify already produces on its own. When Claude then hands off to Spotify's playlist AI, the result is doubly generic. The integration completely undermines the value of asking Claude in the first place. Users should be able to take Claude's specific track curation and have it land in Spotify exactly as intended, including the ability to search for individual tracks by title and artist, and add them to a playlist in a defined order.
records.author: nikramon

records.text: Making the "Recommended Songs" Algorithm Courageous Again Hi Spotify Team, I’m reaching out regarding my experience with Spotify recommendations so that you guys can work around it. I’ve noticed something annoying as a user over the years: back around 2019, Spotify used to give me much better variety in terms of different authors and songs I had never heard before. Nowadays, the user experience feels more like a catalog of things I’ve already listened to and just want to remember. I think you guys could change the algorithm to make it a little bit more courageous . As a user, I think most people would not complain about a song being a little bit "out" of our usual favourite artists or songs if it means discovering a new artist or a different sound. That discovery makes everything feel more magic , and it compensates for the "noise" of occasionally hearing a song we aren't used to. I’m not saying 2019 was the perfect standard, but the 2026 version feels much less adventurous. Please don't just aim to replicate 2019, it wasn't the best either, but it was definitely better. Aim for something new, dynamic, and alive, rather than something static, safe and boring . In fewer lines: The recommendations at the bottom of playlists are static and feel "stuck." They only seem to reflect the first five songs I add and never evolve as the playlist grows. I want to suggest this section to stay alive and be more courageous refreshing constantly to bring me new discoveries instead of staying the same forever. And yes, at the bottom of playlists, because playlists follow contexts like genres, moods, etc., and not only in a separate playlist with mixed discoveries only.
records.author: PauloDrWho

records.text: Re: Question of the Week: Where did your music taste begin and where is it now? Nice question! It was around late 2018 or so when my current preference for heavy psychedelic and stoner rock (as well as related genres like space rock and doom metal) started to take form, in part due to the recommendations shown on the Spotify app. Before then, my music listening habits varied quite a lot, representing genres such as jazz, progressive rock, reggae, old-school metal, Indian classical music, and US-based jam bands, among others. However, around that time and moving towards the present, psychedelic- and stoner rock became my favorite genres of music, and that’s since remained the case. It is not easy for me to remember certain favorite songs that I used to listen to a lot during the years before 2019, since I only created a Last.fm account to keep track of my listening habits around that time, and my "Your Top Songs" playlists for 2016, 2017, and 2018 are somewhat inaccurate (probably because someone had accessed my Spotify account during those years). However, off the top of my head, here are some of my favorite songs from these earlier years: spotify:track:0fzFXOXI6lV1wjolSr4OSU:small spotify:track:0r9ZSCNXKinpOBUl943m3l:small spotify:track:2ytXq1bTu0ewU7hB7GngpK:small spotify:track:48Qs11WwUFNM8BPunM9Xsu:small spotify:track:1NXypJl06i4aiD6Lpi06Sb:small spotify:track:5HO9dFj88plFIHkJJBG7hU:small And as a representation of my current music taste, I’ll share some songs from my "On Repeat" playlist that Spotify updated just two days ago: spotify:track:2qIv3Zylpfdj61H2W6oBxX:small spotify:track:5Y8pL0WdfHiT1ys4ksowfY:small spotify:track:6ceqNG5aOCKcc76sP4dp1S:small spotify:track:4yKxwphYbUraqvMsGWqI2j:small spotify:track:0lskW1AMZu0oqqMNENsIiV:small spotify:track:6y4TahhGBzkynKoJ2WCWfF:small

records.text: Artificial Intelligence Labeling Dear Spotify-Community, I have been a Spotify user for quite some time and really value discovering music by genuine, creative artists. However, over the last months I keep running into more and more tracks that feel strange somehow, only to discover that they were created by artificial intelligence artists. In my opinion, Spotify should require artists and labels to clearly mark all tracks that contain AI-generated elements, so that listeners can immediately recognize them. Spotify should also offer user settings that allow people to turn off recommendations for AI-produced songs entirely if they wish. To make these rules effective, Spotify should then introduce consequences for artists or distributors who intentionally mislabel or fail to disclose AI use, for example by limiting their visibility, removing offending tracks, or suspending their ability to upload or canceling their payment. In my opinion this would protect genuine artists, who put much work and time into producing their songs and also into practising music for years to become professionals. It would also give listeners the chance to control, if they would like to listen to AI generated songs or not. From my point of view, if this topic is not adressed by spotify, it could lead to real problems in the future, destroying the creativity, passion and genius of music artists and producers. If the creative work of real artists is not valued over quick, shallow produced, low-effort AI content, we will see less and less real artists, motivated and able to sustain a serious music career. If you feel the same way about this topic, please support my idea by giving it a vote!!
records.author: paul150

In my opinion this would protect genuine artists, who put much work and time into producing their songs and also into practising music for years to become professionals. It would also give listeners the chance to control, if they would like to listen to AI generated songs or not. From my point of view, if this topic is not adressed by spotify, it could lead to real problems in the future, destroying the creativity, passion and genius of music artists and producers. If the creative work of real artists is not valued over quick, shallow produced, low-effort AI content, we will see less and less real artists, motivated and able to sustain a serious music career. If you feel the same way about this topic, please support my idea by giving it a vote!!

records.text: Re: Wont load/Something went wrong I am experiencing the exact same issue on my device. I have already tried every solution mentioned in this thread, but unfortunately none of them worked. My device is an Honor X7b running Android 14 . I previously had the beta version of Spotify installed, but I completely removed it and installed the official stable version instead (9.1.44.2120) . The strange thing is that I can successfully log into any Spotify account without issues. However, all accounts experience the same problem afterward. For comparison, Spotify works perfectly on my Samsung Galaxy Note 20 5G using the same accounts and network connection. On the Honor phone, the app appears to have internet access because I can see my playlists and saved content. Most strangely, I can still access the Search tab, and it loads playlists, recommendations, and stories — just not the actual music content that used to appear before. However: Opening playlists shows no songs inside Even my own created playlists appear empty, although I can still add songs via Search Search results return “Song not found” Stories and previews also fail to load properly The Premium tab is accessible as well. So it seems the app can partially connect to Spotify’s servers, but it fails to properly load music content — exactly as others described in this topic. I have already tried the following troubleshooting steps: Reinstalling the app (both standard and clean reinstall) Logging out and back in Testing with multiple Spotify accounts Clearing cache and app data Resetting network settings Force restarting the device after each step Unfortunately, none of these solutions resolved the issue.
records.author: Sachi_xCh

records.text: Spotify Smart Shuffle always generates the same track order Plan Premium Duo Country Croatia Device Windows PC Samsung Galaxy S23+ Operating System Windows 11 Pro (version 25H2) Android 16 (kernel 5.15.178) My Question or Issue The Smart Shuffle feature always produces the exact same track order every time I activate it. The order is different from the default playlist order, but it never changes between Smart Shuffle activations. For example, when I enable Smart Shuffle on a playlist, Spotify rearranges the songs and adds recommendations as expected. However, if I turn Smart Shuffle off and then enable it again later, the playlist appears in the exact same shuffled order as before. It does not generate a new random order. By contrast, the normal Shuffle function behaves correctly: each time I enable regular Shuffle, the playlist order changes. The behaviour is identical on Windows and Android apps. What I expected to happen: Each time I activate Smart Shuffle, Spotify should generate a new shuffled order (similar to normal Shuffle), possibly with different recommended tracks. What actually happens: Smart Shuffle always uses the same fixed order every time it is activated. Steps to reproduce: Open Spotify and start a playlist. Enable Smart Shuffle. Note the track order generated by Smart Shuffle. Turn Smart Shuffle off. Enable Smart Shuffle again. Observe that the playlist order is exactly the same as the previous Smart Shuffle order. Troubleshooting already attempted: Tested on both desktop and Android apps Confirmed that normal Shuffle generates a different order each time Restarted the apps and repeated the test The issue persists across devices.
records.author: disposabledominik

records.text: A Miracle Found in a Forest of Over 100 Million Songs Sorry for my English. I’m a Japanese user, but I wanted to share my experience. If you want to enjoy not only listening to your favorite music, but also discovering new music, I truly recommend trying Spotify. At first, I found Spotify’s features—such as automatically inserting recommended songs or creating personalized playlists—to be a bit intrusive and unnecessary. However, I realized something that completely changed my perspective. There is a physical limit to how many songs a person can listen to in a lifetime. Even if we assume a generous pace of 50 new songs every day, from age 10 to the average Japanese lifespan of about 84 years, we would still only be able to hear about 1.3% of Spotify’s library of over 100 million songs. Once I understood this, the way I enjoy music changed. Listening repeatedly to music I already love is comforting, of course. But by opening myself to Spotify’s recommendations, I began to encounter unexpected discoveries—songs I would never have found on my own. It feels similar to how a newborn child hears nothing but new sounds for the first time. Spotify somehow brings me back to that sense of curiosity and innocence, as if I were exploring a vast, untouched forest of music. For anyone who truly loves music, this is a service I hope you will experience. Today again, in my car, I’m looking forward to enjoying another one-in-a-hundred-million miracle.
records.author: MusicForestExplorer

records.text: Re: What if Spotify just knew — the moment you opened it? Spotify has an AI curated playlist feature in beta right now where you can type in a prompt, but it never works that well and glitches out when you try adding or removing songs then enter a new prompt. I don’t think it understands “moods” because it often gives completely different music than what I want it to and even some random songs in another language that sound nothing like what I prompted. Spotify has also made it clear that it will add sponsored music into your recommendations, so those random songs might be related to that. They also tried out the AI feature in last year’s wrapped and it went horribly, people were very displeased. Knowing how poorly this feature works, along with the environmental impact due to the immense amount of resources needed, if Spotify were to implement even more AI into the streaming service I know a huge population of its users would opt for its removal. It sounds like a nice idea if you don’t want to put in the work to find your own songs, but in the long run I can see it frustrating you more when they throw in random sponsored songs that have nothing to do with what you want. In all honesty, if you want songs with certain sounds, try testing out Spotalike or even the AI Playlist feature. If those don’t work out I highly doubt built in AI would be any better.
records.author: criscantspell

records.text: Re: Playback issues: Songs going silent on Android app This happens to me literally every single time I try to listen using a wired connection and firmly believe that if Samsung had kept an aux port, this wouldnt be an issue. The day, time and time zone this has last occurred on: moat recently, 10+ times today alone, 5/25/26 between 7am-12pm PST. Your device's OS version: Android The version of Spotify you're running: 9.1.50.1906 Does the app switch to the next song or is it stuck on the current one (with the timer still counting)? The app never skips songs or pauses them when this happens - Spoitfy continues playing the song but there is no audio output. I usually have to unplug my connection twice to get things working again. Whether this occurs when playing from the mobile device directly or when connected to another device (Bluetooth, headphones, etc.): this happened exclusively when trying to listen through a wired audio connection. It seems like this happens every time my phone drops from 5G to 4G. I have set my Spotify app to listen offline and use downloaded playlists and it still happens. Have done all of the troubleshooting recommendations with no solutions. I can get music playing again after reconnecting the wired connection, but it might stop again seconds later. The ONLY solution for me has been to listen on airplane mode, which sort of defeats the purpose of being able to listen on my phone.

records.text: design changes Hey Spotify team, I’ve been a loyal user for many years and truly love the service — the recommendations, playlists, audio quality, and overall experience are still the best out there. But if I’m being honest, the app’s visual design has started to feel really dated and hasn’t had a meaningful refresh in quite a while. The interface now looks noticeably older compared to many other modern music apps (and even compared to some of Spotify’s own marketing visuals). It desperately needs to become more contemporary, clean, and aesthetically pleasing — something more minimalistic , with better use of space, refined typography, smoother animations, and a truly premium feel. For the iOS version in particular, it would be amazing if you could finally adopt Apple’s new Liquid Glass design language from iOS 26. That translucent, dynamic, glass-like material with subtle depth and light refraction looks incredible in so many apps right now — it would pair beautifully with album art, vibrant colors, and the emotional side of music. Several designers have already shared really stunning Spotify concepts using Liquid Glass, and they feel so much more immersive and fresh. Right now the app just feels visually “stuck” while everything else around it (including the OS itself) has moved forward. A thoughtful redesign wouldn’t just be cosmetic — it would make the whole experience feel exciting and premium again. Please consider giving the UI some serious love in the coming updates. Many of us would really appreciate it.
records.author: alexsptf

records.text: Liked Songs shuffle starts playing recommendations after locking iPhone Plan Premium Country South Korea Device iPhone16pro Operating System iOS26.2.1 My Question or Issue Hello, I primarily listen using Liked Songs with Shuffle enabled and set to reduce repetition. My expectation is that playback continues only within my Liked Songs in random order. However I am seeing different behavior depending on how playback is started. When I open Liked Songs, scroll through the list, select a track, and let it continue playing, the playback continues randomly within Liked Songs only. This is the behavior I expect. When I open Liked Songs, use the search bar at the top to find a specific song, select that song, and keep the app open, the playback also continues randomly within Liked Songs only. This is also correct. The issue happens when I open Liked Songs, use the search bar to find a specific song, select that song, and then immediately send the Spotify app to the background and lock the iPhone. After the selected song ends, it plays one or two songs from Liked Songs, then suddenly switches to recommended tracks that are not in my Liked Songs. I would like to understand why playback starts injecting recommended songs only in this situation. I want playback to remain strictly within Liked Songs regardless of whether I searched for the track or locked the device. I am using an iPhone with the latest iOS version and the Spotify app is updated to the latest version.
records.author: gantodagee

records.text: Re: How to get rid of recommendations of r*ssian music? Hi there! To reduce or stop recommendations of Russian and Belarusian music on Spotify, you can try the following: Use the "Hide" or "Don't play this artist" option: When you see a Russian or Belarusian artist or song recommended, select the three-dot menu next to it and choose “Don’t play this artist.” This signals Spotify’s algorithm to avoid similar content. Like and listen more to your preferred artists and genres: The more you listen to Ukrainian, English, Japanese, Taiwanese, Estonian, and Lithuanian music and actively like those songs and artists, the more Spotify’s recommendations will reflect your taste. Create playlists with only your preferred music: Spotify uses your playlists and saved music to suggest new songs. Building playlists focused on your favorite regions can help shift recommendations. Clear your Spotify cache and reset your recommendations: Sometimes clearing cache or even starting fresh (e.g., temporarily logging out or reinstalling the app) helps reset the algorithm’s assumptions. Use private sessions when listening to music you don’t want influencing recommendations: If you listen to something outside your usual preferences, do it in a private session so it doesn’t affect your recommendations. Block or remove disliked artists from your library: Since you’re already blocking Russian artists, continue doing this actively. While Spotify’s algorithm isn’t perfect and may take time to adjust, consistent use of these methods should reduce unwanted recommendations. Hope this helps!
records.author: Koyubi

records.text: Feature Request: Direct Track-Add API for Third Party Playlist Management I use Claude (Anthropic's AI assistant) integrated with Spotify to get personalized music recommendations and create playlists. The experience highlights a significant limitation in the current integration: all playlist creation is routed through Spotify's AI playlist generator, which produces unreliable results. The problems with the current AI-based approach: • Hits token limits and fails silently, resulting in playlists that don't appear in my library • Substitutes incorrect tracks when it can't find exact matches, with no notification • Generates generic results that don't reflect the specific tracks being requested • Slow and resource-intensive compared to a simple database operation The irony is that I get significantly better music recommendations from Claude than from Spotify's AI playlist feature. But when it comes to actually building the playlist, we're forced to route through a less capable AI layer that introduces errors and failures. The fix is straightforward: a direct track-add API endpoint that accepts track IDs and adds them to a playlist. Licensing and content controls are already solved at the track metadata level and don't require AI interpretation. This would be faster, cheaper for Spotify to run, more reliable for users, and produce better results than the current AI middleman. This isn't a request to remove AI playlist features — keep them for users who want them. This is a request for a reliable programmatic alternative that lets third party integrations like Claude deliver their full value to Spotify users.
records.author: MarkDanielW

records.text: Re: Desktop: New Your Library sidebar My time at Spotify is over. It's no longer just the UI/UX, which is chaotic. Right now on tablet it's terrible. I think the only app that has improved is the one for the TV. My problem is EVERYTHING. The Podcast recommendations that I don't want to hear, the constant changing of the recommendation carousel. It's almost worse than Instagram, you can't focus on what's important: LISTENING TO MUSIC. They don't want you to listen to your music and discover something new every once in a while, they want you to waste as much time as possible in their app. Sometimes I go in, I see an album that appeals to me from the recommendations but I don't save it. A few minutes later I go back in and they've changed, I no longer know which album that is. Favorites get lost, they get duplicated, you can access the same albums from different links. In one link your favorite songs from that album are marked, from another link to the same damn album there are no songs marked. If you mark one as a favorite that is already on the other album, they appear twice in the favorites list, but if you remove one, both are removed! Ultimately, I don't know why I'm wasting my time here. Nothing is going to change. In a couple of months I'm going to Qobuz.

records.text: Re: Clear the queue Hey everyone! I'm not a spotify moderator, but hopefully this post will help. Essentially, what is happening is that you should see something that is under "Now playing" (the song you are currently listening to). After that, you might see either the phrase "Next in queue" or "Next up". To be completely clear, these are two different things. "Next in queue" is whatever song, playlist, album, podcast, etc that you clicked on and clicked "Add to queue" and is prioritized over "Next up", therefore if you simply add in your thing into the queue and suddenly want to move to a different thing without eventually returning to your original thing, you can click the "Clear queue" button to the right of "Next in queue" to clear it completely, then add into queue what you wish to listen to and hit play. If what you want to listen to is under the "Next up" section, then there is no need to add it to the queue. The "Next up" section is where all of your songs that you've listened to already start to accumulate, along with spotify recommendations if you have that on. Think of it as a playlist full of old/recommended music. There is no clear all button for the "Next up", but you can try to log out/close spotify, perhaps it might clear it automatically. Hope this helps!
records.author: enhypen_fan

records.text: Remove Certain Song Recommendations For Each Playlist In each playlist, I usually end up getting similar recommendations despite there being different vibes in each. I would like to go through the recommendations for a single playlist without getting the same songs over and over that I don't want to add - I blocked Mother Mother (despite still liking some of the songs) because that was ALL I was getting recommended in each playlist. This applies to a lot of other songs and artists too, and a lot of the time they're songs I've already worn out and I want new ones. I'd like there to be a way to remove a song recommendation from each playlist (without it removing the song from every playlist) and not get the recommendation again, instead getting new, fresher recommendations. I would like to unblock Mother Mother without it flooding my recommendations for each playlist, and instead have a "dislike" or "hide" kind of button for the recs. I know there is the playlist shuffle recommendations, and that's decent, but I seriously just get the same 10 songs and they come back even if I hit the dislike button. But, I like going through the "add" section at the top of the playlists and the "recommended songs" at the bottom, and would like an option to remove certain songs from those sections and have them NOT come back as recommendations.
records.author: carbinnnate

This applies to a lot of other songs and artists too, and a lot of the time they're songs I've already worn out and I want new ones. I'd like there to be a way to remove a song recommendation from each playlist (without it removing the song from every playlist) and not get the recommendation again, instead getting new, fresher recommendations. I would like to unblock Mother Mother without it flooding my recommendations for each playlist, and instead have a "dislike" or "hide" kind of button for the recs. I know there is the playlist shuffle recommendations, and that's decent, but I seriously just get the same 10 songs and they come back even if I hit the dislike button. But, I like going through the "add" section at the top of the playlists and the "recommended songs" at the bottom, and would like an option to remove certain songs from those sections and have them NOT come back as recommendations.

records.text: Loved Songs I think Spotify should have a second tier above regular liked songs called “Loved Songs.” A lot of people probably use likes the way you think — if you enjoy a song, you like it. But that also means it's easy to end up with a lot of liked songs that all get treated the same, even though there are certain tracks I'm sure we all care about way more than others. For example, I like every song from Hail The Sun but there are still a smaller number of songs that I come back to constantly and consider my personal favorites. Right now the only real way to separate those songs is by making a playlist. But that would mean creating a separate playlist for every artist whose discography I fully enjoy, just to isolate a handful of my absolute favorites. It would be cool if Spotify added: the current green checkmark for liked songs a second option, like a red heart, for “Loved Songs” Then users could: filter only loved songs from an artist shuffle only loved songs quickly revisit their favorite tracks without needing extra playlists I also think this could help Spotify recommendations since it gives a stronger signal about which songs users actually connect with the most, instead of treating every liked song equally.
records.author: Zero011

records.text: Re: The ability to toggle off podcast suggestions on the homepage Amen. I can not express how much this has degraded my experience. The amount of bloat this product has now is just the head of the slow encroachment of suggested content that nobody asked for. Like, I took the time to come to this obscure community page so that other eyes could read how exhausting this is. You don't think I have other things I'd rather do than to come complain about this feature that will probably get worse regardless of what i say? I used to enjoy the recommendations. I simply can not anymore. I simply can not sense what want of mine these recommendations are filling. How many times do I have select that I'm not interested in podcasts before you stop recommendations? And then today I find an immovable block of podcast recommendations right in between the top section of varying usefulness, and the bottomless gradient of music that potentially fits my tastes to whatever happens below where I'm willing to scroll. The experience has become deeply disconnected from how personal an experienced it felt like long long, longlonglong, long ago. I thought I was getting fed up with it a couple years ago. Now I'm searching for alternatives.

records.text: Re: ripoff Hey there @ripoff101 , Thanks for reaching out. I get that audiobook hours can feel limited if audiobooks are the main thing you're using Spotify for. Longer titles can use up the monthly listening time pretty quickly. That said, it's worth keeping in mind that Premium Family is primarily a music and audio plan for multiple people in the same household. The plan includes things like ad-free music listening, offline downloads, on-demand playback, multiple individual accounts, personalized recommendations, playlists, podcasts, and other Premium features for the family members on the plan. The audiobook listening time is more of an added benefit on top of the Premium plan, rather than the whole plan being priced only around audiobooks. For some people, the included hours are enough for occasional audiobook listening. For heavier audiobook listeners, extra hours or a separate audiobook option may make more sense depending on how much they listen each month. I definitely understand the feedback though. It would be great to see more flexible audiobook options in the future, especially for Family plan members who listen a lot. If audiobook value is the main concern, it's worth sharing that feedback in the Ideas section so other users can vote and add their thoughts too.
records.author: JBayudz

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs @Vasil wrote: Hey folks! We're seeing your feedback and, in this regard, we'd like to share some more info on the matter ‌ 🙂 ‌ Spotify is introducing clearer AI disclosures in the song credits, as well as stricter impersonation rules! These updates will help protect artists’ identities, ensure royalties are distributed fairly, and give you - the listeners - more transparency. Note that AI disclosures do not affect how the music is treated for recommendations. For more info in regard to these new introductions, see the Newsroom article here. That's all for now, folks! Stay tuned for possible updates in the future. LOL, what a terrible reply. I don't want AI slop clogging up my playlists (personal OR Spotify created), recommendations, or my time. If a viable solution is not offered to eliminate the chance of this nonsense finding its way into my earholes, I will cancel. I will move to a platform that respects its userbase, or I will go back to pirating as I did as a teen in the 90s, and then use Navidrome to listen to my collection. BTW, you still haven't added personal play counts, star ratings, and smart playlists. Standard features iTunes had in the early f'n 2000s.

records.text: Re: Your Episodes, Remove finished episodes after playing doesn't work It's frustrating that Spotify hasn't communicated any progress on this issue. The way I use the Your Episodes playlist is to enable automatic downloads on my favourite shows so that new episodes are downloaded and added to Your Episodes alongside any episodes that I have added ad-hoc based on recommendations and search. I typically listen to this playlist via a Siri Shortcut (Hey Siri, in Spotify play Your Episodes), but because the playlist still contains episodes which haven't been automatically removed when finished, I end up restarting episodes I've already heard, unless I remember to open the app and manually remove each download. As partial workaround, I have ended up disabling the automatic downloads for all my shows. Instead I use a Python script found on github called "auto-spotify-podcast-playlist" which checks each of the shows I follow and adds any new episodes to the Your Episodes playlist. I have the script scheduled to run in the background a few times a day on my Mac. It's an extreme workaround but others might find it useful while we wait for Spotify to address the issue.
records.author: patthedog

records.text: Re: Just make it make sense... Yes, removing songs of a genre/style is what I’m doing. Unless there’s a mystical “Delete Genre” option I don’t know about, that’s what it means. Basically all the songs that sound similar to each other, in terms of the style I do not want to hear, are being ousted. Considering the entire post is centered around Smart Shuffle, I would definitely say that it factors into the issue, if not being the entire issue itself. Ever since I’ve been removing said similar songs, all in the same style I do not want to hear, Smart Shuffle is flooding my poor ears with that style. It hasn’t recommended me that style in years, but the second I clean out my library, for the purpose of avoiding it as a potential recommendation in the future, Smart Shuffle decides to only recommend that style. I’m mostly talking about Liked Songs though, because I’ve honestly given up on Playlist recommendations entirely as Spotify has apparently condemned each and every one of my Playlists to the having their own personal ~20 recommendations, forever unshuffled.

records.text: Spotify keeps "recommending" inappropriate songs Plan Free/Premium Country Canada Device Web Operating System Web My Question or Issue When I play a specifically curated spotify playlist which I've set up for my children (on an account that has literally no other activity on it), spotify keeps slipping in "recommended" songs/music. I assume this is part of how they support the free tier, so I'm not bothered by this in and of itself. What does bother me is the fact that my profile is completely kids music, and suddenly a rap song with lyrics that make it absolutely inappropriate to play near children is inserted. Attempting to serenade my children with swears and racial slurs is not going to make me buy a premium subscription, it's just going to encourage me to go back to digitally copying my music collection so that I can know that I won't have to run across the room and pull the speaker to avoid having to explain what "b** can *** my ***" means. No matter how many times I say "don't recommend this" or "don't play songs like this", spotify finds a way to do it anyway.
records.author: Dravish
