records.text: Re: Take a Break From Songs Without Deleting Them Hi, @Gabben , You can hide songs in playlists from the three dots menu via the (-) / X buttons or from the option in the three dots menu. Songs you've hidden won't be played by the app and will instead be skipped immediately. You can undo this anytime by repeating this process. Check out the screenshots below for an in-depth view: Spoiler (Highlight to read) Three dots menu. Song is now hidden and won't be played. Three dots menu.Song is now hidden and won't be played. ⚠️ Note : Hiding a track makes it unplayable only in that specific playlist/album. This means that you'll still be able to play it everywhere else, unless you manually hide it there as well. On the mobile app, there's also an option to "snooze" hidden tracks in certain playlists, thus excluding them in all recommendations and across all platforms for 30 days. That way, you can keep things fresh while leaving the door open for a future reunion. There's more info in this article , check it out, if you're interested. Hope you found this useful. Cheers 🤘
records.author: Yordan

records.text: automatically add recommended songs to a playlist So I am frequently listenings to new genres and making personal playlists, and whenever I'm doing so I like to go to the recommended songs for that playlist. However when the music is good I can easily forget I need to add the songs to the playlist, especially when focussing on school projects for example. And when I remember to do that I'm already 10 songs past and don't kow which ones to add. Now I would really love an option where I can let spotify add the songs from the recommended list to my playlist whenever I didn't skip it. It's a lot easier to get a nicely sized playlist than needing to actively think if I want to add it or not for every song, and interrupting my work to do so. And for if you fall asleep or let it play without listening, a tag could be added to all recently added songs like this that goes away after opening the playlist again and scrolling to the end, so you could decide to not add them.

records.text: Re: February 2026 Spotify for Developers update: thread Hello, I am the developer of Apollo, a music-driven social discovery application that integrates with the Spotify Web API. Apollo uses Spotify-authorized data such as top artists, top tracks, and recently played content to personalize recommendations and introduce users to new music. Listening is directed back to Spotify via deep links and native playback. I am currently reviewing the Extended API Access requirements and noticed that the application process appears to require a high monthly active user threshold and supporting market data. As an early-stage production app, this creates a challenge where certain features depend on extended access, but reaching that scale may depend on those same features. I wanted to ask if there is any recommended path for applications in this stage. Specifically, I am trying to understand how developers can best align with Spotify’s platform goals while operating within standard access limitations, and whether there are alternative approaches or considerations for apps that are not yet at large scale. Any guidance would be appreciated. Thank you.
records.author: ApolloInteractive

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs @ jazzmanjohn Did that for 10 years, got slop anyway. I don't think you understand how the algorithms these systems use work. Sadly a lot of slop is pushed not based on your preferences or playlists, but as sponsored/paid content, Spotify is actively being paid to push this garbage on us. On top of that you have botnets pulling crazy numbers in fake streams, but stream numbers are only part of it, to hide their activity they also stream legit artists, so if you listen to any of those legit artists that the botnets fake-play the algorithm thinks you might also like the slop those botnets are actively trying to inflate. Greed + Gaming the system = Slop in your playlists. My Spotify recommendations were so damned near perfect, then the tsunami hit early last year. Perhaps you have been lucky so far, perhaps your personal music interests simply don't include songs popular enough to be fake-streamed yet, but eventually the AI bots will come for you to, it's just a matter of time.

records.text: Best way to read EPUB files on browser while listening to Spotify? Hi everyone, I usually listen to Spotify while reading ebooks (mostly EPUB format), and I was looking for a simple way to read EPUB files directly in the browser without installing heavy apps. Most desktop readers are slow, and switching between Spotify and a reader app breaks the flow. So I started testing some browser-based EPUB readers to keep everything in one tab while music keeps playing. I recently found a lightweight web EPUB reader that runs fully in the browser — no install, no login — and it actually works smoothly alongside Spotify. It supports dark mode, bookmarks, and fast loading even for large EPUB files. If anyone else reads while listening to music or podcasts, this kind of tool is surprisingly useful. I’m curious what others here use: Do you read ebooks while streaming music? Any recommendations for distraction-free reading setups? For reference, this is the browser reader I tested: 👉 https://epubreaderpro.com Would love to hear what tools or setups you all prefer.
records.author: Jindo_US

records.text: option to play/shuffle all music in your library i have countless artists and albums in my library, not to mention playlists (130 by my count (on THIS relatively recent account)), and i think at this point i probably regularly listen to most of the main umbrella genres. i'm often in a mood where i either don't know what i want to listen to, or i want a full mixed bag of everything i listen to. i do actually really like the way liked songs works now as i use it to either save recommendations from friends or highlight my favourite songs in albums and playlists, but i would really love (read: am so, so desperate for) a feature where i can shuffle and/or play all of the music that is saved in my library. ideally, i'd like to be able to shuffle/play through the categories (whole library, playlists, albums, artists, etc), or even split it into genres, but i've been begging for a playlist folder option for yonks, and beggars cant be choosers or w/e.

records.text: Recommended Songs Refresh button not working Plan Premium Duo Country United States Device iPhone 13, Macbook Pro 2023 My Question or Issue The Recommended Songs button seemed to provide good suggestions for songs I may want to add to my playlist (debatable according to some users it seems). Either the refresh button is not working or my hunch is that it’s now being powered by an LLM instead of Spotify’s proprietary “AI”. Most of the time, I get the same songs back after I press refresh, but occasionally (on my Mac only) I will get a subset or superset of the few songs it has continued to recommend…hence my guess that you have moved to using an LLM. It’s bad, I don’t want any of the 5 or so songs it has presented, but refreshing does nothing to the list. Edit: this seems to be specific to a long playlist (~150 songs). It still doesn’t seem reasonable that there are no other good suggestions to make.
records.author: tcuth

records.text: Artist songs in my playlist to appear and act as an album (in-playlist grouping) I wanted to listen to mobb deep today, specifically the songs I have saved in my playlist, but when I search for mobb deep in my playlist and their saved songs appear, they don't appear in a group where I can treat them as I would my regular playlist (play, shuffle play, or smart shuffle). Instead they appear as seperate songs I can choose to add to a queue. My idea is that it would be really helpful if the user could get an "album" of the specific artist they searched for in their playlist where that "album" would act like a playlist does, and that smart shuffle in that album would actually recommend songs specifically from that artist's tracks (it could be a new smart shuffle feature specific for this) on top of the regular smart shuffle that would function as general recommendations within the genre and as close to that specific artist's style as possible.
records.author: do9g1fyqtgxpiy28pdlk

records.text: Re: Half of the weekly playlists are filled with AI generated content Spotify Strengthens AI Protections for Artists, Songwriters, and Producers — Spotify Five months later, there are still no visible results from these promised protections. In fact, the opposite has happened: the platform is being flooded with AI‑generated content more than ever. Spotify announced stronger rules against impersonation, spam, and mass‑generated “slop,” and even talked about new spam filters and AI disclosures. But none of this seems to be working in practice. The amount of low‑effort AI music keeps growing, and it’s now affecting playlists, recommendations, and the overall listening experience. Real artists and real listeners are still the ones paying the price, while AI content farms continue to upload thousands of tracks without consequences. Spotify said they removed 75 million spam tracks and would roll out new systems to protect the ecosystem, but the situation today shows that these measures are either not active or not effective. This needs real enforcement, not just announcements.
records.author: memorpo

This needs real enforcement, not just announcements.

records.text: Re: Annoying “Hidden for 30 days” pop-up appears every time I hide a song Hey folks, Thanks for reaching out! When developing the 'Hide song' feature, we found that many seem to use it as a method to influence the algorithm's suggestions, so we've added an additional functionality where users will be able to ' snooze ' hidden tracks, thus excluding them in all recommendations and across all platforms for 30 days. That way, you can keep things fresh while leaving the door open for a future reunion. That being said, we also hear you that hiding a particular song means you don't want to hear it ever again. Your feedback on this is important, as it may ultimately end up paving the way for our broader user experience. Thanks for sharing it here - we appreciate it. 💚 There's more info on the Hide song feature in this article , check it out if you're interested. The Community is just a post away if something else comes up.
records.author: Yordan

records.text: Fix DJ so it doesn’t just hit play on the “On Repeat” playlist Dear Spotify, When the DJ was first introduced, it was fantastic! I’d get blocks of songs that I might like, deep dives into my music history, and just generally a great mix. As time has went on however, this mix has stopped. I barely use the DJ feature anymore because all it ever does is hit play on my “on repeat” playlist. Constantly. Even when it finishes a block and moves on to something “new”, it’s typically just a different set of songs from my on repeat playlist. I miss the DJ feature of old. I’m more than capable of pressing randomize on my most listened songs. Bring back the DJ that dug up great song recommendations based on my taste, through in nostalgic throwbacks, and played songs that I liked but hadn’t listened to in a while. Sincerely, One of music’s biggest fans
records.author: Drew11194

records.text: (Playlists) Replace the Unused Shorts Button On Playlists With Recommended Songs Overall Idea: You know that useless button on the top left of your Playlists? What if we replaced that useless shorts button with an actually useful feature? You know the small flip through and curated spng suggestions you sometimes find on your feed? What if we placed the "Find new songs for your playlists" thing to that useless button on thr top left of your Playlist screen, wouldn't that be way more useful? Opinions: Also, I personally dont like the current state of the recommended songs on the bottom of the playlists, since it only goes by artists and albums, not the genre or "feel" of the Playlist like those little slideshows do, so it would also improve on that regard as well. If you have any suggestions on making this better in any way, contact me below, and I might add it to the suggestion in the coming days. 🙂

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs @PJ40 Weird they removed your posts for promoting other apps, they've left practically all of mine alone, and I've been quite a prolific poster the past couple of months. Honestly, I would prefer if Spotify just fixed the problem, they do have a far better recommendation system than most other platforms (prior to the AI slop tsunami that is), usually nailing my tastes after only a few weeks of use, sadly Deezer, after 3 months, hasn't quite figured me out yet and keeps recommending a lot of duds. I think I found 1 track from this weeks recommendations that I enjoyed enough to add to my usual rotation. But they don't seem to be interested in fixing anything as it continues to line their pockets for the time being, so until then I will continue to promote the mass exodus to alternate platforms hoping that the drop in revenue might actually spark some change.

records.text: Let Us More Directly Influence Daylists As part of a study at my university, I've been recording some of the recommendations and topics of my various daylists throughout the week. One thing I've noticed is that my daylist has almost nothing in common with my current listening habits. It's all music I listened to months ago, and despite using the app fairly frequently, my daylist algorithm is ignoring my new tastes. Having the ability to more directly influence or provide feedback to our daylists, even if it's just simple "Good playlist" or "Bad Playlist" feedback options, would be very useful for allowing users to tune their experiences to match our current tastes, while still preserving the convenience and novelty of our daylists. If I can tell the DJ to switch it up, I should be able to do this as well. It can't be harder than implementing AI, song mixing, and everything else in here. Make it happen please.
records.author: BlueSpook

records.text: Add option to avoid AI-generated / synthetic artists Hi Spotify team, I’d really appreciate it if you would add a setting that lets me avoid AI-generated or synthetic artists. I don’t have anything against AI in general, but when I listen to music, I want real musicians, real voices and real creativity. Right now it’s impossible to tell what’s human and what isn’t, and some AI projects show up in my recommendations next to real bands. That feels misleading, and it affects how I use the platform. What I’m asking for is pretty simple: a toggle to filter out AI-generated artists clear labeling when a track or artist is AI-driven better verification so synthetic projects don’t slip into the catalogue unnoticed I think a lot of listeners feel the same way. Transparency would make the experience better for everyone and help support real musicians. Thanks for taking it seriously.

records.text: How satisfied are you with Spotify right now? Your experiences? Hey everyone, I’m collecting real user experiences for a university project and would love to hear your opinions on Spotify — good or bad. Here are a few points I’m especially interested in: 1. General usage: Is Spotify stable for you? Any bugs, slow loading, or interface issues? 2. Recommendations: Do the suggestions match your taste, or do playlists feel repetitive? 3. Pricing & subscription: How do you feel about the current pricing? Are you using Premium or Free — and what do you like or dislike? 4. Content (music/podcasts): Are any artists/tracks missing? Are you satisfied with the podcast and audiobook offering? 5. Audio quality & settings: How’s the sound quality for you? Do you use EQ settings or notice any limitations? 6. Device compatibility: Any problems with smart speakers, car systems, or synchronization? Thanks a lot for sharing your experience — every insight helps! 🙌
records.author: JoeShmoe21

records.text: Re: [Web API] Creating Playlist as Public Thank you @Ximzend for your reply on this issue.I am however still confused about it because if the "public: false" attribute doesn't actually change the playlist to truly private (publicly inaccessible even with the link), then it just making it publicly hidden / indiscoverable (not appearing on the user profile, recommendations or search results). It's actually a different state for a public playlist, "hidden". However the real problem is that the API doesn't seem to expose that state in GET /playlists as far as I can see - when calling POST /users/{user_id}/playlist with "public: false" and then calling GET /playlists/{playlist_id} the returned flag would still be "public: true", but that just tells us the playlist is not private - it doesn't actually tell us whether it is truly public or hidden. So how can we know that state from the API? Is there another flag for it?
records.author: yuvalr80

It's actually a different state for a public playlist, "hidden". However the real problem is that the API doesn't seem to expose that state in GET /playlists as far as I can see - when calling POST /users/{user_id}/playlist with "public: false" and then calling GET /playlists/{playlist_id} the returned flag would still be "public: true", but that just tells us the playlist is not private - it doesn't actually tell us whether it is truly public or hidden. So how can we know that state from the API? Is there another flag for it?

records.text: Re: Spotify doesn't download all songs in the offline playlists Yes, exactly! Thanks, @onlytocomplain ! It appears that the songs are interlinked for stream count reasons, as on Spotify this is tracked per-song and everything is aggregated. So due to this interlinking, a song with the same content can appear as a "previous version" of itself, but is ultimately one and the same and shouldn't influence the listening experience. Some artists may do this to expand their reach to new audiences, like having the "previous version" show up only in personalized recommendations (such as your daylist, for example). What most likely has happened here is that the "current version" appears in some of your other playlists which you have downloaded, but due to the interlinking, the "previous version" also shows as downloaded. We can see how this may look confusing, so your feedback on the matter is greatly appreciated - thanks for sharing it here. Cheers 🤘
records.author: Yordan

records.text: Suggestion for recommending producers based on musical style, in a simple and quick way. Hello everyone, how are you? My idea is the following: when I access a producer’s profile, I would like to know that producer’s specific musical style and which styles they identify with. This would allow me to find new producers, tracks, and related content in an easy, fast, and intuitive way. The idea is that on the shared screen, below the producer’s name (or in another visible place), the musical style could be displayed. For example: Main Genre: Trance Other genres they identify with: Psytrance, Progressive House, House. This way, by clicking on the genres, I would be taken to other producers—well-known or not—who play the same style, thus contributing both to established producers and to those who are not yet very famous. I hope this can be implemented! Thank you all!
records.author: jonnetg

records.text: Re: Removing Podcast Recommendations After about half a year of what feels like abuse, these Spotify podcast recommendations keep appearing in my feed and there is still no way to make them go away. If I cannot curate my own taste in podcasts and music, what is the point? I don’t need Spotify to tell me what I like and don’t like. The “Not Interested” button has done nothing after months of pressing it (Spotify will keep recommending it anyway). I’m so tired of my feed that I literally will be terminating my account and creating a new one. This time I won’t make the mistake of accidentally pressing a podcast/video/music that I don’t like and see if my feed gets better. If not, I might try Apply music for the next few years before I switch to Android.
records.author: Clysm

records.text: Re: Bizarre album listed under wrong artist "It is honestly exhausting to see yet another 'fake' album being uploaded under a legendary name like Aztec Camera . A band that hasn't been active since the 90s suddenly 'releasing' Christian-themed content? It’s clearly an imposter or a metadata error, and it’s a slap in the face to fans. Spotify needs to implement a true artist/band certification service for recommendations immediately. At this point, we urgently need a profile setting that allows users to filter out unverified releases or flag blatant 'AI slop' and imposters. The platform is becoming cluttered with low-quality, fraudulent content. If Spotify doesn't find a way to protect the integrity of real artists' discographies soon, the user experience will be permanently ruined. Please fix the Aztec Camera profile and give us better tools to control what appears in our feeds."
records.author: rofrodrigues

records.text: Feature Request: A Dedicated Listen Later List for Music Discovery Currently, when we stumble upon an interesting track, album, or EP but don't have the time to listen right away, our options are limited . We either have to add it to our "Liked Songs" (which messes up our personal algorithms and recommendations) or create a temporary playlist (which clutters our meticulously organized library). I suggest implementing a simple "Listen Later" feature—perhaps accessible via the three-dot menu—that saves music to a specific, easily accessible queue or folder . Just like the "Watch Later" feature on video platforms, this would allow users to bookmark new discoveries instantly without disrupting their established music collections . This small UI/UX improvement would significantly boost user engagement, encourage users to explore new artists without hesitation, and keep the main library perfectly organized . Thank you for considering this enhancement!
records.author: kxyra

records.text: MAKE AN OPTION TO DISABLE ALL RECOMMENDATIONS I've been a subscriber for years. Paying you people who knows how much money. Now you start trying to install some podcast I'm not interested into my feed under the guise of "you might also like". I know what I like. You do not. Stop trying to influence me and stop trying to install some podcast in my listening stream. The very least you could do is make an option to disable it but you don't even offer that. Do you realize how infuriating this is? How tired people are of having things they aren't interested in shoved down their throat?! This is a joke. You even label it as the podcast I'm actually listening to so I can't unfollow, I can't mark as not interested, I can't rate it poorly. There's literally nothing I can do.

records.text: Re: Recommended Songs - Not appearing in listening history Hey @MadisonnRoxx , Thank you for reaching out to the Community and welcome. That's an odd case since the recommended songs should be added to the listening history as usual if you play them. So to make sure we're on the same page, would you mind confirming if you've already performed a clean reinstall of the app? If not we'd recommend following the steps mentioned in this article to see if it makes any difference. If the issue persists, it's possible that the behavior you mentioned could be related with this thread , which is currently under investigation by internal teams since it's affecting the way that the app "recognizes" the tracks played from outside a playlist (such as the queue or recommendations). Hope this helps. If anything else comes up, don't hesitate to ask.
records.author: OscarDC

records.text: Re: Issues with using "Search" - keyboard does not appear automatically This is how it works today. To open keyboard when hitting the search button: 1. Tap search -> moves to page for search 2. Tap search field at top of search page. Old searches and recommendations can be viewed here-> field transforms to a writeable field in grey 3. Tap the grey field to type in it -> keyboard upens up. This is ridiculously complicated. It should open the keyboard once you tap the first search button. But it could allow you to scroll through past searches and recommendations as you can. This is however a problem that is found in Google Play Store as well so I understand that this may be a Google problem as well. But in play store it is only two taps to open the keyboard.
records.author: Orschild

records.text: Re: Smart shuffle on desktop Hi @j_pope , Thanks for reaching out. The process to using Smart Shuffle on desktop is slightly different now, but still functions the same. All you have to do is open a playlist and tap the shuffle icon twice, either at the top of the playlist before starting it or in the Now Playing field at the bottom. When it turns on, you should see a notification that recommendations have been added to the queue. Does this work for you? If the option isn't there, make sure it's enabled from the settings as shown at the end of this article . If it still doesn't work and you continue to face issues with it, let us know in more detail what the exact behaviour is and we'll be glad to help you. Hope this helps.
records.author: Joan

records.text: Re: Trouble Resizing New Android Widget Hi there @TOG_2 , Thanks for reaching out. We'll be glad to help you with the Android widget. The default size of the widget is 4x2, but it can be resized to anything from 1x1 to 4x4. Be advised though that this does vary depending on the device. Make sure you're using the new widget with all the visible playlist recommendations and not the old one with just the player. The old widget is deprecated, but it's possible you may still see it and that one lacks the resizing options of the new widget. If the resizing of the new widget still doesn't work, send us some screenshots and let us know in greater detail what the behaviour is so we can look into it and assist you further. Hope this helps.
records.author: Joan

records.text: Re: Ai generated content should be labeled. AI Generated Music should definitely not be a recommendation if it's not something you casually listen to. The problem is that Spotify doesn't categorize AI music separately form human made music. That means if you block AI music, it actively messes with your algorithm that includes human made music of the same genre. So if I block an AI generated song in the genre of 60s surf rock, I'll less likely be shown 60s surf rock (which I enjoy). That is a severe, nearly critical, impact to user experience. If Spotify keeps AI music on their platform, it not only needs to be tagged as such, but categorized differently such that your listening algorithm doesn't affect human-made music. Furthermore, for ease, a setting to disable AI generated song recommendations.
records.author: ZacL

records.text: Auto-Remove or Prompt to Remove Songs You Repeatedly Skip I have huge playlists and it’s hard to clean them up manually. My idea is that Spotify should notice when you keep skipping the same song and either remove it automatically or just ask if you want to. You guys already track skips for recommendations so the data is already there. A few things to consider though — skipping in the last few seconds shouldn’t count because that’s basically a full listen. And it should take a few skips before anything happens, not just one, because sometimes you skip a song just because you’re not in the mood even though you still like it. If those edge cases are handled right this could make playlist cleanup basically automatic just from listening normally.
records.author: SawyerE

records.text: Re: i have a few problems here lol Hey there @op1umofthepeople , Thanks for reaching out on the Community! Hope you don't mind us moving your post from the Ideas section, as it fits better here 🙂 Your Spotify account will not automatically be removed even if you're not using for a bit, no need to worry. However, certain features such as recommendations, Made for You playlists, and Recently played might not be up-to-date if you're not actively using the app. While it's not possible to restore previously recently played tracks in the app, Recently played should update with time and reflect your current listening habits. Feel free to have a look at this article for more details on Recents. We hope this clears things up! If anything else appears, we're just a post away.
records.author: Maria

records.text: Recommendations in playlists do not refresh. Plan Premium Country Türkiye Device PC Operating System Windows 11 My Question or Issue I've created a new playlist today and wanted to add more songs to the playlist. I usually add 50-60 songs before using the recommended section at the bottom of the playlist so that the algorithm can understand what kind of music I want to have on the playlist. The issue is that the recommended songs section is stuck in a loop of 2-3 songs. I will be providing an image of the issue. Whenever I refresh, it is still the same songs repeated. Although I've stated that this issue occurs on PC it also occurs on my iPhone 12, so it is not strictly a device issue.

records.text: Re: Why are recommendations so terrible Recommendations are total garbage compared to when I first started using Spotify. The "your niches" only contains genres I would never listen to, even the your genres contains all but one genre that I never listen to. I don't ever care about the time the music was produced in, so all the 1990s, 2000s and similar suggestions are absolutely irrelevant for me. And lastly, just because I like one track from one artist, it doesn't mean that I like even a single other track of that artist. Why isn't it possible to actually recommend similar music to what I listen to? The recommendations are all about artists, genres and time periods, which are all not relevant when trying to find music I like.

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs At this point I seriously don't know how I am supposed to support my local music scene or support any real human being at all. I get AI artists recommended at a pace where i dont even know who is real and who isn't. I want to listen to music where an actual human being put effort into, not some AI that put something together and got uploaded. My Girlfriend and her Metal Band is sitting at 700 monthly listeners while AI bands rake in 100k listeners, thats just discouraging for anyone starting in the music scene. I want to be able to disable AI recommendations and seek them out on my own volition, not get blasted by slop appearing in my mix.

records.text: Hey folks, Thank you for your messages. We can confirm that some changes have been made in the backend by the internal teams and the number of recommendations you're getting should be back to normal. Make sure you have your Spotify app updated to its latest version. If the suggestions are still episodes instead of music, we'd recommend creating an exact duplicate of the playlist from the three dots menu > Add to other playlist > New playlist to see if it makes any difference. If anything else comes up, don't hesitate to ask. OscarDC Moderator Help others find this answer and click "Accept as Solution" . If you appreciate an answer, maybe give it a Like . Are you new to the Community? Take a moment to introduce yourself!

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs jonny99 Hello Like you I've been around quite a while. After reading your post I went back to today's recommendations. I would estimate that a good 15-20 per cent of the albums recommended today were human or real artists I'd never heard of. I've got great albums in almost every genre that I like saved for streaming. Plus I have checked the FOLLOW window on hundreds of artists that I consider favorites. I am certain this greatly affects my daily and weekly recommendations from Spotify. And I'm seeing virtually zero AI. This is working for me. It's so simple, and I believe it will work for anyone else who wants to eliminate AI music from their Spotify recommendations.

records.text: Re: [All Platforms] Option to have a true shuffle Removed Smart Shuffle recommendations. Whenever I read this –– or any of the bad press Spotify keeps attracting for justified reasons – I'm reminded not to rely on streaming services at all, but I'm also happy that there are alternatives. Come on, as if a developer, with or without the help of AI, wouldn't be able to implement a shuffle function. It's so obvious the management does not want no true shuffle functionality. Management wants customers to listen to AI and trending music, not to their own and other people's curated playlists. Yes, it's nearly 2026 and we're still here, but there are alternatives. Have a nice holiday season everyone and let's keep this thread alive and active!
records.author: fraktalisman

records.text: Re: [All devices] Recommended songs for playlists Hey folks. We've tried to replicate the issue after the latest update, and it seems that the recommendations are not suggesting duplicated songs anymore. Bear in mind that the recommendations may still include live or different versions of the same songs. If you're still having this issue, we'd recommend making sure your app is updated to its latest version and even performing a clean reinstall of the app by following the steps mentioned in this article . In case the behavior persists, include in your next response the OS version of your device and the Spotify version you're currently running to continue investigating this. We'll be on the lookout.
records.author: OscarDC

metadata.title: Re: [All devices] Recommended songs for playlists
metadata.search_snippet: Hey folks. We've tried to replicate the issue after the latest update, and it seems that the recommendations are not suggesting duplicated songs anymore. Bear in mind that the recommendations may still include live or different versions of the same songs. If you're still having this issue, we'd recommend making sure your app is updated to its latest version and even performing a clean reinstall of the app by following the steps mentioned in this article . In case the behavior persists, include in your next response the OS version of your device and the Spotify version you're currently running to continue investigating this. We'll be on the lookout.

records.text: Filter and block AI generated music Recently got duped by Spotify recommendations with AI generated music by an AI artist called Orion7. I submitted the complaint formally to Spotify's chat and am posting to the community. I support real artist and have been a Spotify subscriber for a decade but this is the first time I am truly debating cancelling my subscription. I hate that I am being fed AI generated music in my recommendations without an easy way to identify that it is AI generated or the ability to filter out AI generated music entirely. Spotify should combat this immediately and should not be suggesting AI music to folks that don't want it.
records.author: kevinbkopp

records.text: Re: Live events doesn't show recommended artists As iterated by others, the concerts page was the de facto source for most concerts by artists you listen heavily to and follow in your area. The recent update has turned it into a useless feature that lists a handful of concerts I’m actually interested in and inundates the rest of the page with a bunch of undesirable recommendations. Why not include these new features but retain the simple list of all artists you follow touring in your set radius? A colossal downgrade to arguably one of the best features in the app that set it apart from other streaming devices and live event ticketers.

records.text: Re: Duo members share one Apple iPad. Hey @Trentify , thanks for reaching out to the Community! It’s not possible to switch libraries between accounts without logging out and logging in to another account. Premium Duo works by having two members under the same plan so only the plan’s manager is billed. This means that both accounts are still separate and have their own libraries and content recommendations. However, there is a feature that may be rolled out to iPad in the future which allows you to switch accounts on the same device. More info on that here . There’s more info on how Premium Duo works here . Hope this helps!
records.author: Billy-J

metadata.title: Re: Duo members share one Apple iPad.
metadata.search_snippet: Hey @Trentify , thanks for reaching out to the Community! It’s not possible to switch libraries between accounts without logging out and logging in to another account. Premium Duo works by having two members under the same plan so only the plan’s manager is billed. This means that both accounts are still separate and have their own libraries and content recommendations. However, there is a feature that may be rolled out to iPad in the future which allows you to switch accounts on the same device. More info on that here . There’s more info on how Premium Duo works here . Hope this helps!

records.text: Re: The band Laundry You’re absolutely right. The Spotify listing appears to be mixing multiple bands named “Laundry” into one profile. The description matches the Tim Alexander project, but only the album “Motivator” is correctly listed, while “Black Tongue” is missing entirely. Since those were the only two official releases from that specific band, the remaining albums clearly belong to different artists using the same name. Hopefully Spotify can separate these catalogs properly soon, especially for fans trying to find the original work. Also, while searching music online I somehow ended up finding “laundry service london” recommendations everywhere — funny coincidence with the band name!

If the suggestions are still episodes instead of music, we'd recommend creating an exact duplicate of the playlist from the three dots menu > Add to other playlist > New playlist to see if it makes any difference. If anything else comes up, don't hesitate to ask.

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs @mimiczone If you like AI, go for it. Many of us just don't like it. As I mentioned earlier, it's quite easy to control your Spotify recommendations. Spotify was a human only club until AI entered the picture. Most of us, I think, still like that formula. But that ship has sailed never to return. I just think many of us want an option to filter out AI music just as we can explicit material. There's certainly nothing wrong with that. Otherwise, why are so many subscribers commenting in this discussion?

records.text: Suggest songs by lyrics / themes Spotify needs to ask us what variable we would like song recommendations to be based on. As simple as: would you like Similar artists? Similar beats/genre? Similar lyrics?! For example, when I have a playlist comprises solely of songs about being in the military and Spotify offers me similar bands or beats, I can't use a single one of them. It shouldn't be that hard to make an algorithm based on song lyric theme, but you have to ask us to know that that's what we want.

records.text: Content preference to disable AI generated music In the same way Spotify gives me the choice to listen to “explicit” music or not, please give me the choice to listen to “AI generated” music or not. For example, souloverai.com independently and algorithmically tags music as gen AI. For now, I have to manually filter and block 1/5 of the Spotify recommendations on my favorite genre. This has become exhausting and disheartening for someone wanting to discover and support professional, human musicians.

records.text: Re: [Your Library] Search for individual songs within all Playlists This is a major usability problem for anyone with large music libraries. Searching Your Library should return individual tracks across all playlists, not just playlists, albums, and artists. It makes no sense that Spotify can identify songs for recommendations and playlists internally, yet users cannot globally search their own saved tracks by title.

records.text: Spotify AI dj settings page 1. DJ AI Settings Overview: Users should have the ability to customize the DJ AI to play specific genres, moods, or artists. Implementation: Genres and Moods: Users can select from a predefined list of genres or moods. Artist and Song Suggestions: Users can specify favorite artists, bands, albums, or songs. Playlist Preferences: Users can create playlists and specify their preferences for the DJ AI to follow. 2. AI/Prompted Playlist Settings Overview: Users can define specific prompts or themes for AI-generated playlists. Implementation: Prompt Input: Users can input a text prompt that the AI will use to generate the playlist. Theme Selection: Users can choose from predefined themes or create custom ones. Playlist Options: Users can specify the length of the playlist or the number of songs. 3. Mixed Playlists Settings Overview: Users can customize the AI to mix playlists based on specific criteria. Implementation: Criteria Selection: Users can choose from different criteria such as song popularity, genre diversity, or tempo. Mixing Preferences: Users can specify the mixing style (e.g., smooth, fast-paced). Playlist Customization: Users can adjust the number of tracks or the time between songs. 4. AI Artists/Bands/Albums/Songs Settings Overview: Users can manage and customize AI recommendations for artists, bands, albums, and songs. Implementation: Artist and Band Suggestions: Users can input their favorite artists or bands. Album and Song Recommendations: Users can specify genres or themes for album and song suggestions. Personalized Recommendations: Users can receive personalized recommendations based on their listening history. 5. Control Over AI Playback Overview: Users should have the ability to control the playback of AI-generated content. Implementation: Pause and Play: Users can pause and play AI-generated content. Skip Tracks: Users can skip tracks if they are not enjoyable. Volume Control: Users can adjust the volume of AI-generated content. 6. Feedback and Improvement Overview: Users should have the option to provide feedback on the AI's performance. Implementation: Rating System: Users can rate the AI's performance on a scale of 1 to 5. Comments Section: Users can leave comments on their experience with the AI. Suggestions: Users can provide suggestions for improvement. By implementing these settings, users will have a comprehensive and customizable experience with the AI features in Spotify, allowing them to tailor the music recommendations to their preferences and enjoy a personalized listening experience. 👤 You Now add custom voice section, such as different english accents, such as American, Australian, British, etc, and maybe even the ability to use your own voice 🤖 Assistant Here is an updated version of the Spotify AI settings page with an added custom voice section: # Spotify AI Settings Page AI Chat Conversation 👤 You help me complete this feature request and elaborate on each point 🤖 Assistant To complete this feature request, we can design a comprehensive AI settings page for Spotify that includes detailed controls for each AI functionality. records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs There’s an article in the Guardian today 14 Nov “AI slop tops Billboard and Spotify charts as synthetic music spreads”. Not sure if I can share the link here as it's a newspaper, but it discusses how a couple of AI songs have managed to top Spotify's Viral 50 chart, and how this slop is saturating the streaming services. There’s a link to the statement as posted by @Vasil at the top of this discussion, and there’s also a link to a study from 12 Nov by Deezer, looking at whether most listeners can identify AI tracks, and the need for transparency; Deezer Survey The Deezer study was conducted with 9000 people across 8 countries, it makes interesting reading. Deezer are the only streaming company to at least tag AI at this time, and appear to be proactive in trying to find a solution how to deal and/or live with this. Spotify’s statement of introducing a spam filter this autumn, and “rolling the system out conservatively over the coming months” sounds similar to the lossless announcement from several years ago, how long did we end up waiting for that? Spotify’s statement does not say we will see which songs are AI-generated in playlists, recommendations, or search results. The only visible identification of AI use will come from new AI disclosure tags that appear inside the track’s detailed credits, these won’t be prominently displayed in the playlists, and will only show up if labels, distributors, or rights holders submit that information through the new industry-standard system. There is no suggestion Spotify will prevent AI content from appearing in users recommendations, only spam and abusive mass-generated content, not AI music in general. Spotify is the biggest and most established music-streaming service in the world. But anyone who cares about the state of the music industry, the shrinking payouts to artists, the inferior audio quality compared to competitors, the growing deluge of AI slop creeping into their recommendations, (and don’t get me started on the ICE ad’s, AI Drone warfare and podcasters payouts), will eventually look elsewhere. I already have. This discussion, as of today, only has just over 3,160 votes, which suggests the issue isn’t gaining much traction outside this community, and with more than 280 million premium subscribers, Spotify probably won’t notice the small minority who leave. But many of us have been loyal for years, tolerating the absurdly long wait for lossless streaming while other platforms have offered it for ages, and overlooking Spotify’s low artist payments because the app was always smooth, played nicely with external speakers, and the recommendations used to be genuinely great (along with AI content, my Discover Weekly has absolutely deteriorated, and has been dreadful ever since Spotify introduced the genre tags. I know I have an eclectic taste in music, but even by my own standards the stuff being recommended to me lately is bizarre). Now, though, the red flags are piling up. Spotify feels like it has lost its moral compass, and the flood of AI generated slop appearing in our playlists, with no way to report it, filter it out, or opt out, feels like the final nail in the coffin. When one of the smaller streaming services has already taken the innovative of tagging AI content and even surveying their users about these concerns, it’s hard not to conclude that Spotify has completely lost touch with its audience. We have a choice. I’ve already made mine.
records.author: AntGil

records.text: Request for Extended API Access for AI-Based Spotify Thesis Project Hello, I am currently developing an AI-powered music assistant as part of my university thesis project, focused on enhancing the way users interact with Spotify through intelligent analysis, recommendations, and conversational interaction. At this stage, my system is already functional and operates using the data currently available through Spotify’s API (primarily liked/saved tracks). Based on this data, I have implemented the following capabilities: Storage and structuring of liked tracks, including artist, album, release year, and metadata Automatic genre inference (especially for Drum & Bass subgenres such as neurofunk, jungle, liquid, techstep, etc.) Mood detection based on audio features (energy, valence, danceability, acousticness, etc.) Classification of tracks into mood categories such as: aggressive, melodic, atmospheric, energetic, uplifting, dark, etc. Sorting and organizing tracks by genre, mood, and historical period Identification of most frequent (top) artists based on listening data Basic recommendation engine based on: genre (e.g. "recommend neurofunk tracks") mood (e.g. "I want something aggressive") combined mood + genre queries Additionally, I have implemented a natural-language query system that supports prompts such as: "show all my Spotify tracks sorted by genre" "sort all tracks in my Spotify liked songs by genre" "recommend tracks" "suggest jungle tracks" "recommend me drum and bass classic" "recommend me drum and bass only" "I want something aggressive" "I want something melodic" "I'm in the mood for dark Drum" "suggest something atmospheric" "recommend emotional liquid" "I want energetic jungle" "which artist appears most in my liked songs" "what Drum and Bass genres exist" "when did jungle appear" The system also supports historical genre analysis and era detection, allowing users to explore the origins and evolution of specific styles. The next stage of my project (currently conceptual and partially designed) includes: A voice-based AI assistant (similar to Alexa), for example: "Hey Spotify, recommend me tracks for chilling with friends at home." "Hey Spotify, give me music for a late-night party." "Hey Spotify, what did I listen to most this week?" Advanced mood and atmosphere detection based on listening patterns and context Weekly and monthly listening recaps (beyond the current annual Spotify recap) Discovery of new artists based on deep preference analysis Context-aware playlist generation (e.g. social events, studying, nightlife, etc.) More advanced recommendation logic using full catalog data Potential future extension: intelligent mixing / track sequencing (for advanced usage) However, in order to fully realize these ideas, I would need broader access to Spotify data. At the moment, my system is limited to what Spotify currently allows through the API (mainly liked songs). While this is sufficient for building a prototype, it significantly restricts the ability to: recommend new tracks outside of the liked library analyze the full spectrum of available genres and artists build more advanced AI-driven discovery features implement deeper personalization and context-aware recommendations For this reason, I would like to ask: Is it possible to obtain extended access to Spotify’s data for research or academic purposes? Are there programs, partnerships, or special permissions for thesis-level or experimental AI projects? What would be the appropriate path to request broader metadata or catalog-level access? I believe this project aligns with the future direction of music platforms toward more intelligent, conversational, and personalized interaction with music. Thank you very much for your time and consideration. I would be happy to provide additional details, documentation, or a demonstration of the system if needed. Kind regards, Hristina
records.author: HrissTsvetanova

metadata.title: Request for Extended API Access for AI-Based Spotify Thesis Project
metadata.search_snippet: Hello, I am currently developing an AI-powered music assistant as part of my university thesis project, focused on enhancing the way users interact with Spotify through intelligent analysis, recommendations, and conversational interaction. At this stage, my system is already functional and operates using the data currently available through Spotify’s API (primarily liked/saved tracks). Based on this data, I have implemented the following capabilities: Storage and structuring of liked tracks, including artist, album, release year, and metadata Automatic genre inference (especially for Drum & Bass subgenres such as neurofunk, jungle, liquid, techstep, etc.) Mood detection based on audio features (energy, valence, danceability, acousticness, etc.) Classification of tracks into mood categories such as: aggressive, melodic, atmospheric, energetic, uplifting, dark, etc. Sorting and organizing tracks by genre, mood, and historical period Identification of most frequent (top) artists based on listening data Basic recommendation engine based on: genre (e.g. "recommend neurofunk tracks") mood (e.g. "I want something aggressive") combined mood + genre queries Additionally, I have implemented a natural-language query system that supports prompts such as: "show all my Spotify tracks sorted by genre" "sort all tracks in my Spotify liked songs by genre" "recommend tracks" "suggest jungle tracks" "recommend me drum and bass classic" "recommend me drum and bass only" "I want something aggressive" "I want something melodic" "I'm in the mood for dark Drum" "suggest something atmospheric" "recommend emotional liquid" "I want energetic jungle" "which artist appears most in my liked songs" "what Drum and Bass genres exist" "when did jungle appear" The system also supports historical genre analysis and era detection, allowing users to explore the origins and evolution of specific styles. The next stage of my project (currently conceptual and partially designed) includes: A voice-based AI assistant (similar to Alexa), for example: "Hey Spotify, recommend me tracks for chilling with friends at home." "Hey Spotify, give me music for a late-night party." "Hey Spotify, what did I listen to most this week?" Advanced mood and atmosphere detection based on listening patterns and context Weekly and monthly listening recaps (beyond the current annual Spotify recap) Discovery of new artists based on deep preference analysis Context-aware playlist generation (e.g. social events, studying, nightlife, etc.) More advanced recommendation logic using full catalog data Potential future extension: intelligent mixing / track sequencing (for advanced usage) However, in order to fully realize these ideas, I would need broader access to Spotify data. At the moment, my system is limited to what Spotify currently allows through the API (mainly liked songs). While this is sufficient for building a prototype, it significantly restricts the ability to: recommend new tracks outside of the liked library analyze the full spectrum of available genres and artists build more advanced AI-driven discovery features implement deeper personalization and context-aware recommendations For this reason, I would like to ask: Is it possible to obtain extended access to Spotify’s data for research or academic purposes? Are there programs, partnerships, or special permissions for thesis-level or experimental AI projects? What would be the appropriate path to request broader metadata or catalog-level access? I believe this project aligns with the future direction of music platforms toward more intelligent, conversational, and personalized interaction with music. Thank you very much for your time and consideration. I would be happy to provide additional details, documentation, or a demonstration of the system if needed. Kind regards, Hristina

records.text: Enable Hi-Res Losless / Dolby Atmos Streaming and Downloading With the launch of Spotify Lossless in September 2025, Premium subscribers finally got FLAC streaming up to 24-bit/44.1 kHz — a great first step. However, Spotify still trails the competition in two key areas: true Hi-Res audio and immersive/spatial formats. The request: Hi-Res Lossless streaming beyond the current 24-bit/44.1 kHz ceiling, ideally up to 24-bit/192 kHz where the source masters allow it. Dolby Atmos / spatial audio support for tracks that have a dedicated Atmos mix. Offline downloads in both formats , so the full quality is also available without a connection — essential for travel, areas with poor coverage, and professional use cases (DJs, audio engineers, reviewers). Why Apple Music is the benchmark: Apple Music has offered Lossless (up to 24-bit/48 kHz), Hi-Res Lossless (up to 24-bit/192 kHz) and Dolby Atmos since May 2021 — at no extra cost, including downloads. Amazon Music Unlimited and Tidal offer comparable tiers. The catalog of Atmos mixes and Hi-Res masters already exists at the labels; Spotify is currently the only major platform not delivering it to listeners. Why this matters: Many subscribers currently pay for a second service (Apple Music or Tidal) purely for audio quality, while preferring Spotify's recommendations, playlists and ecosystem. Closing this gap removes the last reason to maintain two subscriptions. Hi-Res-capable hardware (DACs, LDAC/aptX devices, wired IEMs, home audio systems) has become mainstream and affordable. The audience for this feature is no longer a niche. Spotify Connect combined with Hi-Res and Atmos would be a genuinely unique selling point: no competitor matches Spotify's device integration. Suggested implementation: Add "Hi-Res Lossless" and "Dolby Atmos" as selectable quality options in the existing audio quality settings, with clear badges on eligible tracks (similar to Apple Music's labeling). Make both available for downloads, with a visible storage/bandwidth warning given the larger file sizes. If cost is a concern, even a paid add-on tier would be preferable to no option at all — though competitors have shown it can be included in the standard subscription. Spotify Lossless proved the infrastructure for high-quality delivery exists. Extending it to Hi-Res and Atmos is the logical next step to make Spotify the complete platform for both casual listeners and audiophiles.
records.author: ChrisLupus

records.text: Discography listening experience feels undermined by current playback behavior Hello Spotify Team, I would like to share feedback regarding the current artist and album listening experience in the desktop application. For years, many users requested better discography browsing, especially the ability to view and explore releases in chronological order. Spotify's community forums contain years of feedback asking for proper oldest-to-newest sorting, and more recently improvements were introduced to make discography navigation much better. As someone who enjoys discovering artists through their complete body of work, this was exactly the kind of improvement I had been waiting for. Unfortunately, the value of these changes now feels reduced because the playback experience increasingly pushes listeners away from album-focused listening. I often want to start with an artist's earliest album and continue through their catalog, album by album, experiencing their evolution over time. The recent addition of chronological sorting finally made that possible from a navigation perspective. However, when playback behavior starts steering users toward autoplay, artist radio, recommendations, or algorithm-generated content, it works against the purpose of exploring a discography in the first place. If I open an artist page, sort releases from oldest to newest, and start listening to albums, my intention is very clear: I want to listen to that artist's albums. Not radio. Not recommendations. Not a personalized stream. If I want artist radio, I will deliberately start artist radio. What makes this especially frustrating is that Spotify has already invested effort into improving discography organization and chronological browsing. Those were positive changes that acknowledged how many listeners still care about albums as complete works. The playback experience should reinforce that goal rather than interrupt it. I would love to see Spotify continue improving album-focused listening by: Supporting seamless album-to-album discography playback. Respecting chronological listening workflows. Keeping album listening clearly separated from recommendation-driven listening. Bringing advanced discography features to mobile apps rather than simplifying desktop behavior toward a more radio-focused experience. Albums are one of the foundations of music culture. Many listeners still enjoy experiencing music the way artists released it, one record at a time. Thank you for your consideration. Plan Premium Country PL Operating System (iWindows 11) My Question or Issue
records.author: mikcio2

records.text: Re: Ai generated content should be labeled. I strongly support the idea that AI-generated content on Spotify should be clearly labeled, and I want to explain why this matters so much to me as a listener who values human-made art. I want to **exclusively** listen to music created and performed by humans, including all vocals. When AI-generated tracks or AI-assisted vocals appear in my recommendations or playlists without any clear labeling, I feel like my time is being stolen. I did not choose this, and I don’t want to be tricked into listening to something that isn’t genuinely human-made. More and more big artists and labels are incorporating AI vocals and AI elements into their releases, and I personally dislike this trend a lot. It actively decreases my satisfaction with Spotify as a platform, because I can no longer trust that what I’m hearing is actually a human performance. I’m not saying this music shouldn’t exist on the platform at all, but I should be able to opt out of it completely. What I’m asking for is simple and fair: - Artists and labels must clearly indicate if a track contains any AI-generated or AI-assisted elements (especially vocals). - Spotify should display this clearly and offer a filter or setting to hide AI-generated or AI-assisted content from my recommendations, playlists, and radio. - This should apply across the whole platform: editorial playlists, algorithmic playlists, radios, and personalized mixes. I pay for Spotify because I want to listen to art created by human beings, with human intent, emotion, and performance. That’s the experience I value and choose to support financially. If I can’t reliably avoid AI-generated content here, I will seriously consider leaving the platform and looking for a service that respects this preference and gives me full control over whether I engage with AI-generated music or not. Fun fact: I did however use AI to write this comment 😛
records.author: hiiambandit

records.text: AI music transparency labels and optional controls As a Spotify Premium subscriber of more than 10 years, I would like to suggest greater transparency around AI-generated music on the platform. To be clear, I am not asking Spotify to remove or ban AI-generated music. Listeners should be free to enjoy whatever music they like, and artists should be free to experiment with new technologies. My request is simply that users have access to information that helps them make informed choices. Proposed features: • Add a small badge or disclosure icon on tracks, albums, or artist profiles when music is substantially generated by AI. • Allow users to view additional information about how a song was created. • Add an optional setting that allows listeners to exclude AI-generated music from influencing recommendations, listening statistics, Spotify Wrapped, and taste profiles. Why this matters: Recently, I discovered a song that I genuinely enjoyed, but I realized there was no easy way to determine whether it had been primarily created by human artists or generated through AI systems. As AI-generated content becomes more common, I believe many listeners will want greater transparency. This proposal is not about restricting access to AI-generated music. It is about providing information and giving users more control over their listening experience. Spotify has already demonstrated leadership in AI-powered features such as DJ and personalized recommendations. Implementing clear disclosure standards for AI-generated music would be another opportunity for Spotify to lead the industry in transparency and consumer choice. People do not need to agree on the role AI should play in music, but they should have access to information that allows them to make their own decisions. If this is something you would like to see, please vote and comment so Spotify can better understand community interest.
records.author: Stefaniepantz

records.text: AI Chat-Based Playlist Generator (Prompt-to-Playlist Feature) I would love to see a new feature in Spotify that allows users to generate playlists or radio stations using natural language through an AI-powered chat interface. Instead of relying only on existing playlists or passive algorithmic recommendations, users could simply describe what they want to listen to, and Spotify would generate a personalized, dynamic playlist in real time. The idea is to introduce a new “AI Mode” where users type anything—from a mood to a very specific concept—and the system interprets it to build a playlist that adapts continuously based on user interaction (likes, skips, or prompt refinements). For example, a user could write “songs that could be World Cup 2026 anthems, but are not official World Cup songs,” and the system would understand elements like high energy, stadium vibe, and global appeal, while excluding existing official tracks, instantly generating a playlist that fits that concept. This would enable true intent-based music discovery, allowing users to explore highly specific or creative ideas in a way that feels active and engaging rather than passive, while also increasing time spent on the platform and differentiating Spotify’s experience. Additional features could include refining prompts (e.g., “make it more electronic” or “more Latin”), saving generated playlists, or integrating this experience with existing features like Radio or AI DJ. Overall, this would shift Spotify from simply recommending music to truly understanding what users want to feel or explore, making discovery more personalized, interactive, and creative. And if by any chance someone from the team finds this interesting, valenvota23@(gmail. com) (It gets censored, thats way is written weirdly) — I’m currently looking for an internship and always happy to chat (had to shoot my shot 😄 ).
records.author: valenvota

records.text: Allow playlist creators to insert non-playable text headers (section separators) inside playlists. Description Long-form and chronological playlists lose clarity because Spotify playlists are currently a flat list of tracks with no structure. This is especially noticeable for: Decade or year-by-year music history playlists Educational playlists (music, language, classroom use) Fitness playlists (warm-up / intervals / cooldown) DJ or long-form curated sets There is currently no way to indicate sections, years, or phases inside a playlist without disrupting playback. Proposed Feature Allow playlist creators to insert non-playable text headers (section separators) directly into playlists. Key characteristics: Headers are not tracks and do not play They do not affect shuffle, autoplay, or recommendations Visible inline within the playlist track list Optional and controlled by the playlist owner Examples: — 1994 — Warm-Up Peak Set Cooldown Side A / Side B Why This Matters This improves playlist usability, navigation, and comprehension without changing Spotify’s playback model. It benefits many user groups: Music history and archival listeners Educators and students Fitness instructors and athletes DJs and playlist curators Anyone managing long playlists This feature encourages higher-quality curation and makes long playlists easier to follow and return to. Minimal Implementation Suggestion To keep scope small: Allow insertion of a non-playable text item Character limit (e.g., 50 characters) Appears only in playlist views Ignored by playback, shuffle, and recommendations No changes needed to audio playback or discovery systems. Example Use Case A playlist titled “Rock #1s for the 1990s” could appear as: — 1991 — Track A Track B — 1992 — Track C Track D This adds clarity without affecting how the playlist plays.
records.author: jon_hughes

records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs I did it. After 10 years... I canceled Spotify premium and am fully acclimated to using Symfonium with my local files + Deezer and Bandcamp for recommendations. (FYI my 3,000 mp3 files are only 12GB, so your library might not take up as much space as you think) I made a comment here early this year voicing my grievances and sadly as expected nothing has really gotten better. Spotify has been paying con men for their laundered goods they've made from unwilling artists, happily recommended it everywhere on the platform, and then ignored users who want to know what they're listening to - the recent(ish) news is too little too late (if not a simple legal decision to cover themselves). I feel I've been lied to for too long. I've finally got everything in order and can move on. It's time for me to say goodbye forever. Streaming could have been great. Could've continued to be great - in it's own way (convenience). But you've gone too far down the path of "enshitification" and I'm not paying for it any longer. And I'm not even staying so you can make ad revenue off me. "Cry harder. You're not making a difference. No one cares" Bro, I'M sick of seeing this. I want to feel good about my music again. You can go wallow in your endless pit of artificially intelligent meaninglessness. I'm leaving. I'm done and gone. And maybe buying a Deezer subscription just to spite Spotify and support their competitor.

records.text: Please Add a “Music-Only Mode” Hi Spotify Team and Community, I’ve been a Premium subscriber for over 10 years. Spotify has been my primary music platform throughout that entire time, and I’ve recommended it to countless friends and colleagues. Recently, however, I’ve been increasingly frustrated with the growing emphasis on video content in the Home feed. I want to be clear: I am not against video as a feature. I understand that Spotify is expanding and evolving as a platform. What concerns me is the lack of user control. As a paying Premium subscriber, I would like the option to use Spotify strictly as a music platform — without video recommendations dominating the Home experience. Right now, there is no way to disable or opt out of this content globally. For users like me, who primarily use Spotify for music discovery, playlists, and daily listening, the shift toward video feels intrusive and misaligned with why we subscribed in the first place. This isn’t just a minor UI preference. It affects the overall experience of the product. If a simple “Music-Only Mode” toggle existed, this wouldn’t be an issue. Autonomy is what’s missing. I’m genuinely considering canceling my subscription over this — not because I dislike Spotify, but because the experience no longer reflects how I want to use the platform. I hope this feedback is taken seriously. Many long-time users value Spotify for its music-first identity. Please give us the choice. Thank you.
records.author: Pasteleiro2D

records.text: Optional Dynamic Range filter for recommendations I would like to propose a small, optional feature that could meaningfully improve listening quality for users who care about musical dynamics — without changing audio playback or affecting the default experience. Idea Add an optional “Dynamic Range filter” that allows users to exclude tracks with extremely low internal dynamic range from recommendations and autoplay. Important design principles: The filter would be OFF by default. It would only affect content selection (recommendations, radio, autoplay), not playback processing. No compression or DSP would be applied. The original audio would remain untouched. A simple implementation could use an existing loudness-based metric such as EBU R128 Loudness Range (LRA), calculated offline per track. Example user setting: Enable Dynamic Range filter Minimum dynamic range (LRA): slider Apply only to selected genres (e.g. classical, jazz, acoustic, etc.) Why this is valuable: It allows listeners who prefer natural dynamics to avoid overly dense and heavily limited masters. It avoids any artistic interference with the audio signal. It introduces a quality-oriented selection criterion, similar in spirit to how loudness normalization removed the incentive for excessively loud mastering. This would not be a “quality judgement” feature, but a personal listening preference tool — much like genre or mood filtering. From a system perspective, it could also provide useful feedback to content producers, as excessively low-dynamic masters would be less likely to appear in certain listener profiles. Thank you for considering this idea. Best regards, Tõnu Valk
records.author: ebepost

metadata.title: Optional Dynamic Range filter for recommendations
metadata.search_snippet: I would like to propose a small, optional feature that could meaningfully improve listening quality for users who care about musical dynamics — without changing audio playback or affecting the default experience. Idea Add an optional “Dynamic Range filter” that allows users to exclude tracks with extremely low internal dynamic range from recommendations and autoplay. Important design principles: The filter would be OFF by default. It would only affect content selection (recommendations, radio, autoplay), not playback processing. No compression or DSP would be applied. The original audio would remain untouched. A simple implementation could use an existing loudness-based metric such as EBU R128 Loudness Range (LRA), calculated offline per track. Example user setting: Enable Dynamic Range filter Minimum dynamic range (LRA): slider Apply only to selected genres (e.g. classical, jazz, acoustic, etc.) Why this is valuable: It allows listeners who prefer natural dynamics to avoid overly dense and heavily limited masters. It avoids any artistic interference with the audio signal. It introduces a quality-oriented selection criterion, similar in spirit to how loudness normalization removed the incentive for excessively loud mastering. This would not be a “quality judgement” feature, but a personal listening preference tool — much like genre or mood filtering. From a system perspective, it could also provide useful feedback to content producers, as excessively low-dynamic masters would be less likely to appear in certain listener profiles. Thank you for considering this idea. Best regards, Tõnu Valk

records.text: Option to automatically trigger "Private Session" for specific playlists Hi everyone, I would like to suggest a feature to improve how Spotify handles listening privacy and user statistics. Currently, "Private Session" is a global toggle. It would be extremely helpful to have a setting within individual playlists (such as sleep, meditation, or focus/study playlists) to automatically launch them in Private Session mode . Why this feature is needed: Clean Data & Stats: Many users track their listening habits via services like Last.fm or look forward to their "Spotify Wrapped." Sleep or ambient playlists often "pollute" these stats if we forget to manually toggle the Private Session. User Convenience: Manually switching Private Session on and off every time is tedious. Users often forget to enable it before sleep, or worse, forget to disable it the next day, losing hours of tracked "active" listening. Better Recommendations: This would allow for a clear separation between "utility listening" (background noise, white noise, concentration) and "active music discovery," keeping our personalized recommendations and "Daily Mixes" accurate. A simple toggle like "Always play this playlist in Private Session" within the playlist settings would be a game-changer for many of us. Thanks for considering this! PS : I'm french so i used Gemini tu write a message that is precise in English. That is why the message is in this format in order to be the more comprehensive possible.
records.author: rc_luigi

records.text: Full Account Reset Option (Clean Slate Mode) Idea Description: I’d like to suggest a feature that allows users to completely reset their Spotify account to a “brand new” state. Right now, recommendations, liked songs, playlists, and listening history all accumulate over time. This becomes problematic when: An account has been shared with others who have very different music tastes A user wants to completely restart their music journey The account’s data no longer reflects the user’s preferences at all In these cases, there is currently no way to fully start fresh without creating a new account. Proposed Solution: Introduce a “Clean Slate” or “Full Account Reset” option in account settings that: Deletes all listening history Removes all liked songs Deletes or clears all playlists Resets recommendations (Discover Weekly, Daily Mixes, Release Radar, etc.) Restores the account to a true “new user” state Optional Safeguards: A clear warning and confirmation step before reset Option to export or back up playlists before deletion Benefits: Gives users full control over their account and data Eliminates the need to create a new account just to start fresh Helps users who previously shared accounts Improves recommendation accuracy from a clean starting point This feature would provide a simple, user-friendly way to completely restart the Spotify experience without losing the account itself. Thanks for considering this idea!
records.author: rj_devil_killa_91

records.text: Add an option to pause briefly before Autoplay starts after an album When Autoplay is enabled, Spotify automatically starts playing recommended songs after the last track of an album. This is useful, but when listening to a full album, it can be hard to tell by ear when the album itself has ended and when Autoplay has started. This is especially noticeable when listening while working, commuting, or using a Bluetooth speaker or smart speaker. In those situations, I often listen without looking at the screen, so I may not realize that the album has ended and that Spotify has already moved on to recommended tracks. My idea is to add an optional setting that inserts a short pause before Autoplay starts after an album finishes. For example, after the final track of an album ends, Spotify could wait a few seconds before starting the first Autoplay recommendation. This would make it much easier to notice that the album has finished, without having to turn Autoplay off completely. I do not want to disable Autoplay, because it is useful for discovering new music and keeping playback going. I would just like a clearer audible separation between the album and the Autoplay recommendations. This would improve the experience for users who enjoy listening to full albums, while still keeping Autoplay available for continuous listening.
records.author: guijty15699

records.text: Re: Unwanted items in library Hey @Momma_Jo , Thanks for your reply. Currently, it's not possible to remove items from the Home page. As @Yordan mentioned previously, only certain recommendations have the " Not interested " option, which, if selected, will hide the content. We appreciate you taking the time to share your feedback with us, as it helps improve the overall user experience. Also, we recommend you giving a +Vote to this live idea , if this is something you'd like to see in the future. The Idea Exchange is a great way for us to see what our users are interested in and how many people like a certain idea. Check out this FAQ to learn more about it. On another note, you mentioned that you tried deleting 2 of your playlists, but weren't able to. Could you let us know if you tried to delete those from Your Library or from the Recently played on your Home page? If you tried to delete them from the Recently played section on the Home page, we can confirm that it's not possible, but you should be able to delete them from Your Library by following these steps. Hope this helps. If you have any questions or need a hand with anything else, the Community is here for you.
