usable_records.text: Ongoing Issues Review: April 2026 Hey folks! Welcome to this month's Ongoing Issues Review. We want to keep you in the loop with regards to some of the most trending ongoing issues reported here, in the Spotify Community. We're sharing updates from behind the scenes to let you know what's been fixed, what we’re tracking, and what we need your help with. Still experiencing an issue? You can check the full Ongoing Issue section here . FIXED 1. Interaction with AI DJ does not work 📌 We’ve received reports that for some folks, interactions with the AI DJ showed the error “Your current speaker does not support interaction with DJ”, even when no external speaker was connected. If you’re still experiencing this, please make sure your Spotify app is up to date. 2. [Android Auto] Larger playlists do not load when offline Reports came in of larger playlists not loading in Android Auto when offline. Some of you experienced a “Spotify is not responding” message on the car screen. This should now works as expected! 3. Cannot open desktop player on MacBook 📌 The Spotify desktop app was failing to launch on macOS for some users. Should you still experience this, please make sure to give these steps a try. If it persists, let us know here in the Community and we'll investigate. 4. Mandopop category not loading in Browse The Mandopop genre page in Browse appeared empty or failed to load content. We appreciate the reports, and rest assured things are all fixed here too! 5. Sorting albums by release year from oldest to newest makes the library not to work Applying this sort order caused Your Library to stop functioning or displaying properly. This has been fixed! 6. Can’t play downloaded songs on search on offline mode Downloaded tracks could not be played from Search results when the app was offline. Thanks for all the information provided whilst this was looked into, we've got no new reports here! 7. Spotify Personalized Mixes don't show up Personalized mix playlists were missing entirely from users’ libraries or Home views. These now appear correctly again. 8. popup screen every time app takes focus (choose a device to connect ) Reports came in that some folks were being repeatedly prompted to select a playback device whenever returning to the app. This is now fixed 9. Smart Shuffle feature missing on playlists The Smart Shuffle toggle had disappeared from some playlists. This is all fixed now, and as always we appreciate the reports. 10. Custom Order sorting option missing inside folders Playlists inside folders couldn’t be manually reordered. The Custom Order option is available again! 11. If a local file is FLAC, the artist name repeats with a semicolon Reports came in to let us know Artist metadata was duplicated for FLAC local files. This is now resolved. 12. Black screen when playing music videos on Google TV Streamer 4K 📌 Videos played audio only and showed a black screen for some folks. A working solution was identified and shared in the thread here . 13. Toggle for the Auto-Download feature goes off screen on iOS if the name of a podcast is too long Some UI issues were identified, causing the Auto-Download toggle to be pushed out of view. This has been corrected. 14. Queued Songs Not Being Tracked in On Repeat Playlist Reports came in to let us know songs played from the queue were not influencing the On Repeat playlist. This has been fixed. 15. Recommended songs at the bottom of my playlist won't refresh - button does not work The Refresh button for recommendations wasn't generating a new list. This is now working as expected. 16. Liked songs appear empty across devices Some folks' Liked Songs libraries showed as empty across multiple devices. This is now fixed! 17. Discover Weekly suggesting songs from family members listening history and updating twice a week Reports came in to suggest recommendations were being influenced by Family members activity and refreshed twice a week. This has now been fixed. UNDER INVESTIGATION 18. IOS Spotify app resumes playback after disconnecting bluetooth audio device On iOS, we're getting reports that Spotify unexpectedly starts or resumes playback after a Bluetooth device is disconnected. This is currently being looked into by the relevant team. 19. Spotify disconnects from bluetooth when open and changes playlists Bluetooth audio appears to drop when some of you are opening Spotify or switching playlists, forcing manual reconnection. Our teams are actively investigating this. 20. Make this a playlist feature is creating a copy of the whole Liked Songs playlist We've seen reports that using the Make this a playlist option incorrectly duplicates all Liked Songs instead of only the selected subset. This is under investigation. 21. Can't upload show art Reports confirm some folks are unable to upload or update artwork for podcast shows. This is being investigated. NOT RIGHT NOW 22. Desktop App now hiding media controls when using Expand Now Playing view button 📌 This is currently expected, however if you'd like you can add your vote to this idea . 23. Certain UI elements overlap on foldable devices 📌 We’re aware of this, and whilst there’s no ETA for a full resolution right now, rest assured it's on the team’s radar. In the meantime, we recommend keeping the app up to date. 24. Lyrics don't stay on the screen when changing songs 📌 We can confirm that this is expected. However, our Idea Exchange is always open for votes or to submit your own here . 25. "Your Episodes" can't be removed from Your Library 📌 We’ve received reports that Your Episodes can’t be removed from the Your Library section on desktop. The team has identified a temporary workaround, which requires changes on our side. If you’re experiencing this, please use the Send us a message option on this page so we can assist you directly. 27. Short silence tracks on playlist excluded from shuffle 📌 Shuffle mode will remove duplicate tracks from the queue to prevent accidental repetition, which helps keep sessions more varied and fresh. If you’d prefer to hear repeated tracks, you can switch to linear playback. 28. Command-L focuses search field but doesn't put cursor there This behavior is known and remains on our radar, we recommend keeping your app up to date. 29. Tab switching resets view state instead of preserving scroll/navigation position Right now, resetting scroll and navigation position when switching tabs is currently known and expected. We appreciate the comments. What if you spot something? We encourage you to check our Ongoing Issues board and hit +VOTE if you’re affected, it really helps! Need quick help? Post your question in the Help Boards. Got feedback? Share it in Ideas. Want to see some changes before other users you can sign-up to join our thousands of Beta testers here . Thank you for all your reports and for sharing in the Spotify Community! The Community Team 💙
usable_records.author: MihailY

usable_records.text: Support and report Here are more powerful kid-friendly and no-swearing ideas that Spotify or other music apps could add: Clean Lyrics & Safe Listening “No Swearing Ever” mode. AI that removes curse words automatically. Clean-only search results. Kid-safe lyric checker before songs play. Replace bad words with funny sound effects. Family-safe radio stations. And Crashes reports A powerful feature for Spotify could be: “Scan Before Listening” Mode Before a song plays, the app could automatically scan: Lyrics Swear words Violent content Sexual content Hate speech Loud scary sounds Disturbing themes Explicit cover art Then it could warn or block the song before you hear it. Powerful Features Green Safe Badge Shows songs that are fully family-friendly. Yellow Warning Badge Mild language or emotional themes. Red Block Badge Explicit or inappropriate songs blocked automatically. AI Lyrics Scanner Reads lyrics in seconds before playback. Live Audio Detection Detects hidden swearing even if lyrics are missing. Parent Safe Mode Parents lock unsafe music completely. “Only Positive Music” Filter Allows uplifting songs only. Holiday Safe Listening Christmas, Easter, Thanksgiving, Hanukkah, and family holiday playlists checked automatically. Community Reporting System Users help report unsafe songs or playlists. Custom Word Blocking Block specific curse words or topics. Safe Podcast Scanner Scans podcasts before listening too. Mood Safety Check Warns about extremely dark or disturbing songs. Cover Art Scanner Blurs inappropriate album covers. Trusted Creator Verification Shows artists and playlists with family-safe reputations. Kid Voice Assistant “This song may not be safe for children.” School & Church Safe Mode Automatically filters playlists for classrooms or family gatherings. AI Music Clean Version Creator Generates cleaner versions of songs when possible. Family Dashboard Parents can review listening history safely. Positive Community Rewards Rewards creators for uplifting content. Safe Concert & Live Event Filtering Filters livestream concerts with family settings. That kind of system would make streaming music much safer, more family-friendly, and more caring for listeners of all ages. And a lot of security and functions and report even videos like **bleep** shaking Automatic skipping of explicit songs. “Only Positive Lyrics” setting. Safe versions of trending songs. Block songs with harmful topics. Kid-Friendly Music Modes Cartoon music mode. Disney-style musical playlists. Bedtime lullaby worlds. Animal-themed songs and sounds. Train adventure music playlists. Space exploration soundtracks. Superhero workout music for kids. Singalong bouncing lyrics. Learning songs with animations. Happy-only playlist filter. metadata.title: Support and report
metadata.search_snippet: Here are more powerful kid-friendly and no-swearing ideas that Spotify or other music apps could add: Clean Lyrics & Safe Listening “No Swearing Ever” mode. AI that removes curse words automatically. Clean-only search results. Kid-safe lyric checker before songs play. Replace bad words with funny sound effects. Family-safe radio stations. And Crashes reports A powerful feature for Spotify could be: “Scan Before Listening” Mode Before a song plays, the app could automatically scan: Lyrics Swear words Violent content Sexual content Hate speech Loud scary sounds Disturbing themes Explicit cover art Then it could warn or block the song before you hear it. Powerful Features Green Safe Badge Shows songs that are fully family-friendly. Yellow Warning Badge Mild language or emotional themes. Red Block Badge Explicit or inappropriate songs blocked automatically. AI Lyrics Scanner Reads lyrics in seconds before playback. Live Audio Detection Detects hidden swearing even if lyrics are missing. Parent Safe Mode Parents lock unsafe music completely. “Only Positive Music” Filter Allows uplifting songs only. Holiday Safe Listening Christmas, Easter, Thanksgiving, Hanukkah, and family holiday playlists checked automatically. Community Reporting System Users help report unsafe songs or playlists. Cover Art Scanner Blurs inappropriate album covers. Trusted Creator Verification Shows artists and playlists with family-safe reputations. Positive Community Rewards Rewards creators for uplifting content. Safe Concert & Live Event Filtering Filters livestream concerts with family settings. usable_records.text: #MusicMonday Review - November 2025 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check October's Part 2 review for more music. Is life a blessing or a curse? When situations drag you down, this issue's tracks make you realize you may not be the only one there, and things can get better. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 Craig Edmonds – August Flowers Locked out Of my brain again I feel knocked out Every now and then Like there's a chain lock Buried deep within And I can't find the combination to get me in We begin in London, England, for a singer & songwriter Indie Pop track about feeling distant with who you are, and letting things go: "It started as a poem really, the chorus was a standalone thing I wrote before the music (something I don't usually do) and then everything followed from that. To me, the words were about depression creeping in. When its something you've lived with for a long time, you understand that it isn't going anywhere, but how you recognize it, and how you cope with it are the parameters that can change. So I wrote the verses to match that sentiment. How it feels to me to describe depression to someone in the first verse, and reassurance/hope in the second verse. I knew the song needed a lift somewhere, so in the middle 8 its like an affirmation of feeling grateful to be alive and to be breathing and present." spotify:track:6nRE6hksBAbsBHsSJAETqf:small Richie Flo – REGGAE (DO WHAT WE WANT) Don’t rush! Girl we ago tek wi time My aim is to entertain yuh, for the night, Wooii Cause inna reggae, I a pro and mi nuh no novice So when yu see mi di vibe mi bring and it nuh diminish So mek we reggae guaranteed ﬁ mek yuh astonish No hablo ingles, yeah, we do it even in Spanish Seh don’t be silly, reggae music nah go vanish Di worl a ﬁ wi, reggae music dun govern it Seh inna reggae bands man a play di snares a hit Wi nah guh lef it, Roots rock and rub a dub a it We fly now to St. Thomas, Jamaica for a Reggae track about dancing all night long, to that drum and bass sound, with the girl of your dreams right next to you. This groove is like magic: "I received the instrumental from House of Riddim. When I heard the beat I immediately thought of doing a authentic reggae song. The song is a love song to reggae music. It is difficult to say exactly what inspired the song. I wanted to create a song that reggae music lovers would share a similar appreciation for the genre." spotify:track:3y7Ihy5RwgguUA92HWHYc2:small The Corvids – Ghost (In Your Arms) You've heard enough of my voice choke me while I try to speak the city's in flames but the fire won't speak for me I'm a minute from home but I feel so far away and I don't care if I don't but you made me feel so safe in your arms that day We go back to Pontefract, England for an Alternative Rock track about longing for a moment of your past you won't let go, without saying anything out loud: "When we wrote Ghost (In Your Arms) , the idea was to create an energetic crowd-pleaser. We stripped things back technically to keep it simple but gave it a strong, driving pulse that we knew would translate into something fun for our live sets. It’s also a way for us to break up our discography a little—injecting a track that’s built less on complexity and more on immediacy. From the very start, we wanted people to be able to recognize it within the first few seconds, something that hooks them right away and sets the tone for the rest of the song." spotify:track:6dqIVjjVcMyI9CKSyx8pcg:small The PainKillers – Do You Know can you see me see my tears are we really seeing clear can you feel it feel my fear I'm falling down now Hold me near We now travel west to Manchester for a L o-Fi, Alt Rock infused track about those strong feelings that make you question everything about yourself. Do you know? "It was from an experience I had coming off some awful medication I had to take for my chronic pain condition it really effected me and made me feel super low so I got quite down and depressed and it’s about realizing your changing and doing something about it as I know lots of people experience that too one way or another hope that makes sense ha!" spotify:track:2DB6qXte00q8f2XqGOy0Ke:small Shady Quid – Dogtail I’ve been frantically running around Running round in circles wearing myself down I’ve dug myself into another hole in the ground I’m deep down below and there’s no one around I shout up for help but they can’t hear a sound I'm stuck hear forever in this pit I am bound I deserve to be here but I want to be found I want to be found We end up in Newcastle upon Tyne, England, for a Country influenced, Americana track about life being unfair, where you never seem to win. Are we paying for our sins? "I would say it’s about the poor young man’s experience with moving from adolescence to adulthood. The metaphor of the pit or hole he finds himself in after wearing himself down with worry is carried through both verses. Ultimately, at the end of the song, it’s about realizing that you have to grow up and stop feeling sorry for yourself, and therefore becoming an adult." spotify:track:3J7NT7TeCUPnwMwTO1uZPG:small Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P See you next time!
usable_records.author: osornios

usable_records.text: In Our Headphones: January 2026 Howdy folks, Welcome to 2026! We're kicking off the year by bringing back one of our favorite musical traditions, 'In Our Headphones'. There's no better way to beat the winter chill we're having here, than by sharing the tracks that have been keeping us moving, thinking, and vibing over the last few weeks. And as the famous saying goes: "New Year, New Beats!". So welcome one and all, to "In Our Headphones" January 2026 ! Our team of Community Moderators have been busy hitting "play" all month long, and we're thrilled to kick-off the first edition in the year of the fiery horse. We’ve gathered a diverse collection of tracks directly from the personal playlists of team members Vasil, Joan, Ivelina, Dessi, Elena, and Kiril. Whether they were discovering new indie gems, or revisiting timeless classics, these are the songs that defined their January. Starting off with @Vasil 's who wanted to share with us a pleasant new favorite of his from a young Bulgarian artist named Mona with the tune called Sila (meaning strength/power in Bulgarian). spotify:track:4MT6GrseYj0lRmXh6CS8jU:small Next up, another song that re-emerged in his most listened tracks was an old favorite of his which he used to listen to a lot while he was working abroad in the US! It's always a nice feeling when you rediscover a song that's been buried down in your playlists for a while. spotify:track:3l9CW99AHtExIRV4hW2N5m:small To top things off, we have the newest addition to his Liked Songs, expanding his collection of "phonk" masterpieces! spotify:track:66iH61IAYwCNnQG6qJ1gIy Moving on, we have the submissions of @Joan . Continuing a trend he's been on for a while now, January has been very space/sci-fi themed! He claims to remain clueless what it is about this time of year that brings these vibes, yet he states that it keeps happening to him. Here's what he shared in his own words: "This time round, I finally got to explore the discography of the young Ukrainian band with the amazing name Labyrinthus Stellarum (sorry to anyone if a portal to another dimension opened in your room if you read that out loud). Their synth-heavy black metal songs brimming with awesome themes of space, sci-fi and aliens are really atmospheric and immersive, yet also have some beautiful melodies and are really catchy and fun too! Each album is a banger in its own way. They remind me of my favourites from Mechina and that's a big compliment coming from me!" spotify:track:1GOUPRx5hdTLoId4MwhU0Q spotify:track:56m3H4IrnOC3TYdVoELSqA spotify:track:673mWKIRiWIiAXixEmtRgz Speaking of artists that sound like other artists that Joan loves, he recently found this guy Master Boot Record, who very much reminded him of his chum The Algorithm . Joan added: "He has his own style though, very chiptune, fun and epic metal-infused electronic bangers. Didn't think I'd ever find another worthy computer metal artist, but I'm sure glad that I did." spotify:track:0gWWHHxdPOGrda1IXGpDoY spotify:track:0G79BRxeEzF0OcjCriLEV5 spotify:track:2Z8Cy3SGbCXEMrAdIsdIX5 Now comes the time to hear the special submissions of @Ivelina ! Why special you might ask? Keep on reading and you'll find out... Ivelina has been going to Pilates classes for a little over a year now, and she's been enjoying some very relaxing and very awesome sounding reggae remixes of famous songs. Here we have the two in particular that she loves the most and she also adds that: "They simply make the workouts so much more enjoyable!" https://open.spotify.com/track/622x3ps1iSIjTZPcEMCwnX?si=5f3ed816d16f4a54 https://open.spotify.com/track/3UESPBdyP6tRay7KoZOIGp?si=d7146e2c80dc4ac6 And now, the cozy submissions of @Dessi ! Here's what she said about her picks: " I don't know if it's because of the cold weather and spending a lot of time cosying at home, but Aretha always warms my soul so I've been enjoying her music a lot lately!" spotify:track:3NfxSdJnVdon1axzloJgba:small Dessi also eagerly waits for her Discover Weekly to come out, as she's been getting some pretty good recommendations in the past couple of months. Here are some of her latest finds: spotify:track:2qcht4Pl9MOi1EIjmpsz1B:small spotify:track:29pp9gao3MsPN3dp5mrrgf:small spotify:track:77sfcW5VjDAbZwmdIqCKKb:small Moving from space, to pilates, to cozy songs we now have @Elena with a brand new theme. Elena has been on an "organizing and cleaning spree" in her house throughout the months of December and January. Her motivation has been powered exclusively by Florence and the Machine. She admits that: "The whole album has been on constant repeat and max volume at all times. Besides the 2 singles Kraken has been one of my absolute faves - so strong and unexpected!" https://open.spotify.com/track/4BswiLDcr4ChDGl2NrfhC1?si=ffb2a8d526584511 Thank you so much for sharing everyone. Finally, we arrive at my submissions (yep, @Kiril 's)! Maybe I've been sleeping under a rock, or maybe taste for good music comes at a later stage (age) in life, but this January I've finally appreciated the great music of the Grateful Dead. I can happily say that I am a newly recruited "deadhead" now! Enjoy two of their songs that always go together back to back on their live concerts! https://open.spotify.com/track/3euDGpS2R0NC2Xssqxohva?si=10563104d8dc4f03 https://open.spotify.com/track/4DpBfWl3q8e0gGB76lAaox?si=4877894fd6294149 So we close the door on another month of our Moderators favourite songs, and that's it for January! We hope these tracks brought some fresh energy to your speakers! We’d love to hear what you think of our picks, and more importantly, what have you been listening to lately? Head over to the comment section to share your own January jams. The full playlist is below, happy listening! https://open.spotify.com/playlist/6lv6DjjryalvuDqg0oirRb?si=cKSvcxjSRo2k4C3SaCjstA
usable_records.author: Kiril

Didn't think I'd ever find another worthy computer metal artist, but I'm sure glad that I did." spotify:track:0gWWHHxdPOGrda1IXGpDoY spotify:track:0G79BRxeEzF0OcjCriLEV5 spotify:track:2Z8Cy3SGbCXEMrAdIsdIX5 Now comes the time to hear the special submissions of @Ivelina ! Why special you might ask? Keep on reading and you'll find out... Ivelina has been going to Pilates classes for a little over a year now, and she's been enjoying some very relaxing and very awesome sounding reggae remixes of famous songs. Here we have the two in particular that she loves the most and she also adds that: "They simply make the workouts so much more enjoyable!" https://open.spotify.com/track/622x3ps1iSIjTZPcEMCwnX?si=5f3ed816d16f4a54 https://open.spotify.com/track/3UESPBdyP6tRay7KoZOIGp?si=d7146e2c80dc4ac6 And now, the cozy submissions of @Dessi ! Here's what she said about her picks: " I don't know if it's because of the cold weather and spending a lot of time cosying at home, but Aretha always warms my soul so I've been enjoying her music a lot lately!" spotify:track:3NfxSdJnVdon1axzloJgba:small Dessi also eagerly waits for her Discover Weekly to come out, as she's been getting some pretty good recommendations in the past couple of months. Here are some of her latest finds: spotify:track:2qcht4Pl9MOi1EIjmpsz1B:small spotify:track:29pp9gao3MsPN3dp5mrrgf:small spotify:track:77sfcW5VjDAbZwmdIqCKKb:small Moving from space, to pilates, to cozy songs we now have @Elena with a brand new theme. Elena has been on an "organizing and cleaning spree" in her house throughout the months of December and January. Her motivation has been powered exclusively by Florence and the Machine. She admits that: "The whole album has been on constant repeat and max volume at all times. Besides the 2 singles Kraken has been one of my absolute faves - so strong and unexpected!" https://open.spotify.com/track/4BswiLDcr4ChDGl2NrfhC1?si=ffb2a8d526584511 Thank you so much for sharing everyone. Finally, we arrive at my submissions (yep, @Kiril 's)! Maybe I've been sleeping under a rock, or maybe taste for good music comes at a later stage (age) in life, but this January I've finally appreciated the great music of the Grateful Dead. I can happily say that I am a newly recruited "deadhead" now! Enjoy two of their songs that always go together back to back on their live concerts! https://open.spotify.com/track/3euDGpS2R0NC2Xssqxohva?si=10563104d8dc4f03 https://open.spotify.com/track/4DpBfWl3q8e0gGB76lAaox?si=4877894fd6294149 So we close the door on another month of our Moderators favourite songs, and that's it for January! We hope these tracks brought some fresh energy to your speakers! We’d love to hear what you think of our picks, and more importantly, what have you been listening to lately? Head over to the comment section to share your own January jams. The full playlist is below, happy listening! https://open.spotify.com/playlist/6lv6DjjryalvuDqg0oirRb?si=cKSvcxjSRo2k4C3SaCjstA

usable_records.text: I miss when Spotify felt human I've used Spotify for a long time. I remember my dad telling me about this cool thing coming to the US way back in 2011 when I was a middle schooler. We shared an account between myself, my dad, and my brother. I dug around on Spotify looking for my favorite songs from the CDs that were burned just for my mom's minivan (now scratched or lost). Spacehog, Queens of the Stoneage, Snow Patrol, Death Cab, The Killers, Coldplay, Spoon, U2. I stopped using my iPod Nano because I loved Spotify so much. We would open Spotify at the family computer and play music while an oven pizza heated up. I vaguely remember the first lyrics feature coming out. I think they were partnered with Musixmatch, which is where I already went to find all the lyrics to songs I didn't get as a young child, but suddenly got as a teenager. It wasn't like the realtime lyrics they have now. Users would type in the lyrics to the songs they were listening to, which meant they were wrong half the time, but we were all getting them wrong together (reminiscent of 'Hold me closer, Tony Danza'). What I do remember all too vividly is Spotify phasing out the Starred playlist around 2014 or 2015, I think. One day, I opened the desktop app and the star button was GONE, my starred playlist was CAST ASIDE and replaced by 'Your Music' or something like that. The star was replaced with a heart, which, in hindsight, just looked like a play to align Spotify design with Instagram. But the heart was okay. It made sense. I spent years building playlists, deleting playlists, growing my music library, finding artists I hadn't heard of before. I got through pubescent depression with Spotify. I had my first kiss with music playing on Spotify. I drove my first car with Spotify, graduated high school with Spotify, moved 1,200 miles from my home with Spotify. I had a 17 hour playlist I'd been adding songs to since 2018 for one of my friends since we were living in different countries. Even now in 2026, I'm sitting on my couch in a boxed up apartment as I prepare to move in 60 hours. I've left my speaker unpacked so I can still enjoy music from my Spotify playlists as I get ready for a fresh start. I remember when I could finally start paying for my own premium subscription; I was earning less than $40K a year and made it a point to spare budget on the monthly so I could get my ad-less experience. I ate beans and rice for weeks , but wouldn't give up that subscription. But I feel like we're the only human thing left about Spotify, the users. When the heart button was replaced with a plus sign a few years ago, I think that was when I really noticed the change. It was small, but it felt significant. To take a representation of love and replace it with what was a effectively an add-to-cart button felt ugly. No hate to the designers. All the hate to the branding decision-makers. I'd bet a decent amount that a c-level or higher is the reason that change was even suggested in the first place. I don't want to come off as completely ungrateful. I found some of my favorite artists because of Spotify recommendations in my playlists or from Discover Weekly pre-2020. But the recent infiltration of genAI music and artists, genAI song/brand description features, AI DJ, and whatever other f*ckass features I've left out have made me feel gross. I'm not trying to con myself into thinking that early investors in Spotify were looking out for my listening experience, or that they cared about any of us. I'm just saying that now it feels like everything they do is to fill their already-full pockets. Remove the human element to increase profitability. At least have the decency to lie to me without contradicting the first half of your own mission statement: Our mission is to unlock the potential of human creativity—by giving a million creative artists the opportunity to live off their art. Isn't removing humanity from music dumb as sh*t? I just feel like music is such an inherent part of the human experience. Rhythm & melody are in us. You can hear it in our speech and see it in our strides. The thing is that there aren't other great streaming platforms. The smaller ones that have gained traction -- I don't trust them to not sell out if they gain any more traction. You won't catch me paying for a YouTube Music, Apple Music, or Amazon Music subscription; they're entirely capable of making sh*tty UI decisions without my extra data. But it's okay I think. I'm trying to recreate the CDs we used to keep in the minivan, and there are 2 music shops within a 7 minute walk from me. A neighbor gave me a CD/cassette deck for my new apartment. I fixed my busted record player with the help of a random guy I met at a Goodwill. I'm learning about more venues where I can find smaller, local bands. I'm going to try to ween myself off of Spotify. I hope it works out.

usable_records.text: “Emotional Safety” or “Relapse-Avoidance” listening feature. Suggestion for Spotify: Emotional Safety & Relapse-Avoidance Feature Spotify could introduce a feature that allows users to automatically isolate and manage songs associated with emotional relapse, unhealthy memories, grief spirals, toxic relationships, addiction triggers, or depressive episodes. The goal is not to censor music, but to help users create healthier listening environments while still preserving personal choice and emotional awareness. Core Feature Concept Instead of only tagging songs manually, Spotify could automatically compile emotionally triggering songs into a separate private playlist or category based on: - Repeated skips - Emotional tagging - Listening behavior patterns - Late-night replay cycles - Sudden emotional listening spikes - User feedback and avoidance behavior These songs would remain accessible, but Spotify would avoid inserting them into: - Daily mixes - Autoplay - Recommended tracks - Mood playlists - Casual shuffle sessions - Regular sound trips This creates emotional boundaries without fully removing the music from the user’s library. Proposed Features 1. Automatic “Relapse Playlist” Compilation Spotify can create a private playlist containing songs users emotionally associate with: - Painful memories - Toxic relationships - Depression - Anxiety - Addiction relapse - Emotional spirals This playlist acts more like emotional quarantine than deletion. The songs stay available if the user intentionally wants to revisit them, but Spotify avoids casually resurfacing them during normal listening. 2. Emotional Separation System The system could: - Detect emotionally repetitive listening loops - Identify songs linked to emotional distress patterns - Separate these tracks from healthy daily recommendations - Prevent accidental emotional triggering during normal listening sessions 3. “Do Not Reintroduce” Option Users can choose: - Hide from autoplay - Hide from algorithmic recommendations - Hide from mood mixes - Hide during vulnerable hours (late nights, etc.) - Keep only inside the isolated playlist This gives users control without permanently deleting meaningful songs. 4. Emotional Awareness Prompts Optional reminders such as: «“This track is currently placed in your Emotional Boundary playlist.”» Or «“You previously chose to avoid this song during regular listening sessions.”» The prompts should remain subtle and respectful. 5. Recovery-Oriented Redirection Instead of replaying emotionally harmful loops, Spotify could gently redirect users toward: - Calmer music - Recovery playlists - Grounding sounds - Confidence-building tracks - New music discovery - Emotionally stabilizing recommendations Why This Matters Music strongly affects memory, emotional conditioning, and mental state. Many people unintentionally replay songs that reinforce: - Emotional dependency - Rumination - Depression - Heartbreak cycles - Substance cravings - Isolation A system that helps separate emotionally harmful patterns from daily listening could improve digital wellbeing while still respecting artistic freedom. Important Considerations - All emotional data should remain private and encrypted. - Users should always maintain full control over their music. - Songs should never be globally labeled as “negative.” - Emotional responses are personal and contextual. - The feature should support self-awareness, not censorship. Possible Feature Names - Emotional Boundaries - Recovery Mode - Mindful Listening - Safe Listening - Reset Playlist - Emotional Filter - Reflection Mode Closing Thought Sometimes healing is not about deleting memories, but preventing them from constantly reappearing when you’re trying to move forward. Music platforms already shape emotions through algorithms — this feature would simply give users more control over that emotional influence. Additional Feature Clarification: “Blocked Playlist” System The feature could work as a dedicated private “Blocked Playlist” where users can place songs they no longer want appearing in their regular listening experience. Instead of deleting or permanently hiding the music, Spotify would: - Compile these songs into a separate reviewable playlist - Prevent them from appearing in autoplay - Exclude them from Smart Shuffle - Remove them from recommendations and mood mixes - Avoid resurfacing them during casual listening sessions Users would still retain full access to the songs and could revisit the playlist anytime intentionally. This creates a healthier balance between: - preserving meaningful music memories - and preventing unwanted emotional triggering during everyday listening Key Difference From Existing Features Unlike simply hiding a song, this system would function more like: - an emotional boundary list - a controlled archive - or a personal “do not auto-surface” collection The important part is that Spotify’s algorithm would actively understand: «“Do not casually reintroduce these songs into my listening experience.”» Suggested User Controls Users could: - Add or remove songs anytime - Review blocked songs privately - Restore songs back into normal recommendations - Set temporary or permanent blocking - Block entire artists, albums, or playlists if desired Why It Matters Many users do not necessarily want to erase certain songs permanently. Some songs carry emotional significance, but users may not want them unexpectedly appearing during vulnerable moments, commutes, late nights, workouts, or random shuffle sessions. This feature would give users healthier emotional control over algorithmic resurfacing while still respecting personal choice and music ownership. Thank you for your time and consideration. I appreciate the work Spotify continues to do in creating deeply personal listening experiences for millions of users worldwide.
usable_records.author: emilvaldivia

usable_records.text: Music-taste test to enhance personalisation Spotify should offer a dedicated “ music taste test ” to improve recommendation accuracy , since the current algorithm often feels inconsistent. The feature could live in its own tab and begin by showing users a stream of completely random songs . Instead of a simple like/dislike system, users would rate each song across five categories : “Horrible,” “Bad,” “Neutral,” “Good,” and “Great.” The “ Great ” rating should carry especially high weight , because that is what users actually want to hear more of. The other ratings would help the algorithm learn the boundaries between levels of preference : what makes a song “Good” rather than “Great,” what separates “Neutral” from “Bad,” and which elements consistently trigger strong rejection or strong approval . To make this genuinely effective, Spotify should not rely mainly on broad genres or categories . It should analyze finer details such as instrumentation, dynamics, rhythmic patterns, harmonic structure, complexity, tempo, and mood . The goal is for the system to understand taste at a deeper level, not just match surface-level labels . What this does? The algorithm takes into consideration the songs actual character, not their popularity, genre, trendiness etc. In short: What actually matters. The test should also never stop completely . From time to time, Spotify should introduce new and slightly unfamiliar songs to broaden the user profile and prevent the algorithm from becoming too narrow. It could measure how long users listen, whether they skip quickly, and how they rate these more experimental recommendations. That would help the system learn not only what users already like, but also what they are open to discovering . The format could be inspired by Instagram Reels : an endless, swipe-based flow of suggestions that becomes more personalized over time. The difference is that, instead of passive scrolling, it would be an active taste evaluation tool . In other words: continuous suggestions, more precise feedback, and deeper pattern recognition, combined with occasional novelty to keep the model from stagnating . Why? Because then, Spotify can suggest Playlists by other users that fit the personalised taste profile best and in general suggest new songs more accurately. This would have huge effects, because accurate recommendations are THE key to a more satisfying experience. A big flaw right now , is the exaggerated repetition . People want NEW songs. It makes no sense to recommend what they know already, unless the last time they listened to them is years ago. Spotify should furthermore distinguish between active and passive listening, because background playback currently distorts what the algorithm interprets as actual preference. That’s why my suggestion actually matters: the active rating design ensures representation of the users actual preferences . The Core of my suggestions in summary: Spotify should learn what users truly love — and why they love it. 1.Spotify should introduce a dedicated music taste test that collects explicit, high-quality feedback instead of relying mostly on passive listening data. 2.Users could rate random songs on a detailed scale from “Horrible” to “Great,” giving the algorithm a much clearer picture of real preference. 3.That matters because background listening can distort the data: a song played while studying, talking, or falling asleep is not the same as a song actively chosen and enjoyed. 4.Spotify should therefore distinguish between passive playback and genuine engagement, and weight the latter much more heavily. 5. At the same time, the system should go beyond broad genres and surface labels. It should analyze the actual musical structure of songs — instrumentation, dynamics, rhythm, harmony, tempo, complexity, and mood — so it understands taste at a deeper level rather than just matching categories or popularity. 6.The test should remain ongoing, with occasional unfamiliar songs to keep broadening the profile and reduce repetition. Spoiler (Highlight to read) In the end, the goal is simple: Spotify should stop guessing from noisy signals and instead learn true user preference with precision, depth, and variety. In the end, the goal is simple: Spotify should stop guessing from noisy signals and instead learn true user preference with precision, depth, and variety.
usable_records.author: birkfy07

usable_records.text: Fast Account Switching in the Spotify App 1. Context Many households currently use a single Spotify Premium account across multiple devices. While this allows centralized control through Spotify Connect, it also creates limitations: only one stream can play at a time, and all users share the same recommendations, playlists, and history. To improve the experience, I plan to move to a Family Plan with separate accounts: fatherAccount motherAccount child1Account child2Account carAccount homeAccount (admin) Each account would be assigned to a specific device (phones, tablets, Google Home speakers, Android Auto, etc.). However, managing these accounts becomes cumbersome because switching between them requires logging out and logging back in manually. 2. Problem Spotify currently does not support quick account switching. This creates friction in several everyday scenarios: A. At home Google Home speakers use homeAccount (admin) . If music is playing in a room, other family members cannot pause, change volume, or modify playback from their own devices unless they log out and log in with the homeAccount. B. In the car Android Auto restricts interaction for safety reasons. With a dedicated carAccount , passengers could safely search and control playback from their own phones — but only if switching accounts were fast and seamless. C. Multi‑speaker households Modern homes often include multiple smart speakers and even speaker groups . With separate accounts, each room could play different music without interference. For example: In a bedroom: A Google Home speaker using homeAccount (admin) can start playback via voice (“Ok Google, play Spotify”) without affecting other rooms. In the living room: Using the Spotify app on a phone linked to fatherAccount , a user could send a specific song to a particular speaker or speaker group without logging out or interrupting playback elsewhere. Today, this flexibility is limited by the need to constantly switch accounts manually. 3. Requested Feature: Fast Account Switch I propose that Spotify introduce: a) Multi‑account registration within the app Allow users to store several Spotify accounts on the same device. b) A fast, one‑tap account switcher Similar to Instagram, YouTube, or Twitter, enabling instant switching without re‑authentication. c) Device control per active account Once an account is selected, the user can control all devices linked to that account via Spotify Connect. 4. Benefits A. For individual users Seamless control of shared devices without logging in and out. Personalized recommendations preserved for each account. Safer and more convenient music control in the car. Full flexibility to manage smart speakers and speaker groups. Significant time savings in daily use. B. For families and shared households Smooth management of multiple speakers and rooms. Adults can supervise or stop playback without accessing children’s accounts. Easier adoption of separate profiles for each family member. True simultaneous playback across different rooms and devices. C. For Spotify (strategic advantages) A strong incentive for users to migrate to the Family Plan. Many households currently share a single account because switching is inconvenient. A fast account switcher removes this barrier and encourages users to upgrade. Reduced support issues related to account conflicts and device takeover. Increased usage of Spotify Connect across smart homes and vehicles. Competitive advantage over platforms that already support multi‑account switching. Better integration with the growing smart‑home ecosystem. 5. Conclusion A Fast Account Switch feature would significantly enhance the user experience, especially in multi‑device and multi‑speaker households. It would streamline control of shared devices, improve safety and convenience in vehicles, and unlock the full potential of smart‑home audio setups. Most importantly, it would serve as a powerful incentive for users to upgrade to the Spotify Family Plan , benefiting both customers and Spotify.
usable_records.author: rmompo

metadata.title: Fast Account Switching in the Spotify App
metadata.search_snippet: 1. Context Many households currently use a single Spotify Premium account across multiple devices. While this allows centralized control through Spotify Connect, it also creates limitations: only one stream can play at a time, and all users share the same recommendations, playlists, and history. To improve the experience, I plan to move to a Family Plan with separate accounts: fatherAccount motherAccount child1Account child2Account carAccount homeAccount (admin) Each account would be assigned to a specific device (phones, tablets, Google Home speakers, Android Auto, etc.). However, managing these accounts becomes cumbersome because switching between them requires logging out and logging back in manually. 2. Problem Spotify currently does not support quick account switching. This creates friction in several everyday scenarios: A. At home Google Home speakers use homeAccount (admin) . If music is playing in a room, other family members cannot pause, change volume, or modify playback from their own devices unless they log out and log in with the homeAccount. B. In the car Android Auto restricts interaction for safety reasons. With a dedicated carAccount , passengers could safely search and control playback from their own phones — but only if switching accounts were fast and seamless. C. Multi‑speaker households Modern homes often include multiple smart speakers and even speaker groups . With separate accounts, each room could play different music without interference. For example: In a bedroom: A Google Home speaker using homeAccount (admin) can start playback via voice (“Ok Google, play Spotify”) without affecting other rooms. In the living room: Using the Spotify app on a phone linked to fatherAccount , a user could send a specific song to a particular speaker or speaker group without logging out or interrupting playback elsewhere. Today, this flexibility is limited by the need to constantly switch accounts manually. 3. Requested Feature: Fast Account Switch I propose that Spotify introduce: a) Multi‑account registration within the app Allow users to store several Spotify accounts on the same device. b) A fast, one‑tap account switcher Similar to Instagram, YouTube, or Twitter, enabling instant switching without re‑authentication. c) Device control per active account Once an account is selected, the user can control all devices linked to that account via Spotify Connect. 4. Benefits A. For individual users Seamless control of shared devices without logging in and out. Personalized recommendations preserved for each account. Safer and more convenient music control in the car. Full flexibility to manage smart speakers and speaker groups. Significant time savings in daily use. B. For families and shared households Smooth management of multiple speakers and rooms. Adults can supervise or stop playback without accessing children’s accounts. Easier adoption of separate profiles for each family member. True simultaneous playback across different rooms and devices. C. For Spotify (strategic advantages) A strong incentive for users to migrate to the Family Plan. Many households currently share a single account because switching is inconvenient. A fast account switcher removes this barrier and encourages users to upgrade. Reduced support issues related to account conflicts and device takeover. Increased usage of Spotify Connect across smart homes and vehicles. Competitive advantage over platforms that already support multi‑account switching. Better integration with the growing smart‑home ecosystem. 5. Conclusion A Fast Account Switch feature would significantly enhance the user experience, especially in multi‑device and multi‑speaker households. It would streamline control of shared devices, improve safety and convenience in vehicles, and unlock the full potential of smart‑home audio setups. Most importantly, it would serve as a powerful incentive for users to upgrade to the Spotify Family Plan , benefiting both customers and Spotify.

usable_records.text: Re: Ai generated content should be labeled. https://community.spotify.com/t5/Live-Ideas/Mark-Disable-AI-Generated-Songs/idi-p/6641329/page/5#comments Like the Main Case. There shouldnt be an Issue to Label those songs. And if you have an Label of those Songs, then there should be an Option to turn it off fully. Checked the Main Request .. at start of 2025 we had maybe 5-6 Sites of Users who complains ( again .. already way to much), now at Start of 2025 we are on Site 115? 116? Not Everyone wants AI Music. For me it ruins Core Spotify Features like the "made yor you" Playlists. "Your Weekly Songs" - the 30 Songs 1x per Week, was my main source of new Music, as the Algorythm was so good. I also used Release Radar. Now they changed the "Your Weekly Songs" to 5x Genres * 30 songs, and the "normal" Weekly. We now have 180 Songs in Weekly, like written in the other Thread .. at least 30 are AI Songs. instead of Joy hearing new Music, you get old Music with 1000 Plays and doesnt match any of your "Music" and many AI Songs which Topics are Religion, Political Stuff of the US ( Kirks Death, Orange Man and so on, while im not intrested in that, nor am i American). Shared Songs, liked Songs, Songs used in Playlists wont show up in Weekly Recommendations, but AI Songs spawn .. and that increases when you do block other AI Artist. I dont complain about the Funny Songs where Michael Jackson is singing Barbie Girl. But when AI is using Artists Names same as an Original Artist, or very close to it, using Pictures to make everyone believe " yeah its just like the Original" then i have an Problem with it .. Deeps an Artist with around 1 Million monthly Listeners, i have like 4-5 liked songs. Had "Deeps" Songs in my Weekly, but it wasnt the Original, it was an AI Clone. Same with "linkon Mark" - Name looks similar to an very known Band. The Pictures used look like an AI drawing of that famous Band and Front Man who isnt alive anymore. ( dont even listen to that music). Others with the same Artists name show up in Weekly, while the Original Artist has new Songs and isnt showing up anywhere, which he should .. when i literally listen to that Artist. Its not Joy to listen Spotify Playlists, its more like Work.. You cant enjoy to just start the Playlist and listen, when you now the next Songs are AI, sound the same, and you cant do anything about it. Like written on OG Post, if you can Label it ( Deezer has no issue to label that) .. then there should also be an Option for EVERYONE to turn that stuff off.

usable_records.text: Re: How do I stop spotify adding songs to my playlist Hi there, @Journeyjackson , This behavior might be stemming from Smart Shuffle - the feature adds song recommendations to the queue which, if you like, can save directly in your playlist or Liked Songs. Recommended songs are indicated with a special green icon next to the artist's name (left) . This article goes in-depth on the topic + how to potentially disable Smart Shuffle, I'd recommend checking it out. Below are a few other common examples where you may encounter the app playing songs you haven't personally added to your playlists. Click the arrow to expand the list: Spoiler (Highlight to read) Leftover songs in the queue - Manually added, most likely during a past listening session. Premium users can remove those with the 'Clear queue' button. Autoplay - When the current queue ends, Autoplay automatically generates a new one with similar content so that the playback doesn't stop. You can toggle it off in the app's 'Settings' > 'Playback'. Check 3rd-party apps - Third-party services linked to your Spotify account may be adding songs to your playlists. It's recommended you remove any apps you don't recognize to keep your account secure. Active Jam session - You might have an active Jam session running. You can end it either from the Spotify Connect menu or from the Queue . Collaborative playlists - Someone else whom you shared the playlist with could've added the song. You can double-check this by opening the playlist's contents - you'll find the collaborator person who added the song next to its title. Active Jam session - You might have an active Jam session running. You can end it either from the Spotify Connect menu or from the Queue. Collaborative playlists - Someone else whom you shared the playlist with could've added the song. You can double-check this by opening the playlist's contents - you'll find the collaborator person who added the song next to its title. Hope this clears things up. In case you need additional assistance, feel free to share a screenshot / short screen recording which captures the described behavior in action so we can take a direct look at what's happening on your end. Cheers 🤘
usable_records.author: Yordan

usable_records.text: Expand Radio & Mix Playlists to 100 Songs I would like to suggest improving Spotify’s official Radio and Mix playlists by increasing their size from 50 songs to 100 songs. At the moment, many Radio and Mix playlists feel too short and repetitive. In my experience, playlists are often built from approximately 80–90% songs that are already in my liked songs, favorites, or listening history, while only 10–15% are truly new recommendations. Because of this, Radio and Mix playlists often do not feel like discovery tools anymore. Instead, they feel like a shorter repeated version of my own library. Current Problems 1. Too much repetition When playlists only contain 50 tracks, Spotify quickly fills them with songs I already know well. This makes the experience predictable. After listening to several radios or mixes, I often see the same songs repeated again and again. 2. Not enough music discovery One of the main reasons people use Radio is to discover similar artists, genres, and tracks. But with only a small number of songs and a high percentage of already liked tracks, there is very little room for new recommendations. This reduces the value of Radio playlists. 3. Short listening session A 50-song playlist can finish relatively quickly, especially for users who listen for many hours. Longer playlists would allow users to listen longer without repetition, avoid manually switching playlists, and stay in one mood or genre for more time. Suggested Solution Increase official Spotify Radio and Mix playlists from 50 songs to 100 songs. A suggested playlist structure could be: 50–60% familiar songs from liked songs, favorites, and listening history. 40–50% new recommendations based on listening taste, genre, artists, mood, and audio similarity. This balance would keep playlists comfortable but also exciting. Benefits for Users With 100-song playlists, users would get more variety, less repetition, more new discoveries, longer uninterrupted listening sessions, and a stronger feeling that Radio is personalized but still exploratory. Radio should feel like an intelligent music discovery engine, not a repeated copy of my existing library. Additional Optional Features Spotify could also let users customize Radio behavior: More Familiar mode — more favorite songs. Balanced mode — equal familiar and new songs. More Discovery mode — mostly new recommendations. This would give users more control over their listening experience. Final Suggestion Please consider increasing official Radio and Mix playlists from 50 to 100 songs. This simple change would significantly improve playlist quality, reduce repetition, and make Spotify much better for discovering new music while still keeping personalization.
usable_records.author: OBIDJON_ABDIEV

usable_records.text: I'm fed up with these glitchy playlists Plan Free Country US Device Moto G Power 25, Cricket Debut s2, Samsung Galaxy S25 Operating System Android 16 My Question or Issue I have so, so many. I wrote a whole @ss essay about this earlier and it got deleted when I tried to send it. Damn, you guys can't even run an online website. Anyways, I don't want to write the whole thing again, so I'm just gonna summarize the issues: 80% of the time, my main playlist only plays one artist, typically Juice WRLD but sometimes other artists. I have over 1,200 songs on the playlist and tons of artists, there is no reason for this to happen. Sometimes it will also only play songs from one album. It also adds songs I have never added or even listened to. (And no, it's not recommendations or in the "We added" section, they were smack dab in the middle of my playlist like I added them myself. When I unadd them, they get re-added as soon as I leave. The worst part is they aren't even songs I would listen to. I listen to 75% rap, and the rest is a mix of R&B, (American) Pop, Rock, and alternative. I don't listen to any K-Pop, and those are the songs they always add. I'm so sick of having to use all my skips on K-Pop songs I've never heard, and that's only on the rare occasions my playlist actually works and doesn't just play Juice WRLD. It's gotten to the point where I had to make a backup playlist that doesn't have Juice in it. Viewing albums is completely wack, it shuffles the order, adds songs that aren't on the album, then removes songs that are on the album. The library is also annoying, as it lists random artists I'm following before any of my playlists or albums. I'm finally just fed up with this f**k @ss playlist glitches. With all the money you make from overpriced subscriptions and giving out 4-5 ads for every 2 songs, you should be able to fix these issues easily. But they've been around for months. Don't try to tell me that it's just me, because I've tried logging out, restarting the app, deleting the app, trying a different phone, everything. Fix this app soon or I'm switching to Apple Music.
usable_records.author: oj_mills

usable_records.text: Give users more control over podcast recommendations and reduce clickbait-style content Feedback: One of the things I value about Spotify is the possibility of discovering high-quality music, podcasts, and long-form audio. But I’m increasingly concerned that the recommendation experience, especially around podcasts and video podcasts, is moving toward the same attention-maximizing pattern as YouTube, Instagram, or TikTok. I do not want Spotify to become another addictive feed optimized for whatever is most clickable, sensational, or emotionally engaging. I want recommendations that respect my intent, my time, and my intellectual standards. A recurring issue is that low-quality, clickbait-style podcasts or videos are recommended repeatedly. By “low quality,” I mean content that feels sensationalized, shallow, or not intellectually rigorous, even if it is highly engaging or popular. I would genuinely appreciate strong recommendations for thoughtful, well-produced, rigorous podcasts — but that is often not what I am seeing. The recommendations can feel like they are drifting toward the lowest common denominator. What I would like: - More control over the types of podcast channels and topics that are recommended. - A way to say “show me less content like this,” not just hide one individual item. - Controls for excluding certain formats, channels, topics, creators, or recommendation styles. - An option to prioritize higher-quality, more thoughtful, less clickbait-driven recommendations. - Better feedback tools that let users actively shape the recommendation system around their own values and listening goals. The underlying issue is not that I dislike recommendations. I actually want good recommendations. But I want recommendations that feel aligned with my values and intentions, rather than recommendations that seem optimized for compulsive engagement. I want to emphasize that I broadly love Spotify and would much rather see this improved than leave the product. But this issue matters enough to me that I have already had to aggressively delete apps or install extensions for other platforms to protect my attention. If Spotify moves further toward an addictive recommendation-feed model without giving users strong in-app controls, I may eventually be forced to cancel my subscription, even though I would prefer not to. A good outcome would be: When I open Spotify, I should feel like the product is helping me listen intentionally — not nudging me into the same kind of addictive browsing loop that exists on YouTube, Instagram, and TikTok.
usable_records.author: hugomoran159

usable_records.text: Re: How can you report fake AI artists? Sadly I don't believe spotify cares for the forseeable future. Noticed this a year ago, created a post somehwere here and on reddit i think. Nothing changed at all. Did a slightly more frustrtated one... they deleted it almost instantly. Seemingly got un-deleted afterwards. Still, bot or not, I don't care anymore. It "broke the camels back for me". I had spotify for pretty much 10 years at this point and still really like the service. The message I received 1 Minute after posting. The big response above "Histrocore...". Most likely automated. But doesn't help in this case if Spotify permits AI songs, then uses AI to clean up the reception of it. Surely accidental, but why should I honestly care. But it is pretty evident, that they don't care about customers at all. Let the customer choose and require the artist to specify stuff. I understand it's not an easy feat, but it seems there is not even any attempt about doing something. I'd gladly come back once there is some kind of option to filter out songs that have been mostly generated by AI (for purposes of non-artists making cheap songs quickly, a proper artist using it as a tool is not a problem imo). Ever since a year, I very often had this paranoia "Is the song I'm listening to from some human or did an AI generate it". Especially since I heard some songs of friends that made quick meme songs, I started to notice the familiar voice hard. I understand that spotify may not want to out-right ban something that could prove a valuable opportunity for them. After all a companies goal is still to make money and become sustainable/profitable. But not giving users any choice about this and praying it doesn't get noticed is the wrong way imo. So sorry, not sorry, but for me this ruined what I loved most about spotify for me: Discovering cool music and just being able to listen for hours to recommendations care-free while finding some awesome gems. If this ever gets adressed I might come back, but by then I might also have settled with my alternative too much already...
usable_records.author: LinusCDE98

usable_records.text: Re: Spotify - The Worst App in the World EVER The problem with opinions... is that everyone has one 😉 While I can definitely agree that the app isn't perfect I also don't recognize myself in your issues either. First things first: when you mention the app I'm assuming you're talking about the phone app, and not the Windows (or Mac (if that exists)) app? But second... very important: all those apps use the same so called 'engine'. Your recommendations are not necessarily provided by the app, but rather the "Spotify engine" (as I like to call it). Here's the problem: Spotify recommendations are solely based on what you checked off as stuff you like (and I'm also assuming they sometimes take listening time into consideration as well). The good news though is that you can (re)train Spotify if needed, let me show you: If you check the lower right corner of either the Spotify website (or the desktop (!) app) then you'll notice the controls shown above (at the bottom): see the control I highlighted? That's the queue button, and it will show you what songs Spotify has picked for you to play next, based on your selections / preferences. So if there are songs here which you really don't appreciate then you can actually mark them as such. Just open their context menu (the three dots which will pop up when you hover your mouse over an entry) and click on that. This allows you to perform tasks on that song. Stuff like adding it to your favorites, but also allowing you to exclude those songs from your taste profile; thus telling Spotify not to recommend those anymore. Sure, this can become a drag: having to do this a few times but trust me when I say that things will get better over time. And those changes will also reflect on your apps as well. Hope this can help. PS: I dunno about you but my moods and thus taste in music also often changes. When I'm doing an evening walk I prefer softer, more casual, music then during a powerwalk during the day. SO songs which you may dislike now... may be soothing sometimes later. Or maybe that's just me 😉
usable_records.author: ShelLuser

usable_records.text: Just make it make sense... Plan Premium Country N/A Device Yes. Operating System Definitely. My Question or Issue I've recently actively been removing a specific genre of music from my Liked Songs, my Playlists, etc. and Smart Shuffle is now exclusively recommending me that same genre ... The genre I'm actively removing from my profile . It wasn't doing this before I started removing it. In fact, my taste profile wasn't in too bad of a shape before I started deciding to refine my Liked Songs literally for Smart Shuffle , so it would have an easier time with recommendations for me. However, it has somehow had the exact opposite effect , and now a genre I am actively removing and have not listened to in about 8 years is dominating my taste profile. Let's be real here: Spotify's algorithm with things such as Discover Weekly, For You, and Smart Shuffle in general has historically, and continues to be, as effective at curating diverse music discovery as a quadriplegic is at pole vaulting. However, even despite the service feeling like it's actively trying to rage-bait me into cancelling my Premium, my taste profile had recently managed to be okay-ish and I had my hopes. Now those hopes have been shoved into a urinal and given a swirlie... I don't even want a solution at this point, as it's pretty clear from over my many years of Spotify use that for some wild reasoning, the company refuses to improve their algorithm at all and instead decides to force tone-deaf features down the consumer's throat. Things such as removing Disliking from Discover Weekly and replacing with the completely benign "Hide Song" button for example. This is just so incredibly wack. Like, at first it was frustrating, then disappointing, and eventually saddening. Now it's like watching a paper school bus full of infants slowly drifting down a snow bank into a pit of starving feral hogs. It's just depressing. All I want, all I genuinely want, someone to try and make it make sense to me. Please just try... How does it make sense that while actively removing a genre from my taste profile, the algorithm then feeds me ONLY that genre?
usable_records.author: JoeCeezy

usable_records.text: Why I canceled my subscription due to "algorithmic pollution" and lack of in-app profile switching Dear Spotify Product & Development Team, I am writing to provide constructive feedback regarding a fundamental UX and algorithmic issue that ultimately led me to cancel my Spotify subscription after being a loyal user for a long time. I hope this insight is helpful for your future product roadmap. The Context: For several months, I regularly visited a nursing home and played music for the residents using my smartphone. The Core Issues: Lack of In-App Profile Switching: Even with a Premium Family plan, switching to a different profile on a single device is highly impractical, as it requires a complete manual logout and login. Because of this friction, I had to use my primary personal profile to play music during these visits. Irreversible "Algorithmic Pollution": The music played during these visits completely altered my personalized recommendations. Even after the nursing home visits ended, I waited several months for the algorithm to normalize and relearn my actual taste. Unfortunately, it never recovered. No Algorithm Reset Option: There is no dedicated tool to manually correct the algorithm or completely reset the taste profile. The frequently suggested workaround in the community—deleting the account and starting fresh—means losing all curated libraries and playlists. From a user's perspective, being told to "start over from scratch" is functionally equivalent to being prompted to cancel the subscription and move to a competitor. Conclusion: I genuinely enjoyed Spotify's features. However, the inability to shield my algorithm (e.g., via a seamless profile switch on a single device, similar to video streaming platforms) or easily correct it once it was altered made the service practically unusable for my personal music discovery. I hope you will consider implementing a true "Reset Algorithm" button or a frictionless multi-profile switch within the app in the future. Best regards, Robert Jaeckel
usable_records.author: rjackmuc

usable_records.text: Last.fm Integration for AI DJ — Better Recommendations from Your Full Listening History The AI DJ is a great concept, but its recommendations are only as good as the data it has access to. Right now, there are two significant limitations: It only considers the last 2 years of your Spotify listening history It completely ignores playlists you've created or saved , which often reflect your music taste better than recent streams It has no awareness of music you listen to outside of Spotify , whether that's local files, other streaming services, or offline libraries This means the AI DJ can end up in a bubble — constantly recycling familiar recent listens while missing entire genres, artists, or eras you love but just don't happen to stream on Spotify. The fix: Last.fm integration. Last.fm is a free, widely used service that "scrobbles" (tracks) your listening activity from virtually any music source — Spotify, Apple Music, YouTube Music, Tidal, Winamp, local media players, and more. Millions of users already have years, even decades, of listening data logged there. By allowing users to optionally link their Last.fm account to the AI DJ, Spotify could: Draw on a complete, long-term picture of your music taste — not just 2 years Include listening activity from other platforms , so no genre goes unrecognized Serve genuinely personalized recommendations instead of safe, recent-only picks This would be an opt-in feature , so it doesn't affect anyone who doesn't want it. But for the many listeners who use Spotify alongside other services, or who have deep listening histories that predate their Spotify account, it could transform the AI DJ from "decent shuffle" to a truly intelligent music companion. Last.fm already has an established API and an active user base. The integration path is well-defined. All it takes is the will to build it.
usable_records.author: KazuyaYuza

usable_records.text: Feedback on Spotify DJ voice and personality I want to give feedback on the current Spotify DJ voice and personality. In terms of music selection, it usually works quite well. The recommendations are often relevant, and the feature itself has clear value. My issue is not with the concept of DJ, and not with having a distinct style in itself. My issue is that the current style does not fit a large number of genres very well. The DJ has a very pronounced and distinctly American radio-style personality. This is not necessarily a problem in itself. In some contexts, this strong style can work. But with many genres, it becomes distracting rather than immersive. With genres such as folk, for example, the transitions feel tonally out of place because the spoken presentation clashes with the atmosphere of the music. This is made even more noticeable by the fact that many listeners in the countries where Spotify operates do not have the same cultural familiarity with this kind of highly expressive American host style. Because of that, the DJ can feel less like a natural companion to the music and more like an interruption. That stands out even more because Spotify is not an American company. I would expect either a more neutral default voice, or a wider range of presentation styles that fit different genres and audiences better. I would strongly suggest adding one or more of the following: a neutral DJ voice option, multiple voice/personality options, genre-sensitive presentation styles, a setting to reduce or disable spoken transitions. Again, the music selection is often good. The main problem is not that the DJ has a distinct personality, but that the current personality and delivery style are too culturally specific and too mismatched with many genres to work well as a universal default.
usable_records.author: SpaceAlot

usable_records.text: Re: Remove the DJ AI or at least give the option to turn it off The DJ is very annoying. I've tried it a few times and he's constantly talking instead of just playing music. It's feels like the same exact annoyance as an advertisement playing between songs. I just tested it again, he starts talking as soon as you click play, 5 skips in, he babbling again. Just play the music and shut up, sheesh. I wish they would actually implement some better features or good AI features. Like describing a song or song video and being able to find that song based on your description. Google/YouTube do this for me and guess the song accurately most of the times. I try that on Spotify and it's just a waste of time. So I'm forced off the platform to go search and just end of watching/listening somewhere else. Or the terrible ability with finding specific songs from artists. Want to find an old popular song you can't remember from an artist? Have fun clicking through 20 albums trying to hunt it down. The artist page gives you what, 10 popular tracks only from the latest album? Other platforms give you 20-30 song list of their most popular songs all-time. Much easier to find an old song you were looking for. There's some other annoying little things they do, like dimming the video preview on the sidebar. Why? You leave album covers undimmed, but then dim video snippets? What logical sense does it make forcing users hover over the video to see it at full brightness? One of the most annoying things, constantly playing the same songs over and over in discovery/recommendations. Crazy they don't have a "I don't like this song" to stop playing it in 2026.
usable_records.author: _jeffrey

usable_records.text: Stop Payola and paid placement in Recommendations I'm not sure how long this has been going on but it's gross and makes me not want to use the radio feature anymore. Paying for placement on radio without announcement is payola (2). There's a disclaimer on playlists notifying that now the recommendation engine can be manipulated with money, and spotify isn't subject to FCC, but the concept is the same. I don't care about the reassurances that my tastes will be taken into account. Why do I pay monthly? I pay monthly so I don't get advertised to. This is another form of advertisement. Please stop. (1) https://www.spotify.com/us/safetyandprivacy/understanding-recommendations/plain (2) https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/47/317 How do commercial considerations impact recommendations? Spotify prioritizes listener satisfaction when recommending content. In some cases, commercial considerations, such as the cost of content or whether we can monetize it, may influence our recommendations. For example, Discovery Mode gives artists and labels the opportunity to identify songs that are a priority for them, and our system will add that signal to the algorithms that determine the content of personalized listening sessions. When an artist or label turns on Discovery Mode for a song, Spotify charges a commission on streams of that song in areas of the platform where Discovery Mode is active (Discovery Mode is not active in our editorial playlists). This signal increases the likelihood of the selected songs being recommended, but does not guarantee it. We only recommend songs there is a high probability listeners will enjoy. As with all recommendations, we take note when a listener isn't engaging with a song — including those in Discovery Mode — and factor this in when determining what to recommend in the future.
usable_records.author: 34ĩ73867576asdf

usable_records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs Spotify - it’s time to address what’s becoming an obvious problem: the platform is being flooded with AI-generated filler music, and users have no way to filter it out. I’m a paying listener. I curate my playlists. I rely on Discover Weekly, Release Radar, and radio recommendations to find human-made music (i.e real artists, real creativity, real performances). But lately, it’s getting harder to trust that what I’m hearing is even written, performed, or produced by an actual person. Let me be clear: I’m not anti-technology. AI has its place as an efficiency tool. But there’s a massive difference between people using technology and algorithms churning out fully generated tracks with no human authorship or even musicianship. And right now, Spotify treats them as if they’re the same. They are not. What users need is simple: A filter that excludes AI-generated music from recommendations, playlists, searches and feeds, clear metadata that shows when AI was used in lyrics, composition, performance, production, or all of the above, Transparency. Choice. Control over the listening experience we’re paying for. If Spotify can filter explicit content, live versions, remasters, and audio quality, it can absolutely let users filter out AI-generated content. There’s no technical limitation, only a lack of prioritization. And yes, this matters. AI music is already diluting the platform, overwhelming algorithms, and burying real artists who put genuine human creativity into their work. Spotify: If AI music is here to stay, so is the need for transparency - Give users the option to hear human-made music. This is a reasonable, necessary step for the future of the platform. Please make it happen.
usable_records.author: AutomaticEight
