usable_records.text: #MusicMonday Review - March 2026 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check February's Part 2 review for more music. In this issue, let's explore the inner works of the mind, and decision making. What makes us anxious? What gives us the sense of freedom? Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 Fayette – I Should Never satisfied Never enough time Only perfect moves Now I crave the mess that makes me mine Always out of rhyme Never being true Holding on to flawless lines Now I crave the mess that makes me mine We could, we should, and we will begin our journey in Nijmegen-Arnhem, Netherlands for a Downtempo Indie Pop track about the decisions we make, and the process we go through, for better or for worse: " The song came from noticing how often my inner voice says ‘I should’ instead of ‘I want.’ It wasn’t one single person or moment, but more a pattern I saw in myself — constantly trying to meet invisible standards and over-correcting every little flaw. The track grew out of that tension between perfectionism and authenticity, and the realization that I was performing control instead of allowing myself to be human. Writing it felt like loosening that grip a little. But it’s a process of course. 😉 " spotify:track:6FF3hIQaLWDHGwVBGtyMb0:small Sophia Galaté – Please Don't Talk To Me you know you haven't seen me around I've been spending all my days inside I'm just tryna get my mind right even if it takes me such a long time and I'm not gonna let nobody bother me I really think I've lost my mind I'm not in the space to even entertain anything until I've really changed I'm getting my life together babe From hesitation, we now go to isolation in Los Angeles, CA with a soulful R&B jam about living better by healing in peace on your own: "So in the summer of 2023 I was, I wouldn't say depressed, but I was just going through a very low point in my life, and a family friend of mine called me to catch up on the phone, and she was like so bubbly on the other line. Like 'hey girl, how are you? How's your summer? Are you talking to anyone? Are you going out?' To which I replied, 'No, I'm literally just inside going to therapy, and if anyone tries to talk to me during this time, I'm just like, please don't talk to me'. And she was like 'Ha, that should be a song'. Then I conveniently just received a bunch of beats from this producer overseas, and one of them was the beat to Please Don't Talk To Me . It just fit perfectly. I just basically wrote all the lyrics kind of over the course of the month, and it was just like it was exactly what I was going through at the time, but what's so cool about the song is that the song doesn't sound depressing at all. It's very much like a celebration of your alone time and being excited to get your life together and move into the next phase." spotify:track:7aH4Gbn3k600txlFtTV7Az:small Filthy Red – The Afters So he called her once Called her twice Smoking cheap cigs from a dodgy local And spouting bollocks through the night He can’t get what he wants So now he’s gagging for a fight But you can’t get close to anyone When you’re a snakeskin parasite Let's fly now to Kingston upon Hull, England for an Indie Rock track about waking up, and asking yourself, just for a moment, how things got derailed the night before: "You know, usually I’ll come up with a line or melodic idea that sticks with me when I write. I came up with the first verse to this one pretty quickly and it kind of wrote itself from there. Once I had that verse nailed I started to really delve deep and it turned into this vibe about how we can get so stuck in the rat race, working a 9-5 that when it gets to the weekend we lose ourselves in the chaos of letting go sometimes and don’t always know when to stop. I know a few people where that’s definitely the case. So I guess that’s what it ended up being about really. Slogging it out through the week and then when the free time hits, letting loose, but maybe going a little too far sometimes." spotify:track:0xjaxlBRjmWNO5hAgL9Prx:small ABFAB – Wide Open Spaces The sun sinks low behind the hills The air is quiet, the world stands still Empty roads stretch for miles unseen A painted sky in shades of tangerine Tires hum like a lonesome tune Under the glow of a silver moon A sign says nowhere just up ahead But I'm alive in the life I've led We now travel far to Cape Town, South Africa for a catchy Pop song about the freedom of driving by yourself under the blinking stars of the night, leaving everything behind, and looking forward to what tomorrow may bring: "The song is about me and my drummer on the roads to our gigs. We wrote it for all the long drives we have had and the opportunities we have experienced. It’s all self experiences with all my writing" spotify:track:3WKsyeKrAkC5pFJWqCa8mc:small Portrait Generation – Grassroots Being as optimistic as I can Not my fault it’s just the way that things are ran It seems that triumph's written in their fate Well it’s easy when it’s handed on a plate For us the struggle's real Their wealth will be concealed The labour grows the fruit Not for the Grassroots We finish our trip in Southend-on-Sea, England, for an Indie Rock track with no lack of drive or passion, but knows that breaking through may depend on your class: " So the story behind Grassroots really came from the sad reality that grassroots bands are really struggling in the music industry at the moment and that there’s an illusion of equal opportunity when it’s just not the case! Bands with financial backing from either a label or parents (for example) are the ones that succeed, and working class musicians continue to struggle working full time jobs alongside the music just trying to stay afloat. " spotify:track:3GLVWuFPKPWHAmMynVVu9X:small Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P See you next time!
usable_records.author: osornios

usable_records.text: #MusicMonday Review - October 2025 Part 2 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check October's Part 1 review for more music. We continue our music listening journey with five unique genres, with different takes in what reality means nowadays, and, as usual, do not disappoint. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 NEVEN – 12 Bars I love the twelve-bar blues alright, but the twelve-bar windows ain't for me, I stood my ground you locked me up, drug lords and killers walking free, I got twelve bars on my window baby, I sing twelve-bar blues tonight Our journey lands us in Liverpool, England, for a Blues Rock track about having strong convictions, and putting up a fight for them when necessary: "It's a mix of personal experience and just something that we see everyday as well really - people getting penalized and locked up for speaking up their mind while a lot of big criminals get away with what they do. Basically it's criticism of corrupt justice systems globally. But of course it's also wordplay on 12 bars, which in this context represents the bars on a prison-cell window as well as 12 bars in the traditional blues structure." spotify:track:5eN21PrnxXyygeu4CjZTaQ:small Cranc. – Something Don't Feel Right I'm tired off all these pound shop lad bands going on about how working class they are My dad was born in a council estate, my mum came here on a boat but you don't hear me going on about it... wait? "All my life I felt like something was wrong in this land," she said But there ain't not secret plot or dark forces trying to implant microchips in your bloodstream The rot... the rot! It's inside us; it's us We travel now to Barcelona, Spain for a Post Punk track about being uneasy, without being able to explain or ignore why. All you can see is the emptiness in people's eyes: "The track grew out of feelings of otherness. The seemingly progressive protagonist purports to loathe the mining of working class experience for artistic respect, while arguably doing the same thing himself. Conspiratorial apocalyptic movements instinctively feel how wrong the world is, but their alienation leads them astray. Everything is not quite as it seems. The song was written in a single night in a kind of inchoate fury against where we are right now." spotify:track:6xXccrqvVevWLnc6nM9Gfq:small Moss Side Sauna Club – MY WEIRD APPETITE Up on the English coastline, Up where the air is clearer, I'm getting weird effing looks, From everyone I hold dear, Don't send me any postcards, Not least till I get better, Go tell the English coastguard, To readdress my letters, Let's travel back to Manchester, England for an ElectroClash song about the desire for the new, wanting all of everything, all the time. Is it a crime?: "the song’s a satire on how people see bisexuality - so it was a way of owning the caricature! NYC dance punk, Peaches & M.I.A were an influence." spotify:track:6V7E6yq3c9xHTSZ4H0Tls1:small RxGhost – Sponsored Content Even the dullest setting on that light’s too bright Some of us are trying to sleep Being around is not the same as being here Neither of us are in this room Top ten places you can go to find yourself Click the link to find out more Looking at pictures of the places we won’t go I’d much rather stay indoors We now to Kansas City, MO. for a shoegazey Danger Pop track about what social gatherings, and reality in general, have become: sitting tight, and doom scroll. The content on the screen is just more real: "There were times when we were all hanging out and everyone was on their device and it got me thinking about how it’s just kind of a strange new option that wasn’t even available ten years ago, how you never really have to be bored at all anymore and how you can be in a group of people but everyone is in their own world. I feel like boredom can be helpful, in that it can trigger creativity, and now that there’s the option to never be bored, it’s a struggle to get people to try hard things or invent ways to entertain themselves, it’s just so easy and comfortable to scroll. And then that kind of spiraled into thinking about how inauthentic most of what we see is, and how advertising is creeping its way deeper and deeper into the content, so that the lines are super blurred between a real endorsement of something by someone you follow and a paid ad." spotify:track:44oBUcv9gsvueUwvSjB4TM:small Suspire – Deepstate Dive Say about your day, The thought fell away, You stare, Deep into my mind's eye, I'll take a step if you say, Bye, You think that you went for the one you wanted, You thought that you would get the gift of time, You said I could solve the case you're solving, Guess lies hard to do while the courts adjourned We end up in Oxford, England for an Indie Rock track with a punky bass that takes you on a blindsided trip down to nowhere and back, where nothing is what it was before: " Deepstate Dive explores a moment, or an encounter, that pushes the subject to a deeper level of consciousness. What follows is a spiral of reevaluating past choices that were made, and struggling to come to terms with how a newfound world view, doesn’t fit with the path of life they’ve walked down to date. The track treads a tightrope of ominous anthemia, reminiscent of Skinty Fia-era Fontaines DC." spotify:track:3c8GS83zYBpl77HMWzunQi:small Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P See you next time!
usable_records.author: osornios

metadata.title: #MusicMonday Review - October 2025 Part 2
metadata.search_snippet: #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check October's Part 1 review for more music. We continue our music listening journey with five unique genres, with different takes in what reality means nowadays, and, as usual, do not disappoint. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 NEVEN – 12 Bars I love the twelve-bar blues alright, but the twelve-bar windows ain't for me, I stood my ground you locked me up, drug lords and killers walking free, I got twelve bars on my window baby, I sing twelve-bar blues tonight Our journey lands us in Liverpool, England, for a Blues Rock track about having strong convictions, and putting up a fight for them when necessary: "It's a mix of personal experience and just something that we see everyday as well really - people getting penalized and locked up for speaking up their mind while a lot of big criminals get away with what they do. Basically it's criticism of corrupt justice systems globally. But of course it's also wordplay on 12 bars, which in this context represents the bars on a prison-cell window as well as 12 bars in the traditional blues structure." spotify:track:5eN21PrnxXyygeu4CjZTaQ:small Cranc. – Something Don't Feel Right I'm tired off all these pound shop lad bands going on about how working class they are My dad was born in a council estate, my mum came here on a boat but you don't hear me going on about it... wait? "All my life I felt like something was wrong in this land," she said But there ain't not secret plot or dark forces trying to implant microchips in your bloodstream The rot... the rot! It's inside us; it's us We travel now to Barcelona, Spain for a Post Punk track about being uneasy, without being able to explain or ignore why. All you can see is the emptiness in people's eyes: "The track grew out of feelings of otherness. The seemingly progressive protagonist purports to loathe the mining of working class experience for artistic respect, while arguably doing the same thing himself. Conspiratorial apocalyptic movements instinctively feel how wrong the world is, but their alienation leads them astray. Everything is not quite as it seems. The song was written in a single night in a kind of inchoate fury against where we are right now." spotify:track:6xXccrqvVevWLnc6nM9Gfq:small Moss Side Sauna Club – MY WEIRD APPETITE Up on the English coastline, Up where the air is clearer, I'm getting weird effing looks, From everyone I hold dear, Don't send me any postcards, Not least till I get better, Go tell the English coastguard, To readdress my letters, Let's travel back to Manchester, England for an ElectroClash song about the desire for the new, wanting all of everything, all the time. Is it a crime?: "the song’s a satire on how people see bisexuality - so it was a way of owning the caricature! NYC dance punk, Peaches & M.I.A were an influence." spotify:track:6V7E6yq3c9xHTSZ4H0Tls1:small RxGhost – Sponsored Content Even the dullest setting on that light’s too bright Some of us are trying to sleep Being around is not the same as being here Neither of us are in this room Top ten places you can go to find yourself Click the link to find out more Looking at pictures of the places we won’t go I’d much rather stay indoors We now to Kansas City, MO.  for a shoegazey Danger Pop track about what social gatherings, and reality in general, have become: sitting tight, and doom scroll. I feel like boredom can be helpful, in that it can trigger creativity, and now that there’s the option to never be bored, it’s a struggle to get people to try hard things or invent ways to entertain themselves, it’s just so easy and comfortable to scroll. And then that kind of spiraled into thinking about how inauthentic most of what we see is, and how advertising is creeping its way deeper and deeper into the content, so that the lines are super blurred between a real endorsement of something by someone you follow and a paid ad." spotify:track:44oBUcv9gsvueUwvSjB4TM:small Suspire – Deepstate Dive Say about your day, The thought fell away, You stare, Deep into my mind's eye, I'll take a step if you say, Bye, You think that you went for the one you wanted, You thought that you would get the gift of time, You said I could solve the case you're solving, Guess lies hard to do while the courts adjourned We end up in Oxford, England for an Indie Rock track with a punky bass that takes you on a blindsided trip down to nowhere and back, where nothing is what it was before: " Deepstate Dive explores a moment, or an encounter, that pushes the subject to a deeper level of consciousness. usable_records.text: #MusicMonday Review - January 2026 Part 1 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check December's review for more music. In this issue, the music is bold, deeply reflective, and unapologetically honest. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 AVA RENN – None the Wiser This land I’ve walked is cold and barren No time to talk, no I’m not sharing anymore... Black and blue, the sirens blaring Vow of silence quiets the screaming in my core Find no peace beside this water So I wade out where I can’t breathe I’ll get no rest inside this slaughterhouse I’ve built... These walls have robbed me of my sleep Hello, from Marfa, TX. We begin in full force with a gritty Rock track about the pieces that are missing, and the colors not seen by the numbness we all occasionally feel: "Thematically, the song is about a desperate try to break cycles that harm you. As humans the knowledge of what should be done sometimes gets lost in excuses or turmoil or bouts of depression. This song is an emblem of introspection to try and get out of that deep end." spotify:track:7kpD4QFkmmyIcWTUw8ZfSl:small Vik Faded – Sansa Stark tus stories me dan celos cada vez que tú sales roneando por los bares que cualquiera te vale vivo encaprichao’ de ti buscándote en mil lugares pensando que lo haces pa dejarme señales We now fly to Madrid, Spain, for a Latin Pop track about those physical, but toxic, relationships you can't get out of, just because it makes you feel truly alive: "I came up with the idea in the studio with Mena Labib. It took as one hour and a half completing the first demo, the inspiration was literally the two of us making noise!" spotify:track:3wG7i2SsymfWHslZkDcAmQ:small Project Overload – Late Night Dancing Our heads in the clouds While we run around No cares in the world This moment is ours Lost in a daydream I’m feeling hazy Wrapped in our fun We're going crazy From Coventry, England we get a Janglepop track about the joy of dancing, being surrounded with like-minded people. Get into the groove: "It’s our third single of the year and the final part of a three-song narrative. Silhouettes was starry-eyed romance, Wildfire was that new love breaking down, and Late Night Dancing is about moving on and dancing the blues away. It started as a rehearsal jam with some new effects pedals, then sprang to life taking inspiration from Nile Rodgers, cowbells, water-glass chimes, ‘50s B-movies and Blondie’s Heart of Glass. We played it live for Record Store Day the week it was written, with lyric sheets scattered on the stage. It came together pretty quickly, and though it doesn’t sound much like the rest of the new album, we knew it was a banger, so it had to be a single. I wrote the chords as a bit of fun but it seemed to work because we all really like this one." spotify:track:09Nu3wglBGgIHLMQiQFbRU:small Jared Popma – The Cosmic Joke Do you ever feel like you're not where you should be And everything comes a little too slowly And no one's saying you got so much time to figure it out? Maybe it's time now to figure it out So you get a job where you make all your money It's kind of sad but you laugh like it's funny When it feels like you're part of a big cosmic joke You work your whole life but you still wind up broke Let's travel now to Vancouver, BC, for a Folk Pop track about finding out who you really are, by looking for your place in the world: "I was in nearing the end of my degree, and struggling with the weight of beginning life in the 'real world'. Would I be able to make it in music? Is that what I even want? If not, what else am I going to do? It all felt overwhelming, but I also felt like my feelings didn’t matter since everyone has to go through similar things. It’s an acknowledgment that my struggles are nothing necessarily special, but still wishing for some sort of validation in my feelings." spotify:track:6skEfSyls9yXF6lUPB74D4:small Pinocchio is dead – The boy who never dies Paper heart, folded twice, Kept it burning under ice. Every truth I tried to say Turned to ash and blew away. Mirror eyes, wooden smile, I’ve been lying all the while. If you cut my strings tonight, Would I fall or learn to fly? Our trip ends in Rome, Italy, for a Synth Pop track about performing to others in automatic, without questioning why. Part exploration, part emotion, who are you performing for? "The track came from real life... moments, mistakes, and all the noise in between. The Boy Who Never Die is about looking back, cutting loose what holds you down, and finding peace in the wreckage. Not everyone’s meant for your story, and that’s fine. In my mind, it had to sound poetic, dark, and a little theatrical 😜 🤣 it’s about the fragility and the rebellion of someone who can’t help but stay true to who they are." spotify:track:6t0pEQSffKYxzPNh2Jwl7k:small #MusicMonday 2025 It's time to recap 2025 with this year's compilation. 76 titles from original artists covering all types of genres, represent the multiple trends independent artists were proud to establish. Have a listen: spotify:playlist:71fadol0hgsdSjLpM828HZ Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P See you next time!
usable_records.author: osornios

usable_records.text: Playlist options for personal app development Hello Developer community, and Spotify. Longtime listener , first-time caller . Investor , also, if that matters. Plan Premium Country USA Device iPhone 17 Pro Max, Macbook Air 2019, iPad Pro (newest) Operating System iOS 26, MacOS Sonoma My Question or Issue I am a personal user aspiring to enhance music recommendations in the context of my life - what I'm doing, where I'm going, etc. I have built an app designed to be used by me and only me, but I'm having trouble getting anything playlist related to work (i.e. adding to one, getting track lists from existing, saving liked songs, etc). I receive a 403 forbidden error. From what I can tell, this is because I am in "development mode". Since it's a personal application, I don't qualify for "extended quota mode". I have an LLC I could use to request EQ mode but am wondering if it'll get approved, much less whether it's necessary. Is there a path for personal/developer use that unlocks read access to playlist tracks and basic library write operations? These are operations on my own data, for my own account, running on my own infrastructure. More Details ## Context I'm building a personal music curation tool — a single-user app that only I use. It tracks my listening history, builds a taste model from my own library, discovers new music from artists I follow, and creates curated sessions. It runs on Cloudflare Workers (serverless) and interacts solely with my own Spotify account. My account is Premium. My Spotify email is listed in the app's User Management. The app has both "Web API" and "Web Playback SDK" checked in the dashboard. ## What works These endpoints function correctly in Development Mode: | Endpoint | Method | Purpose | |----------|--------|---------| | `/v1/me` | GET | User profile | | `/v1/me/tracks` | GET | Read saved/liked songs | | `/v1/me/top/tracks` | GET | Top tracks (all time ranges) | | `/v1/me/top/artists` | GET | Top artists (all time ranges) | | `/v1/me/playlists` | GET | List user's playlists | | `/v1/me/following` | GET | Followed artists | | `/v1/me/player/currently-playing` | GET | Currently playing track | | `/v1/me/player/queue` | POST | Add track to queue | | `/v1/search` | GET | Search for tracks/artists/playlists | | `/v1/artists/{id}/albums` | GET | Artist discography | ## What is blocked (403 Forbidden) ### Playlist track access (READ) | Endpoint | Method | Purpose | Impact | |----------|--------|---------|--------| | `/v1/playlists/{id}/tracks` | GET | Read tracks from any playlist | **Cannot read tracks from my own playlists.** This is the single biggest limitation. My app builds a taste model partly from seasonal playlists I've manually curated — I need to read the tracks in my own playlists to know what songs I've associated with each season. This is read-only access to my own data. | This also blocks reading Spotify editorial playlists (New Music Friday, Fresh Finds, etc.) for music discovery. ### Browse endpoints (READ) | Endpoint | Method | Purpose | Impact | |----------|--------|---------|--------| | `/v1/browse/new-releases` | GET | New album/single releases | Cannot discover new releases. Workaround: using `/v1/search` with `year:2026` filter, but this is less comprehensive. | | `/v1/browse/featured-playlists` | GET | Spotify's featured playlists | Cannot browse editorial playlist selections. | ### Library write operations | Endpoint | Method | Purpose | Impact | |----------|--------|---------|--------| | `/v1/me/tracks` | PUT | Save a track to Liked Songs | Cannot like/save tracks programmatically. My dashboard has a "like" button that can't function. | | `/v1/me/tracks` | DELETE | Remove from Liked Songs | Cannot unlike tracks. | | `/v1/playlists/{id}/tracks` | POST | Add tracks to a playlist | **Cannot populate playlists.** I can create empty playlists but cannot add tracks to them. This means my curated sessions can only output via direct playback or queue — I can't save a session as a playlist for later. | ### Playback (intermittent) | Endpoint | Method | Purpose | Impact | |----------|--------|---------|--------| | `/v1/me/player/play` | PUT | Start playback | Sometimes returns 403. May be device-dependent rather than a blanket restriction. Playback via queue (`POST /me/player/queue`) works reliably. | ## Summary of impact on my app 1. **Taste model is degraded.** I can't read my own playlist tracks, so my seasonal playlist analysis (a core feature) depends on tokens that intermittently gain/lose this permission. 2. **Music discovery is limited.** I can't scan new releases or editorial playlists. I work around this with search, but miss breadth. 3. **No playlist output.** Curated sessions can play immediately or queue, but I can't save them as playlists for later listening. 4. **No library management.** Can't like or unlike tracks from my app's interface. 5. **No editorial playlist scanning.** Can't read tracks from Spotify-owned playlists like New Music Friday, which would significantly improve discovery quality. ## My request I'm a single user building a personal tool. I'm not building a commercial product. I understand Extended Quota Mode requires 250K+ MAU and a registered business — that's clearly not applicable here. Is there a path for personal/developer use that unlocks read access to playlist tracks and basic library write operations? These are operations on my own data, for my own account, running on my own infrastructure. Specific asks, in priority order: 1. `GET /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks` — read my own playlists 2. `PUT /v1/me/tracks` — save tracks to my own library 3. `POST /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks` — add tracks to my own playlists 4. `GET /v1/browse/new-releases` — browse new releases
usable_records.author: chrisbuice

usable_records.text: #MusicMonday Review - April 2026 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check March's review for more music. In this issue, we tackle on the subject of human conflict, including those times when you battle with your own self. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 Morgendust – King of Lonely Souls She went astray With demons haunting her Holding on to every bit of rope Faith is hope and we’re running out You want the world that you can’t control You want to touch what you can not hold Come on and get it, come on and get it I’m pretty sure you know me I’m the king of lonely souls We begin this musical journey in Zwolle, Netherlands, with a Cinematic Indie Rock track about being trapped in a toxic world of sadness, where grief is love with no place to go. Band member Marco explains: " I have a religious background and I wanted to write about the devil… but what/who is the devil? Everyone faces their own ‘devil’. In this song it’s a woman who suffers depression. Something I was a witness of in my circle of friends. 😉 " spotify:track:74WuO3lpYnPUcFzyhAGkta:small Icarus Phoenix – Boiled Peanuts, misheard (feat. Chasing Mice) I could be brutal instead feeding you poison sweetened Desiccate all you once were whatever you’re saying misheard Honey it’s easy to squeeze a trigger that’s pointing toward me A storm of resentment in you Robotic and silent, a ruse Let's fly now to Baltimore, MD. for an Indie Rock track with traces of Americana about a relationship where every conversation becomes the same, without truly listening to what's being said: "Lyrically, it's essentially about my ex-wife. I'm dealing with the difficulty of communication. Musically, [band member] Leena wrote the opening guitar line and then we collaborated in the structure and songwriting as a band for the rest." spotify:track:6oWw2zCT2ceALxQPRQpIcM:small Warm Coat – Expert Every subject I find You're so quick to remind us You're always right It's a skill that you honed Not to leave you exposed in the limelight Quick to claim solutions Though you never know Talk over the rest Talking about broken communication, let's now go to Rhondda Valley, South Wales, in the UK for a shoegazy Indie Rock track about how hard is to teach someone who thinks that knows everything, even when they actually don't: " I think everyone knows someone who can’t be advised because they think they already have the answers. It could be someone in the family or at work. In my case it was based on several people I’ve come into contact with in my life who talk over the room and cannot take advice. " spotify:track:15hCHMygbvR2sJL6Sh8h3Q:small Mike Chek & Grant Griffin – Saki Shots The hazmat kimono was half ass on My whole body was exposed to the acid rap zone I was molded To be a murderous ronin My reflex’s so fast in one second you folded Let's travel now to Detroit, MI for an Alt-Rap collaboration about fighting alone against the world, just like a japanese anime can do: " I met Grant on Discord, and we started building a boom bap album. Theme wise the approach was to alternate who starts each song and this specific one came about after watching an episode of samurai champloo ." spotify:track:0K3uD821Uk7SAlqlrvxUV8:small In the Variant – Anything You Want Are you listening To the sound of my voice Can you tell it’s me Stuck inside you head On repeat Even when you close your eyes You’ll see me all the time But I’m not really there you’re dreaming If there’s one thing That I know for sure Is you can’t have me anymore Our trip ends in Denver, CO. for a Hard Rock song about taking steps away form all that we've ever known, looking for something you may not even know yet: " It was shaped by a universal experience I think a lot of people grapple with. Anything you want is a song of struggle between security and risk. The feeling of knowing what is right for you in the long run but fighting the uncertainty of it. We often search for comfort and find it in places that don’t provide passion or fulfillment in any other areas of our life and the need for these things haunts us. Anything you want is about running from your dreams and your happiness only to find out that happiness and passion will always be where your heart leads you in the end. " spotify:track:5E6BASrrCqn4AaSH2iJO56:small Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P See you next time!
usable_records.author: osornios

usable_records.text: #MusicMonday Review - December 2025 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check November's review for more music. To finish the year, we bring you an all UK lineup, covering a whole range of genres, themes, and moods. Give them a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 Delves In Time – Big in Berlin Grey blocks rise in the freezing night The City life fades in the fading light Sound club calls and the lights go low High heels click where the young ones go I’m big in Berlin Under the shadow of the wall I hear David Bowie call Bahnhof Zoo is where we ran Losing more than we began We begin with an energetic Post Punk track, featuring an 80s inspired synth bass line that takes you to the 'Sound', a sonic evocation of german nightclubbing: "Big in Berlin was inspired by the cult 1981 film Christiane F. — I was struck by how young the kids were when they fell into that world. A trip to Berlin and visiting the film’s locations gave me a deeper connection to the story, and that atmosphere shaped the song." spotify:track:1KK9IR9hwtJGMWMro719cM:small Cheap Escape – Man Of The People Man of the people name your price You're only messing with people's lives We now travel to Kirkcaldy, Scotland for a high energy Punk Rock song featuring a catchy baseline every elected official could dance to: "Man of the People was written in the run up to the UK General Election in 2024. The song takes aim at populists from all political persuasions, in the age of social media, culture wars and disinformation. We were all just a bit annoyed at the state of things in the world, and it came out in the form of the song! The whole EP was inspired by the hopelessness brought about by the people in power and it carries a lot of that in the lyrics." spotify:track:6CboAX9rZMCNE5yYmHUmFi:small Midnight Housework – Just to be Enough It felt like love When she came around But it hurt like hell When she ran away We went different ways Down the road of life On the lonely days I still see you in my mind Do you fear the past Will taint your future life Even weary minds Will one day find the light Next stop, an bittersweet Alt Ambient Rock track from Belfast, Ireland that knows that there's no right way to live your life, just hold on tight, and enjoy the ride: "Instrumentally the song came from a guitar part that was meant to be in another song, but the band felt like the idea was strong enough to stand on its own feet and we went from there. So there wasn’t anyone or anything we were directly wanting to take influence from, it was a more organic, almost improvised process." spotify:track:4O5SBAdGF5AIuBdOOeGGFN:small Future Theory – The Haze Get out the way I need you Leave me to settle Think I'm a fool Thoughts are like whispers Dreams are like ghosts Maybe I'm young maybe I'm old We now move to Lincolnshire, England, for an atmospheric, psychedelic Alternative track about being a complicated self half the time, but positive in our minds. Are we wasting our lives? Band member Max explains: "The guitar part and vocal melody came first then [band member] Jacob added some bass. I got the lyric inspiration from things I see around me and how I've felt and how many people have felt. Something that can connect with people and that's being lost in life, not knowing your purpose, so many people seem lost. Like you feel like you're stuck in a haze that you can't get out of. Sounds a little depressing but then the song has much hope and the end really makes use of that. A sense of working your purpose out." spotify:track:6JtgtGwxUeeSg2tbvZh3sd:small The Blooms – Reminisce See my soul being changed my way I think I'm getting weaker day by day We finish in Derby, England, for an Indie Rock track whose distorted guitar sound takes us to an Indie scene gone by. Reminisce: "As simple as it sounds it just came from a writing session of taking a step back and thinking about things in the past and how times have changed within the band, all of our personal lives and within the city of Derby where we’re from, it’s mainly just a song about looking back on the past and how you can interpret it with many different moods." spotify:track:4fYjRF2l5vGAxZVAz62BnI:small Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:71fadol0hgsdSjLpM828HZ See you next time!
usable_records.author: osornios

usable_records.text: #MusicMonday Review - February 2026 Part 1 #MusicMonday is the hashtag I've been using for quite a while to share music recommendations from up-and-coming artists. Always fresh, and always different, trying to look for trends before they become one. You can check January's Part 2 review for more music. Music convey emotions. It can be by expressing love for someone, the excitement of going out, the peace of being by yourself, or even the (apparent) lack of creativity. Feel them all by giving a listen, with a word from the artists themselves. 🎧 PAO ROVAL – Bucle con tan solo un segundo tu mirada me hipnotiza un abismo sin salida donde no hay final el poder de tu sonrisa es lo que me paraliza o tu simple cercania me pone a temblar Our travels begin in Torreon, Mexico, for a Latin Pop track about the pain of falling in love with someone you think is out of your league. A feeling you just can't control: " The song came from the curiosity of what it feels like to be in love. There isn’t a specific person behind it; it was simply the mindset I had at that time. I knew that if I ever found that person, I would probably resist falling in love, because I’m very selective when it comes to love and, by nature, a free soul. " spotify:track:0EdYTS0PPZxfpseBByTJGe:small Andrew Johnson – Exercise In Style I see it all as an exercise in style My integrity is whatever makes me smile I fell from grace so long ago It's all I've ever really known Let's go now to Stockton-on-Tees, England for a New Wave influenced Pop Rock track that punches straight into emotional intensity from the very beginning: " A lot of the album was written with titles and phrases as a stimulus. Exercise In Style suggests somebody “painting by numbers”, creatively. The song itself is a little bit about my experience and knowledge of the music industry but also about being defiant in the face of either criticism or, let’s face it for the most part, apathy. " spotify:track:4aQy2iB9H4MRa7C1wr8M4S:small first class rebel – Shadows Fade Watching from windows With envious intent A bank full of time Unable to be spent Dreams were measured in the past A time cloaked in fear Let's travel now to Dublin, Ireland for a track that mixes Shoegaze with a touch of Psychedelia that will make you hide in the shadows as you stare into yourself: " The guitar chords came first and I just added to them by layering different guitar lines on them. I like the sound of the guitars and that creates the mood which for me is the most important thing. Simplicity is key for me! " spotify:track:04MTLiOWtrDby0HTDlPhq3:small Abbey Moon – Snooze Put the world on snooze Don't need anyone else just you Sky was clear, everything was frozen We were suspended in a moment Any door we want we can open I know you will get me through We now travel to London, England for an Acoustic Indie track about experiencing deserted streets in company of your loved one: " So the track was inspired by a late night walk with my girlfriend in Portobello road in London. It was very quiet as it was after everything had shut on a Sunday. It felt like a special moment and it made it seem like the world was on Snooze. The track was written acoustically so I intentionally kept it quite sparse with some added lead guitar and touches of piano. I hope it sounds like a bit of a lullaby. " spotify:track:2VsNU0YYiVzy4quIOkF0JB:small Tano Mora – Aquí en Madrid Yo se poco de todo y tu muy poco de mi Tengo el tiempo del mundo que quieras compartir Pégame tu risa y después el carmín Todas estas calles se abrirán para ti Ya se acaba la noche ya se ve amanecer Me miras a los ojos y no sabes que hacer Todo lo he soñado o es realidad? Pero estas a mi lado y no quiero escapar Abre tu corazón y ahora déjame entrar Que Madrid amanece y no puedo esperar From the quiet streets of London, we finish our trip with a busy night out in Madrid, Spain, thanks to this Latin Pop track about leaving everything behind, and get ready to enjoy your evening: " Well, I wanted to reflect how special Madrid nights are. And how special it is to fall in love there. " spotify:track:1TnvOTYlC8bltGElFV7dYm:small #MusicMonday 2025 It's time to recap 2025 with this year's compilation. 76 titles from original artists covering all types of genres, represent the multiple trends independent artists were proud to establish. Have a listen: spotify:playlist:71fadol0hgsdSjLpM828HZ Listen to them and much more on the complete playlist spotify:playlist:2IYRLjcwf2X9vC0QrI0b0P See you next time!
usable_records.author: osornios

usable_records.text: AI Music Transparency — A Subscribers Demand for Honesty I am writing this as a loyal, paying subscriber — someone who genuinely loves music and who has chosen to invest in your platform because I believed it was a place where real, human artistry could be discovered, celebrated, and supported. That trust is being eroded. And I want you to know why. I love soul music. I love the rawness of it, the pain in it, the joy in it — the fact that a real human being poured something of themselves into every note. Music, for me, has always been about connection. When I listen to an artist, I am connecting with another person's experience, their struggles, their love, their humanity. That relationship between listener and artist is sacred, and it is the very foundation of what makes music matter. AI-generated music is now flooding your platform — and you are letting it happen without a single word of warning to your subscribers. I have found myself listening to tracks, feeling moved, and later discovering they were produced entirely by AI via text prompts. No musician. No vocalist. No human experience behind the sound. Just an algorithm mimicking the appearance of feeling. I want to be absolutely clear: I am not here to tell people they cannot create or enjoy AI music. That is not my argument. But I have a fundamental right — as a paying customer — to know what I am listening to. Right now, that right is being denied to me. When I stream what appears to be a soul track, I deserve to know whether a real person bled into that recording or whether it was generated in seconds by a machine. The fact that I cannot tell the difference is not a testament to how good AI has become — it is a testament to how dishonest the current situation is. AI soul music might sound convincing, but it is, by its very nature, soulless. It is a imitation of human emotion without the human behind it. And discovering that I have been unknowingly listening to it feels like a betrayal. My subscription money is meant to support artists. Real artists. Human beings who have dedicated their lives to their craft. When AI-generated content is mixed indiscriminately into my feed, my recommendations, and my playlists with no labelling whatsoever, I have no way of ensuring that my money is going where I intend it to go. That is not acceptable. Here is what I am asking for, and I do not think any of it is unreasonable: — Every AI-generated track must be clearly and prominently labelled as such, before I click play. — AI music should exist in its own dedicated section of the platform, clearly signposted, so that listeners who wish to explore it can do so knowingly and willingly. — I should have a clear, accessible setting that allows me to exclude AI-generated content from my feed, my recommendations, and my playlists entirely. — AI-generated content should never be mixed into genre categories alongside human-made music without explicit disclosure. These are not radical demands. They are the bare minimum of honesty that any platform owes its users. If Spotify is unwilling or unable to implement these changes, then I must sincerely question whether my subscription is something I can continue in good conscience. I am paying for access to music — real music — and if I cannot trust that what I am hearing has been made by a human being unless I am specifically told otherwise, then I am not getting what I am paying for. In that case, I would expect a refund for the period during which I have been unknowingly exposed to unlabelled AI content. I urge you to take this seriously. I am almost certainly not alone in feeling this way. Music lovers everywhere are beginning to ask the same questions, and the platform that steps up and leads on transparency will earn enormous loyalty as a result. The platform that ignores this issue risks losing the trust of the very people who made it great. Please do right by your subscribers. Please do right by artists. And please do right by real music. DJ Chris Anthony
usable_records.author: DJChrisAnthony

AI-generated music is now flooding your platform — and you are letting it happen without a single word of warning to your subscribers. I have found myself listening to tracks, feeling moved, and later discovering they were produced entirely by AI via text prompts. No musician. No vocalist. No human experience behind the sound. Just an algorithm mimicking the appearance of feeling. I want to be absolutely clear: I am not here to tell people they cannot create or enjoy AI music. That is not my argument. But I have a fundamental right — as a paying customer — to know what I am listening to. Right now, that right is being denied to me. When I stream what appears to be a soul track, I deserve to know whether a real person bled into that recording or whether it was generated in seconds by a machine. The fact that I cannot tell the difference is not a testament to how good AI has become — it is a testament to how dishonest the current situation is. AI soul music might sound convincing, but it is, by its very nature, soulless. It is a imitation of human emotion without the human behind it. And discovering that I have been unknowingly listening to it feels like a betrayal. My subscription money is meant to support artists. Real artists. Human beings who have dedicated their lives to their craft. When AI-generated content is mixed indiscriminately into my feed, my recommendations, and my playlists with no labelling whatsoever, I have no way of ensuring that my money is going where I intend it to go. That is not acceptable. Here is what I am asking for, and I do not think any of it is unreasonable: — Every AI-generated track must be clearly and prominently labelled as such, before I click play. — AI music should exist in its own dedicated section of the platform, clearly signposted, so that listeners who wish to explore it can do so knowingly and willingly. — I should have a clear, accessible setting that allows me to exclude AI-generated content from my feed, my recommendations, and my playlists entirely. — AI-generated content should never be mixed into genre categories alongside human-made music without explicit disclosure. These are not radical demands. They are the bare minimum of honesty that any platform owes its users. If Spotify is unwilling or unable to implement these changes, then I must sincerely question whether my subscription is something I can continue in good conscience. I am paying for access to music — real music — and if I cannot trust that what I am hearing has been made by a human being unless I am specifically told otherwise, then I am not getting what I am paying for. In that case, I would expect a refund for the period during which I have been unknowingly exposed to unlabelled AI content. I urge you to take this seriously. I am almost certainly not alone in feeling this way. Music lovers everywhere are beginning to ask the same questions, and the platform that steps up and leads on transparency will earn enormous loyalty as a result. The platform that ignores this issue risks losing the trust of the very people who made it great. Please do right by your subscribers. Please do right by artists. And please do right by real music. DJ Chris Anthony

usable_records.text: Search. - Live Events - MacOS/iOS Dear Spotify Support, The Live Events feature was a great idea, central place to consolidate a lot of music events, etc. Ever since this feature was introduced, I find myself constantly looking for new events. Previously, searching through new events, was no issue, as I kept getting recommendations relatively close to my music taste and/or based on categories I play ed . However, lately I noticed that a TON OF new events have been uploaded, and trying to find an event for an artist I know or would recognise, has become extremely difficult, I find myself now spending a lot more time just going through the HUGE LISTS of events trying to find something, than actually listening to music on Spotify. I also found the user experience for Live Events on MacOS as well as iOS is not as "cool" as it could be and I will point out some of the things that perhaps could be improved. 1. on MacOS, I get 3 Sections for Live Events : A> Discover Near You. B> Recommended for You. C> All. However, when I click on A> "Show All", there is no option to Sort by "Date". Additionally, m aybe interchange the Title and Artist Columns . On B> There is no option to View as “Compact List”, and on C> Since the List so HUGE, here the View as “Compact List” would be very helpful. But actually, I don’t know why I get the whole list, this section could be better synchronized to only show artists based on one’s music taste, what one plays or has played on Spotify, perhaps that’s what B> was meant to be, but even there, I also get some artists I would never play. 2. on iOS, similar to the above, except I get a few more sections, but ideally the same issues. A> Just for You>Show all> View as “Compact List” and Sort by “Date” would be helpful. B> Popular in [Your City]>Show all> Here the Events are not even sorted by Date. Sorting by Date and Compact List would really make people’s lives easier. C> same as in MacOS. Artists maybe pay more to be on top of the list “similar to GoogleAds”, and that’s why some artists show up at the very top more than others, but I just thought I share this with Developers, perhaps one day they find time to modify the Queries. I myself am a SysAdmin and work with DevOps, so I know how difficult it is to implement even small App changes. Thanks anyways for the support! BG
usable_records.author: kathringrün

usable_records.text: unusual account activity and listening history Yesterday, I was unexpectedly asked to log back into my account. At the time, I didn’t think much of it. However, today I noticed that an artist I have never listened to — Fuerza Regida — suddenly appeared in my top listening artists. I immediately checked my “Recently Played” and “Top Songs of the Month,” and found a large number of tracks that I have never listened to. The playback patterns are extremely abnormal and clearly resemble automated bot behavior — for example: ABABABAB, CDCDCDCD, EFEFEFEF loops. (the tracks are Past Struggles- Nune Aka Mr. Propane ICE CREAM - ZR Southern Lights - BigDaddyRai LES MECS - Eva Sintiendo - Castillo La Cone EXPLICIT 69 - Timar) This is very distressing because I have used this account for many years, and I value my personalized music library. Seeing these fraudulent records contaminated my history is quite frustrating. What concerns me further: I received no email notification about any suspicious login or new device access. This is not the first time this has happened — I experienced the exact same issue around 2018–2019, after which I logged out of all devices and set a strong password. Despite that, this has happened again years later, which raises serious concerns about platform security, cause I've never had a similar issue an ANY other content consuming platforms. It’s so stressful knowing this could happen again at any time. I would appreciate your help with the following: Is it possible for your technical team to help me remove or reset these specific bot-generated records? I really don't want these tracks to ruin my future recommendations or my annual summary. I didn't receive any notification about a new login. Could you please help me check if there are any suspicious authorized apps or unrecognized devices that I should be aware of? I want to make sure this doesn't happen again. I am concerned that the system might mistake me for a "bot" because of this activity. Can you confirm that my account status is safe and won't be penalized? If this cannot be resolved properly, I may have to reconsider continuing to use Spotify. Thank you for your attention to this matter. I look forward to your response. Best Regards Plan Premium Country Japan Device Macbook Air, M3, 2024 Operating System Mac OS Sonoma 14.3
usable_records.author: Sky2D

usable_records.text: Re: February 2026 Spotify for Developers update: thread @jsneij wrote: Request for endpoint access review — single-user personal case (Client ID eb476afb75a04275babd1ebfaec1a1b8) Hi Spotify team, Posting here following the February 2026 update. I'd like to request a case-by-case review of endpoint access for my Development Mode app "Claude Code ProjectSpotify". My case in one paragraph: I'm a single individual using the API exclusively against my own Premium account (jsneij, Premium since 2014) to manage my own library — dedupe playlists by ISRC, merge themed collections, batch-rename, archive old ones. The Spotify client supports all of this manually, but at my scale (142 playlists, ~2,400 saved tracks) it becomes prohibitive. The app runs locally on my laptop. No third-party users, no public service, no monetisation, no commercial path. User Management is 0/5 because there's no one else to add. Why I'm posting: The February 2026 post explicitly states Development Mode is meant to support "learning, experimentation, and personal projects for non-commercial use by individual developers." That's exactly my profile. However, the restricted endpoint set for newly-created Client IDs returns 403 on the core read/write playlist endpoints I need, which makes basic personal-account hygiene impossible. The March 9 update postponed these restrictions for existing integrations after community feedback — I'd appreciate that same spirit being applied to a single-user personal case. Specific endpoints requested: GET /v1/playlists/{id} (with tracks.items) GET /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks POST/PUT/DELETE on /v1/playlists/{id}/tracks Not asking for audio-features, audio-analysis, recommendations, or any November 2024 deprecated endpoints. Constraints I'd accept: Lock User Management to my single account, signed attestation, periodic review, read-only access only — whatever scoping works for Spotify. Even read-only on my own playlists' tracks would cover most of the use case. Happy to provide any additional context. Thanks for considering this! Jorge I agree with you. We also believe the personal use-cases should get a lot more freedom. There are security and legal hoops to jump through - but our ambition is to fully support your ability to manage your Spotify.
usable_records.author: LambertSpot

usable_records.text: Re: Fire TV now playing does not match song playing or lyrics Thanks for the response @plushkrazee and it's great to hear that you've managed to find a workaround for the playlist order issue. To help clear up any confusion, here are some answers to your other questions: The pinning option within Spotify lets you keep your most-used content at the top of your library for quick access and you can pin up to 4 items (playlists, albums, or artists). The reason why you're seeing songs in your playlists that are not in Your Library could be because of the Smart Shuffle function that keeps your listening sessions fresh by mixing in recommendations that match the vibe. This button - indicates it's turned On and by clicking on it you can disable it. More info you can also find in this article . Spotify typically displays the Top 5 Popular songs on an artist's profile on desktop and mobile, but on the desktop app, there should be a "See more" button below these songs in order to expand the list to see the Top 10. If you were referring to something else, let us know and we'll try to assist in the best possible way. When it comes to the search engine for your library and playlists not working well, consider performing a clean reinstall with the steps here and if you're still experiencing issues, it'd be very helpful if you could share as many details as possible of the exact behavior. Since the workaround with Sirius temporarily fixes the playback issue within Spotify, it could indicate an issue with how your device handles audio focus between apps. This is also something that could be fixed with a clean reinstall as suggested above, because it also contains the steps of clearing the cache (when corrupted it can cause different performance issues). Lastly, whenever it's possible try with restarting the router as suggested and let us know if that makes a difference. Hopefully your questions have been answered, but we remain a post away if there's anything else you're wondering about. Keep us posted 🙂
usable_records.author: Ivelina

usable_records.text: Proposal for a New Mood-Based Music Discovery IDEA Hello, My name is Jin-young Choi, and I majored in Mechanical Engineering in Korea. From a young age, I have been closely connected to music, playing the piano and clarinet as a hobby. Later, I also played the keyboard in a band with my friends. I am currently developing an application based on a new music discovery idea that is not provided by existing music platforms. I designed the UI using Figma and am developing the application with Flutter, enabling detailed feature implementation and modifications. The problem I have identified in existing music platforms is that it is difficult for users to select music that matches their specific mood and preferences. Personally, I enjoy listening to hip-hop and fast-tempo music with melodies, and music has always been a part of my daily life. However, while using various platforms, I often found it inconvenient that songs matching the mood I wanted and songs that did not match were recommended together within the same hip-hop genre. To address this limitation, I am developing a music platform. The goal of this platform is to help users find the music they want more accurately, not by simply classifying music by genre, but by categorizing songs based on mood and detailed musical characteristics. By applying the classification method I have developed, users would be able to adjust mood-based recommendations, such as those currently provided by platforms like Spotify, according to the specific range they want to listen to. This would allow users to explore music more precisely. In addition, the diverse music preference data accumulated through this platform could be expanded into a future-oriented revenue model connected to the music industry. Furthermore, by using user preference data, this platform could help predict directions for music production and support more proactive music planning and creation. Based on this idea, I would like to propose new possibilities to music production professionals and gain an opportunity to develop my concept into a real service and industry application.
usable_records.author: jjjinyoung0511

usable_records.text: Artist and Genre Algorithm needs improvement Dear Spotify Team, I have been a Spotify Premium subscriber for many years and have always appreciated the platform's ability to help me discover new music and artists. Unfortunately, over the past several months, I've noticed a significant decline in that experience. Many of the playlists and recommendations being presented to me now feel repetitive and stale. Different playlists often contain many of the same songs, resulting in very little actual music discovery. Instead of being introduced to new artists or tracks, I find myself hearing the same selections over and over again. Additionally, the genres and artists being recommended increasingly do not align with my listening habits. It feels as though the algorithm has become less accurate and less personalized than it once was. Rather than helping me explore new music within the genres I enjoy, it often suggests music that has little relevance to my tastes. One of the primary reasons I subscribe to a music streaming service is to discover new music. Lately, that aspect of Spotify feels largely absent, and the overall listening experience has become predictable and, frankly, boring. At the same time, subscription prices continue to increase. As a paying customer, I expect ongoing improvements to the service, particularly in areas like personalization and music discovery. Instead, I feel the experience has moved in the opposite direction. I wanted to provide this feedback because I genuinely enjoy Spotify and would prefer to remain a customer. However, if the recommendation and discovery experience continues to decline, I will seriously consider exploring alternative streaming platforms that do a better job of introducing listeners to new music. Thank you for taking the time to read my feedback. I hope it can be shared with the teams responsible for recommendations, playlist curation, and user experience.
usable_records.author: mbotic1

usable_records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs This situation of AI-generated content flooding spotify, playlists and infecting the recommendation algorithms is getting ridiculous and completely out of control. We pay spotify premium to listen to real human‘s music, not AI slop that’s shuffled and sneaked into our (once great) recommendations or Daily-Mixes, that’s not even labeled as such. Thus is poses a major and user-unfriendly extra effort to manually filter and block out those sloppy ghostbands, which creators are just trying to profit off of it while overshadowing and driving away real music content. To be clear, I don’t consider ALL AI content as bad „music“ - some is actually good and impressive, but it MUST be transparent and be a choice to opt-in. The thing is: you expect to listen to real bands’ music and in many cases you can’t even recognize it anymore as AI, creating a feeling of betrayal when you realize it and a general distrust. There‘s no label for AI content and even typical intuitive indicators don’t work reliably anymore, as many of these ghostband creators try hard to conceal the truth it being AI slop by impersonating bands/interprets and adding plausible/believable band descriptions, images etc. This also damages the reputation and reduces the chances of newer or lesser known real interprets, as they all are now viewed under critical general suspicion with the flood of AI slop. I used to love Spotifys recommendation features to experience new similar music that fits my taste and to broaden my music/genre diversity. But the inclusion and integration of AI slop into Spotify has completely ruined this key feature for me. This situation needs to be addressed asap by Spotify. Act now or lose your customers and reputation.
usable_records.author: Musicify

metadata.search_snippet: This situation of AI-generated content flooding spotify, playlists and infecting the recommendation algorithms is getting ridiculous and completely out of control. We pay spotify premium to listen to real human‘s music, not AI slop that’s shuffled and sneaked into our (once great) recommendations or Daily-Mixes, that’s not even labeled as such. Thus is poses a major and user-unfriendly extra effort to manually filter and block out those sloppy ghostbands, which creators are just trying to profit off of it while overshadowing and driving away real music content. To be clear, I don’t consider ALL AI content as bad „music“ - some is actually good and impressive, but it MUST be transparent and be a choice to opt-in. The thing is: you expect to listen to real bands’ music and in many cases you can’t even recognize it anymore as AI, creating a feeling of betrayal when you realize it and a general distrust. There‘s no label for AI content and even typical intuitive indicators don’t work reliably anymore, as many of these ghostband creators try hard to conceal the truth it being AI slop by impersonating bands/interprets and adding plausible/believable band descriptions, images etc. This also damages the reputation and reduces the chances of newer or lesser known real interprets, as they all are now viewed under critical general suspicion with the flood of AI slop. I used to love Spotifys recommendation features to experience new similar music that fits my taste and to broaden my music/genre diversity. But the inclusion and integration of AI slop into Spotify has completely ruined this key feature for me. This situation needs to be addressed asap by Spotify. Act now or lose your customers and reputation.

usable_records.text: Re: Shuffle mode playing the same songs all the time The issue with Spotify's random shuffle seems to be related to it's recommendations and listening history. As long as it uses this, it could never be truly random. Using the Like system and liking any songs will absolutely ruin random shuffle for instance. The fewer repeat feature accomplishes nothing as it still cycles through the same songs on reset. You can try training the algorithm by clicking individual songs, but it is very slow at adapting. That is not all because it prioritizes songs based on their own recommendation algorithm. Some newly added songs to the playlist will mysteriously play right away when some others never seem to show up. The issue isn't fewer repeats and whatever "more human" is supposed to mean here. You need to simply not weigh in listening history and your own recommendation algorithm in random shuffle and offer "true random". I created a new small playlist and hitting play resulted in playing the exact same song as the very first. That is obviously not random when the first song is the exact same one. How about a simple playtest where the first song being played is actually random? Fewer repeats only after the first set of the same 100 songs is not what we want. Allow the option to not use listening history and your own damn recommendations. It has nothing to do with device limits and smaller chunks of data. It has to do with you forcing and weighing certain songs over others. Just like your limited selection of recommended tracks offering the same group of artists. Let us opt-out of your tracking years of our listening history. I think you know exactly what the problem is, but refuse to offer a true solution.
usable_records.author: px7c0e9q-f_m

usable_records.text: Switching between Family accounts (in browser, macOS, etc.) Sorry, incoming rant: On our Family Premium account, I have created a new account for my son. I can switch back and forth between these accounts on my iPhone in the Spotify app by clicking on my profile and selecting his account. Switching back is secured by a PIN. This feature does not seem to be available on ANY other platform. It's not available in the browser, in the macOS app, or in my car's Android-based app. This effectively means, that I can use my son's "account" only on my iPhone . (I cannot "log out and back in" on other devices because my son's account is not a separate account with email/pw, it's just been created from within the iPhone app as another Family account) I am not sure if Spotify understands their Family users: my playlist is currently a mix of my favourite music and children's songs. I would like to keep this separated so Spotify can give me good recommendations (which is of course messed up by the children stuff). Please don't recommend the Spotify Kids app - it has well-deserved 2 out of 5 stars on the App Store, it's basically useless. By when will it be possible to switch easily between accounts on all devices? I am not sure why Spotify thinks the current way is a good solution. All (!) other streaming services (music, video, etc.) make this possible. If you are afraid that you will make less money (wouldn't know why, but I am sure that's the reason), then at least consider introducing different "profiles" so separate listeners can create their own listening profile. I am considering cancelling Spotify, as are many of my friends, due to the completely messed up account management in Spotify.
usable_records.author: qntmz

usable_records.text: Allow exact track selection when creating playlists via AI / third-party integrations Spotify AI Playlist via Claude integration ignores explicit track requests. When using the Spotify MCP integration through Claude (claude.ai), the playlist creation tool does not honour specific track requests, even when tracks are listed by exact title and artist, numbered in order, with explicit instructions to make no substitutions. I tested this multiple times with increasingly specific prompts. Each time, Spotify's AI generated a thematically related but entirely different playlist, ignoring the requested tracks completely. Expected behaviour: When a user provides an explicit, ordered tracklist, the resulting playlist should contain those tracks in that order. Actual behaviour: The AI interprets the prompt thematically and substitutes its own track selection regardless of explicit instructions. Steps to reproduce: 1. Open Claude (claude.ai) 2. Ask it to create a Spotify playlist and provide a specific numbered tracklist 3. The generated playlist will not contain the requested tracks This makes the tool useful only for vague mood-based requests, not for users who want to build a specific playlist with AI assistance. A prompt override or "exact tracks" mode would fix this entirely. Additional frustration: The reason I come to Claude for playlist help is precisely to get specific, curated recommendations, not the same generic AI-generated suggestions Spotify already produces on its own. When Claude then hands off to Spotify's playlist AI, the result is doubly generic. The integration completely undermines the value of asking Claude in the first place. Users should be able to take Claude's specific track curation and have it land in Spotify exactly as intended, including the ability to search for individual tracks by title and artist, and add them to a playlist in a defined order.
usable_records.author: nikramon

usable_records.text: Making the "Recommended Songs" Algorithm Courageous Again Hi Spotify Team, I’m reaching out regarding my experience with Spotify recommendations so that you guys can work around it. I’ve noticed something annoying as a user over the years: back around 2019, Spotify used to give me much better variety in terms of different authors and songs I had never heard before. Nowadays, the user experience feels more like a catalog of things I’ve already listened to and just want to remember. I think you guys could change the algorithm to make it a little bit more courageous . As a user, I think most people would not complain about a song being a little bit "out" of our usual favourite artists or songs if it means discovering a new artist or a different sound. That discovery makes everything feel more magic , and it compensates for the "noise" of occasionally hearing a song we aren't used to. I’m not saying 2019 was the perfect standard, but the 2026 version feels much less adventurous. Please don't just aim to replicate 2019, it wasn't the best either, but it was definitely better. Aim for something new, dynamic, and alive, rather than something static, safe and boring . In fewer lines: The recommendations at the bottom of playlists are static and feel "stuck." They only seem to reflect the first five songs I add and never evolve as the playlist grows. I want to suggest this section to stay alive and be more courageous refreshing constantly to bring me new discoveries instead of staying the same forever. And yes, at the bottom of playlists, because playlists follow contexts like genres, moods, etc., and not only in a separate playlist with mixed discoveries only.
usable_records.author: PauloDrWho

usable_records.text: Re: Question of the Week: Where did your music taste begin and where is it now? Nice question! It was around late 2018 or so when my current preference for heavy psychedelic and stoner rock (as well as related genres like space rock and doom metal) started to take form, in part due to the recommendations shown on the Spotify app. Before then, my music listening habits varied quite a lot, representing genres such as jazz, progressive rock, reggae, old-school metal, Indian classical music, and US-based jam bands, among others. However, around that time and moving towards the present, psychedelic- and stoner rock became my favorite genres of music, and that’s since remained the case. It is not easy for me to remember certain favorite songs that I used to listen to a lot during the years before 2019, since I only created a Last.fm account to keep track of my listening habits around that time, and my "Your Top Songs" playlists for 2016, 2017, and 2018 are somewhat inaccurate (probably because someone had accessed my Spotify account during those years). However, off the top of my head, here are some of my favorite songs from these earlier years: spotify:track:0fzFXOXI6lV1wjolSr4OSU:small spotify:track:0r9ZSCNXKinpOBUl943m3l:small spotify:track:2ytXq1bTu0ewU7hB7GngpK:small spotify:track:48Qs11WwUFNM8BPunM9Xsu:small spotify:track:1NXypJl06i4aiD6Lpi06Sb:small spotify:track:5HO9dFj88plFIHkJJBG7hU:small And as a representation of my current music taste, I’ll share some songs from my "On Repeat" playlist that Spotify updated just two days ago: spotify:track:2qIv3Zylpfdj61H2W6oBxX:small spotify:track:5Y8pL0WdfHiT1ys4ksowfY:small spotify:track:6ceqNG5aOCKcc76sP4dp1S:small spotify:track:4yKxwphYbUraqvMsGWqI2j:small spotify:track:0lskW1AMZu0oqqMNENsIiV:small spotify:track:6y4TahhGBzkynKoJ2WCWfF:small

usable_records.text: Artificial Intelligence Labeling Dear Spotify-Community, I have been a Spotify user for quite some time and really value discovering music by genuine, creative artists. However, over the last months I keep running into more and more tracks that feel strange somehow, only to discover that they were created by artificial intelligence artists. In my opinion, Spotify should require artists and labels to clearly mark all tracks that contain AI-generated elements, so that listeners can immediately recognize them. Spotify should also offer user settings that allow people to turn off recommendations for AI-produced songs entirely if they wish. To make these rules effective, Spotify should then introduce consequences for artists or distributors who intentionally mislabel or fail to disclose AI use, for example by limiting their visibility, removing offending tracks, or suspending their ability to upload or canceling their payment. In my opinion this would protect genuine artists, who put much work and time into producing their songs and also into practising music for years to become professionals. It would also give listeners the chance to control, if they would like to listen to AI generated songs or not. From my point of view, if this topic is not adressed by spotify, it could lead to real problems in the future, destroying the creativity, passion and genius of music artists and producers. If the creative work of real artists is not valued over quick, shallow produced, low-effort AI content, we will see less and less real artists, motivated and able to sustain a serious music career. If you feel the same way about this topic, please support my idea by giving it a vote!!
usable_records.author: paul150

usable_records.text: Re: Wont load/Something went wrong I am experiencing the exact same issue on my device. I have already tried every solution mentioned in this thread, but unfortunately none of them worked. My device is an Honor X7b running Android 14 . I previously had the beta version of Spotify installed, but I completely removed it and installed the official stable version instead (9.1.44.2120) . The strange thing is that I can successfully log into any Spotify account without issues. However, all accounts experience the same problem afterward. For comparison, Spotify works perfectly on my Samsung Galaxy Note 20 5G using the same accounts and network connection. On the Honor phone, the app appears to have internet access because I can see my playlists and saved content. Most strangely, I can still access the Search tab, and it loads playlists, recommendations, and stories — just not the actual music content that used to appear before. However: Opening playlists shows no songs inside Even my own created playlists appear empty, although I can still add songs via Search Search results return “Song not found” Stories and previews also fail to load properly The Premium tab is accessible as well. So it seems the app can partially connect to Spotify’s servers, but it fails to properly load music content — exactly as others described in this topic. I have already tried the following troubleshooting steps: Reinstalling the app (both standard and clean reinstall) Logging out and back in Testing with multiple Spotify accounts Clearing cache and app data Resetting network settings Force restarting the device after each step Unfortunately, none of these solutions resolved the issue.
usable_records.author: Sachi_xCh
