usable_records.text: Spotify Smart Shuffle always generates the same track order Plan Premium Duo Country Croatia Device Windows PC Samsung Galaxy S23+ Operating System Windows 11 Pro (version 25H2) Android 16 (kernel 5.15.178) My Question or Issue The Smart Shuffle feature always produces the exact same track order every time I activate it. The order is different from the default playlist order, but it never changes between Smart Shuffle activations. For example, when I enable Smart Shuffle on a playlist, Spotify rearranges the songs and adds recommendations as expected. However, if I turn Smart Shuffle off and then enable it again later, the playlist appears in the exact same shuffled order as before. It does not generate a new random order. By contrast, the normal Shuffle function behaves correctly: each time I enable regular Shuffle, the playlist order changes. The behaviour is identical on Windows and Android apps. What I expected to happen: Each time I activate Smart Shuffle, Spotify should generate a new shuffled order (similar to normal Shuffle), possibly with different recommended tracks. What actually happens: Smart Shuffle always uses the same fixed order every time it is activated. Steps to reproduce: Open Spotify and start a playlist. Enable Smart Shuffle. Note the track order generated by Smart Shuffle. Turn Smart Shuffle off. Enable Smart Shuffle again. Observe that the playlist order is exactly the same as the previous Smart Shuffle order. Troubleshooting already attempted: Tested on both desktop and Android apps Confirmed that normal Shuffle generates a different order each time Restarted the apps and repeated the test The issue persists across devices.
usable_records.author: disposabledominik

usable_records.text: A Miracle Found in a Forest of Over 100 Million Songs Sorry for my English. I’m a Japanese user, but I wanted to share my experience. If you want to enjoy not only listening to your favorite music, but also discovering new music, I truly recommend trying Spotify. At first, I found Spotify’s features—such as automatically inserting recommended songs or creating personalized playlists—to be a bit intrusive and unnecessary. However, I realized something that completely changed my perspective. There is a physical limit to how many songs a person can listen to in a lifetime. Even if we assume a generous pace of 50 new songs every day, from age 10 to the average Japanese lifespan of about 84 years, we would still only be able to hear about 1.3% of Spotify’s library of over 100 million songs. Once I understood this, the way I enjoy music changed. Listening repeatedly to music I already love is comforting, of course. But by opening myself to Spotify’s recommendations, I began to encounter unexpected discoveries—songs I would never have found on my own. It feels similar to how a newborn child hears nothing but new sounds for the first time. Spotify somehow brings me back to that sense of curiosity and innocence, as if I were exploring a vast, untouched forest of music. For anyone who truly loves music, this is a service I hope you will experience. Today again, in my car, I’m looking forward to enjoying another one-in-a-hundred-million miracle.
usable_records.author: MusicForestExplorer

usable_records.text: Re: What if Spotify just knew — the moment you opened it? Spotify has an AI curated playlist feature in beta right now where you can type in a prompt, but it never works that well and glitches out when you try adding or removing songs then enter a new prompt. I don’t think it understands “moods” because it often gives completely different music than what I want it to and even some random songs in another language that sound nothing like what I prompted. Spotify has also made it clear that it will add sponsored music into your recommendations, so those random songs might be related to that. They also tried out the AI feature in last year’s wrapped and it went horribly, people were very displeased. Knowing how poorly this feature works, along with the environmental impact due to the immense amount of resources needed, if Spotify were to implement even more AI into the streaming service I know a huge population of its users would opt for its removal. It sounds like a nice idea if you don’t want to put in the work to find your own songs, but in the long run I can see it frustrating you more when they throw in random sponsored songs that have nothing to do with what you want. In all honesty, if you want songs with certain sounds, try testing out Spotalike or even the AI Playlist feature. If those don’t work out I highly doubt built in AI would be any better.
usable_records.author: criscantspell

Spotify has also made it clear that it will add sponsored music into your recommendations, so those random songs might be related to that. They also tried out the AI feature in last year’s wrapped and it went horribly, people were very displeased. Knowing how poorly this feature works, along with the environmental impact due to the immense amount of resources needed, if Spotify were to implement even more AI into the streaming service I know a huge population of its users would opt for its removal. It sounds like a nice idea if you don’t want to put in the work to find your own songs, but in the long run I can see it frustrating you more when they throw in random sponsored songs that have nothing to do with what you want. In all honesty, if you want songs with certain sounds, try testing out Spotalike or even the AI Playlist feature. If those don’t work out I highly doubt built in AI would be any better.

usable_records.text: Re: Playback issues: Songs going silent on Android app This happens to me literally every single time I try to listen using a wired connection and firmly believe that if Samsung had kept an aux port, this wouldnt be an issue. The day, time and time zone this has last occurred on: moat recently, 10+ times today alone, 5/25/26 between 7am-12pm PST. Your device's OS version: Android The version of Spotify you're running: 9.1.50.1906 Does the app switch to the next song or is it stuck on the current one (with the timer still counting)? The app never skips songs or pauses them when this happens - Spoitfy continues playing the song but there is no audio output. I usually have to unplug my connection twice to get things working again. Whether this occurs when playing from the mobile device directly or when connected to another device (Bluetooth, headphones, etc.): this happened exclusively when trying to listen through a wired audio connection. It seems like this happens every time my phone drops from 5G to 4G. I have set my Spotify app to listen offline and use downloaded playlists and it still happens. Have done all of the troubleshooting recommendations with no solutions. I can get music playing again after reconnecting the wired connection, but it might stop again seconds later. The ONLY solution for me has been to listen on airplane mode, which sort of defeats the purpose of being able to listen on my phone.

usable_records.text: design changes Hey Spotify team, I’ve been a loyal user for many years and truly love the service — the recommendations, playlists, audio quality, and overall experience are still the best out there. But if I’m being honest, the app’s visual design has started to feel really dated and hasn’t had a meaningful refresh in quite a while. The interface now looks noticeably older compared to many other modern music apps (and even compared to some of Spotify’s own marketing visuals). It desperately needs to become more contemporary, clean, and aesthetically pleasing — something more minimalistic , with better use of space, refined typography, smoother animations, and a truly premium feel. For the iOS version in particular, it would be amazing if you could finally adopt Apple’s new Liquid Glass design language from iOS 26. That translucent, dynamic, glass-like material with subtle depth and light refraction looks incredible in so many apps right now — it would pair beautifully with album art, vibrant colors, and the emotional side of music. Several designers have already shared really stunning Spotify concepts using Liquid Glass, and they feel so much more immersive and fresh. Right now the app just feels visually “stuck” while everything else around it (including the OS itself) has moved forward. A thoughtful redesign wouldn’t just be cosmetic — it would make the whole experience feel exciting and premium again. Please consider giving the UI some serious love in the coming updates. Many of us would really appreciate it.
usable_records.author: alexsptf

usable_records.text: Liked Songs shuffle starts playing recommendations after locking iPhone Plan Premium Country South Korea Device iPhone16pro Operating System iOS26.2.1 My Question or Issue Hello, I primarily listen using Liked Songs with Shuffle enabled and set to reduce repetition. My expectation is that playback continues only within my Liked Songs in random order. However I am seeing different behavior depending on how playback is started. When I open Liked Songs, scroll through the list, select a track, and let it continue playing, the playback continues randomly within Liked Songs only. This is the behavior I expect. When I open Liked Songs, use the search bar at the top to find a specific song, select that song, and keep the app open, the playback also continues randomly within Liked Songs only. This is also correct. The issue happens when I open Liked Songs, use the search bar to find a specific song, select that song, and then immediately send the Spotify app to the background and lock the iPhone. After the selected song ends, it plays one or two songs from Liked Songs, then suddenly switches to recommended tracks that are not in my Liked Songs. I would like to understand why playback starts injecting recommended songs only in this situation. I want playback to remain strictly within Liked Songs regardless of whether I searched for the track or locked the device. I am using an iPhone with the latest iOS version and the Spotify app is updated to the latest version.
usable_records.author: gantodagee

usable_records.text: Re: How to get rid of recommendations of r*ssian music? Hi there! To reduce or stop recommendations of Russian and Belarusian music on Spotify, you can try the following: Use the "Hide" or "Don't play this artist" option: When you see a Russian or Belarusian artist or song recommended, select the three-dot menu next to it and choose “Don’t play this artist.” This signals Spotify’s algorithm to avoid similar content. Like and listen more to your preferred artists and genres: The more you listen to Ukrainian, English, Japanese, Taiwanese, Estonian, and Lithuanian music and actively like those songs and artists, the more Spotify’s recommendations will reflect your taste. Create playlists with only your preferred music: Spotify uses your playlists and saved music to suggest new songs. Building playlists focused on your favorite regions can help shift recommendations. Clear your Spotify cache and reset your recommendations: Sometimes clearing cache or even starting fresh (e.g., temporarily logging out or reinstalling the app) helps reset the algorithm’s assumptions. Use private sessions when listening to music you don’t want influencing recommendations: If you listen to something outside your usual preferences, do it in a private session so it doesn’t affect your recommendations. Block or remove disliked artists from your library: Since you’re already blocking Russian artists, continue doing this actively. While Spotify’s algorithm isn’t perfect and may take time to adjust, consistent use of these methods should reduce unwanted recommendations. Hope this helps!
usable_records.author: Koyubi

usable_records.text: Feature Request: Direct Track-Add API for Third Party Playlist Management I use Claude (Anthropic's AI assistant) integrated with Spotify to get personalized music recommendations and create playlists. The experience highlights a significant limitation in the current integration: all playlist creation is routed through Spotify's AI playlist generator, which produces unreliable results. The problems with the current AI-based approach: • Hits token limits and fails silently, resulting in playlists that don't appear in my library • Substitutes incorrect tracks when it can't find exact matches, with no notification • Generates generic results that don't reflect the specific tracks being requested • Slow and resource-intensive compared to a simple database operation The irony is that I get significantly better music recommendations from Claude than from Spotify's AI playlist feature. But when it comes to actually building the playlist, we're forced to route through a less capable AI layer that introduces errors and failures. The fix is straightforward: a direct track-add API endpoint that accepts track IDs and adds them to a playlist. Licensing and content controls are already solved at the track metadata level and don't require AI interpretation. This would be faster, cheaper for Spotify to run, more reliable for users, and produce better results than the current AI middleman. This isn't a request to remove AI playlist features — keep them for users who want them. This is a request for a reliable programmatic alternative that lets third party integrations like Claude deliver their full value to Spotify users.
usable_records.author: MarkDanielW

usable_records.text: Re: Desktop: New Your Library sidebar My time at Spotify is over. It's no longer just the UI/UX, which is chaotic. Right now on tablet it's terrible. I think the only app that has improved is the one for the TV. My problem is EVERYTHING. The Podcast recommendations that I don't want to hear, the constant changing of the recommendation carousel. It's almost worse than Instagram, you can't focus on what's important: LISTENING TO MUSIC. They don't want you to listen to your music and discover something new every once in a while, they want you to waste as much time as possible in their app. Sometimes I go in, I see an album that appeals to me from the recommendations but I don't save it. A few minutes later I go back in and they've changed, I no longer know which album that is. Favorites get lost, they get duplicated, you can access the same albums from different links. In one link your favorite songs from that album are marked, from another link to the same damn album there are no songs marked. If you mark one as a favorite that is already on the other album, they appear twice in the favorites list, but if you remove one, both are removed! Ultimately, I don't know why I'm wasting my time here. Nothing is going to change. In a couple of months I'm going to Qobuz.

usable_records.text: Re: Clear the queue Hey everyone! I'm not a spotify moderator, but hopefully this post will help. Essentially, what is happening is that you should see something that is under "Now playing" (the song you are currently listening to). After that, you might see either the phrase "Next in queue" or "Next up". To be completely clear, these are two different things. "Next in queue" is whatever song, playlist, album, podcast, etc that you clicked on and clicked "Add to queue" and is prioritized over "Next up", therefore if you simply add in your thing into the queue and suddenly want to move to a different thing without eventually returning to your original thing, you can click the "Clear queue" button to the right of "Next in queue" to clear it completely, then add into queue what you wish to listen to and hit play. If what you want to listen to is under the "Next up" section, then there is no need to add it to the queue. The "Next up" section is where all of your songs that you've listened to already start to accumulate, along with spotify recommendations if you have that on. Think of it as a playlist full of old/recommended music. There is no clear all button for the "Next up", but you can try to log out/close spotify, perhaps it might clear it automatically. Hope this helps!
usable_records.author: enhypen_fan

usable_records.text: Remove Certain Song Recommendations For Each Playlist In each playlist, I usually end up getting similar recommendations despite there being different vibes in each. I would like to go through the recommendations for a single playlist without getting the same songs over and over that I don't want to add - I blocked Mother Mother (despite still liking some of the songs) because that was ALL I was getting recommended in each playlist. This applies to a lot of other songs and artists too, and a lot of the time they're songs I've already worn out and I want new ones. I'd like there to be a way to remove a song recommendation from each playlist (without it removing the song from every playlist) and not get the recommendation again, instead getting new, fresher recommendations. I would like to unblock Mother Mother without it flooding my recommendations for each playlist, and instead have a "dislike" or "hide" kind of button for the recs. I know there is the playlist shuffle recommendations, and that's decent, but I seriously just get the same 10 songs and they come back even if I hit the dislike button. But, I like going through the "add" section at the top of the playlists and the "recommended songs" at the bottom, and would like an option to remove certain songs from those sections and have them NOT come back as recommendations.

usable_records.text: Loved Songs I think Spotify should have a second tier above regular liked songs called “Loved Songs.” A lot of people probably use likes the way you think — if you enjoy a song, you like it. But that also means it's easy to end up with a lot of liked songs that all get treated the same, even though there are certain tracks I'm sure we all care about way more than others. For example, I like every song from Hail The Sun but there are still a smaller number of songs that I come back to constantly and consider my personal favorites. Right now the only real way to separate those songs is by making a playlist. But that would mean creating a separate playlist for every artist whose discography I fully enjoy, just to isolate a handful of my absolute favorites. It would be cool if Spotify added: the current green checkmark for liked songs a second option, like a red heart, for “Loved Songs” Then users could: filter only loved songs from an artist shuffle only loved songs quickly revisit their favorite tracks without needing extra playlists I also think this could help Spotify recommendations since it gives a stronger signal about which songs users actually connect with the most, instead of treating every liked song equally.
usable_records.author: Zero011

Now I'm searching for alternatives.

usable_records.text: Re: ripoff Hey there @ripoff101 , Thanks for reaching out. I get that audiobook hours can feel limited if audiobooks are the main thing you're using Spotify for. Longer titles can use up the monthly listening time pretty quickly. That said, it's worth keeping in mind that Premium Family is primarily a music and audio plan for multiple people in the same household. The plan includes things like ad-free music listening, offline downloads, on-demand playback, multiple individual accounts, personalized recommendations, playlists, podcasts, and other Premium features for the family members on the plan. The audiobook listening time is more of an added benefit on top of the Premium plan, rather than the whole plan being priced only around audiobooks. For some people, the included hours are enough for occasional audiobook listening. For heavier audiobook listeners, extra hours or a separate audiobook option may make more sense depending on how much they listen each month. I definitely understand the feedback though. It would be great to see more flexible audiobook options in the future, especially for Family plan members who listen a lot. If audiobook value is the main concern, it's worth sharing that feedback in the Ideas section so other users can vote and add their thoughts too.
usable_records.author: JBayudz

usable_records.text: Re: Your Episodes, Remove finished episodes after playing doesn't work It's frustrating that Spotify hasn't communicated any progress on this issue. The way I use the Your Episodes playlist is to enable automatic downloads on my favourite shows so that new episodes are downloaded and added to Your Episodes alongside any episodes that I have added ad-hoc based on recommendations and search. I typically listen to this playlist via a Siri Shortcut (Hey Siri, in Spotify play Your Episodes), but because the playlist still contains episodes which haven't been automatically removed when finished, I end up restarting episodes I've already heard, unless I remember to open the app and manually remove each download. As partial workaround, I have ended up disabling the automatic downloads for all my shows. Instead I use a Python script found on github called "auto-spotify-podcast-playlist" which checks each of the shows I follow and adds any new episodes to the Your Episodes playlist. I have the script scheduled to run in the background a few times a day on my Mac. It's an extreme workaround but others might find it useful while we wait for Spotify to address the issue.
usable_records.author: patthedog

I’m mostly talking about Liked Songs though, because I’ve honestly given up on Playlist recommendations entirely as Spotify has apparently condemned each and every one of my Playlists to the having their own personal ~20 recommendations, forever unshuffled.

usable_records.text: Spotify keeps "recommending" inappropriate songs Plan Free/Premium Country Canada Device Web Operating System Web My Question or Issue When I play a specifically curated spotify playlist which I've set up for my children (on an account that has literally no other activity on it), spotify keeps slipping in "recommended" songs/music. I assume this is part of how they support the free tier, so I'm not bothered by this in and of itself. What does bother me is the fact that my profile is completely kids music, and suddenly a rap song with lyrics that make it absolutely inappropriate to play near children is inserted. Attempting to serenade my children with swears and racial slurs is not going to make me buy a premium subscription, it's just going to encourage me to go back to digitally copying my music collection so that I can know that I won't have to run across the room and pull the speaker to avoid having to explain what "b** can *** my ***" means. No matter how many times I say "don't recommend this" or "don't play songs like this", spotify finds a way to do it anyway.

usable_records.text: Re: Take a Break From Songs Without Deleting Them Hi, @Gabben , You can hide songs in playlists from the three dots menu via the (-) / X buttons or from the option in the three dots menu. Songs you've hidden won't be played by the app and will instead be skipped immediately. You can undo this anytime by repeating this process. Check out the screenshots below for an in-depth view: Spoiler (Highlight to read) Three dots menu. Song is now hidden and won't be played. Three dots menu.Song is now hidden and won't be played. ⚠️ Note : Hiding a track makes it unplayable only in that specific playlist/album. This means that you'll still be able to play it everywhere else, unless you manually hide it there as well. On the mobile app, there's also an option to "snooze" hidden tracks in certain playlists, thus excluding them in all recommendations and across all platforms for 30 days. That way, you can keep things fresh while leaving the door open for a future reunion. There's more info in this article , check it out, if you're interested. Hope you found this useful. Cheers 🤘
usable_records.author: Yordan

usable_records.text: automatically add recommended songs to a playlist So I am frequently listenings to new genres and making personal playlists, and whenever I'm doing so I like to go to the recommended songs for that playlist. However when the music is good I can easily forget I need to add the songs to the playlist, especially when focussing on school projects for example. And when I remember to do that I'm already 10 songs past and don't kow which ones to add. Now I would really love an option where I can let spotify add the songs from the recommended list to my playlist whenever I didn't skip it. It's a lot easier to get a nicely sized playlist than needing to actively think if I want to add it or not for every song, and interrupting my work to do so. And for if you fall asleep or let it play without listening, a tag could be added to all recently added songs like this that goes away after opening the playlist again and scrolling to the end, so you could decide to not add them.

usable_records.text: Re: February 2026 Spotify for Developers update: thread Hello, I am the developer of Apollo, a music-driven social discovery application that integrates with the Spotify Web API. Apollo uses Spotify-authorized data such as top artists, top tracks, and recently played content to personalize recommendations and introduce users to new music. Listening is directed back to Spotify via deep links and native playback. I am currently reviewing the Extended API Access requirements and noticed that the application process appears to require a high monthly active user threshold and supporting market data. As an early-stage production app, this creates a challenge where certain features depend on extended access, but reaching that scale may depend on those same features. I wanted to ask if there is any recommended path for applications in this stage. Specifically, I am trying to understand how developers can best align with Spotify’s platform goals while operating within standard access limitations, and whether there are alternative approaches or considerations for apps that are not yet at large scale. Any guidance would be appreciated. Thank you.
usable_records.author: ApolloInteractive

usable_records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs @ jazzmanjohn Did that for 10 years, got slop anyway. I don't think you understand how the algorithms these systems use work. Sadly a lot of slop is pushed not based on your preferences or playlists, but as sponsored/paid content, Spotify is actively being paid to push this garbage on us. On top of that you have botnets pulling crazy numbers in fake streams, but stream numbers are only part of it, to hide their activity they also stream legit artists, so if you listen to any of those legit artists that the botnets fake-play the algorithm thinks you might also like the slop those botnets are actively trying to inflate. Greed + Gaming the system = Slop in your playlists. My Spotify recommendations were so damned near perfect, then the tsunami hit early last year. Perhaps you have been lucky so far, perhaps your personal music interests simply don't include songs popular enough to be fake-streamed yet, but eventually the AI bots will come for you to, it's just a matter of time.

usable_records.text: Best way to read EPUB files on browser while listening to Spotify? Hi everyone, I usually listen to Spotify while reading ebooks (mostly EPUB format), and I was looking for a simple way to read EPUB files directly in the browser without installing heavy apps. Most desktop readers are slow, and switching between Spotify and a reader app breaks the flow. So I started testing some browser-based EPUB readers to keep everything in one tab while music keeps playing. I recently found a lightweight web EPUB reader that runs fully in the browser — no install, no login — and it actually works smoothly alongside Spotify. It supports dark mode, bookmarks, and fast loading even for large EPUB files. If anyone else reads while listening to music or podcasts, this kind of tool is surprisingly useful. I’m curious what others here use: Do you read ebooks while streaming music? Any recommendations for distraction-free reading setups? For reference, this is the browser reader I tested: 👉 https://epubreaderpro.com Would love to hear what tools or setups you all prefer.
usable_records.author: Jindo_US

usable_records.text: Recommended Songs Refresh button not working Plan Premium Duo Country United States Device iPhone 13, Macbook Pro 2023 My Question or Issue The Recommended Songs button seemed to provide good suggestions for songs I may want to add to my playlist (debatable according to some users it seems). Either the refresh button is not working or my hunch is that it’s now being powered by an LLM instead of Spotify’s proprietary “AI”. Most of the time, I get the same songs back after I press refresh, but occasionally (on my Mac only) I will get a subset or superset of the few songs it has continued to recommend…hence my guess that you have moved to using an LLM. It’s bad, I don’t want any of the 5 or so songs it has presented, but refreshing does nothing to the list. Edit: this seems to be specific to a long playlist (~150 songs). It still doesn’t seem reasonable that there are no other good suggestions to make.

usable_records.text: Re: Half of the weekly playlists are filled with AI generated content Spotify Strengthens AI Protections for Artists, Songwriters, and Producers — Spotify Five months later, there are still no visible results from these promised protections. In fact, the opposite has happened: the platform is being flooded with AI‑generated content more than ever. Spotify announced stronger rules against impersonation, spam, and mass‑generated “slop,” and even talked about new spam filters and AI disclosures. But none of this seems to be working in practice. The amount of low‑effort AI music keeps growing, and it’s now affecting playlists, recommendations, and the overall listening experience. Real artists and real listeners are still the ones paying the price, while AI content farms continue to upload thousands of tracks without consequences. Spotify said they removed 75 million spam tracks and would roll out new systems to protect the ecosystem, but the situation today shows that these measures are either not active or not effective. This needs real enforcement, not just announcements.
usable_records.author: memorpo

usable_records.text: Re: Annoying “Hidden for 30 days” pop-up appears every time I hide a song Hey folks, Thanks for reaching out! When developing the 'Hide song' feature, we found that many seem to use it as a method to influence the algorithm's suggestions, so we've added an additional functionality where users will be able to ' snooze ' hidden tracks, thus excluding them in all recommendations and across all platforms for 30 days. That way, you can keep things fresh while leaving the door open for a future reunion. That being said, we also hear you that hiding a particular song means you don't want to hear it ever again. Your feedback on this is important, as it may ultimately end up paving the way for our broader user experience. Thanks for sharing it here - we appreciate it. 💚 There's more info on the Hide song feature in this article , check it out if you're interested. The Community is just a post away if something else comes up.
usable_records.author: Yordan

usable_records.text: Fix DJ so it doesn’t just hit play on the “On Repeat” playlist Dear Spotify, When the DJ was first introduced, it was fantastic! I’d get blocks of songs that I might like, deep dives into my music history, and just generally a great mix. As time has went on however, this mix has stopped. I barely use the DJ feature anymore because all it ever does is hit play on my “on repeat” playlist. Constantly. Even when it finishes a block and moves on to something “new”, it’s typically just a different set of songs from my on repeat playlist. I miss the DJ feature of old. I’m more than capable of pressing randomize on my most listened songs. Bring back the DJ that dug up great song recommendations based on my taste, through in nostalgic throwbacks, and played songs that I liked but hadn’t listened to in a while. Sincerely, One of music’s biggest fans

metadata.title: (Playlists) Replace the Unused Shorts Button On Playlists With Recommended Songs
metadata.search_snippet: Overall Idea: You know that useless button on the top left of your Playlists? What if we replaced that useless shorts button with an actually useful feature? You know the small flip through and curated spng suggestions you sometimes find on your feed? What if we placed the "Find new songs for your playlists" thing to that useless button on thr top left of your Playlist screen, wouldn't that be way more useful? Opinions: Also, I personally dont like the current state of the recommended songs on the bottom of the playlists, since it only goes by artists and albums, not the genre or "feel" of the Playlist like those little slideshows do, so it would also improve on that regard as well. If you have any suggestions on making this better in any way, contact me below, and I might add it to the suggestion in the coming days. 🙂

usable_records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs @PJ40 Weird they removed your posts for promoting other apps, they've left practically all of mine alone, and I've been quite a prolific poster the past couple of months. Honestly, I would prefer if Spotify just fixed the problem, they do have a far better recommendation system than most other platforms (prior to the AI slop tsunami that is), usually nailing my tastes after only a few weeks of use, sadly Deezer, after 3 months, hasn't quite figured me out yet and keeps recommending a lot of duds. I think I found 1 track from this weeks recommendations that I enjoyed enough to add to my usual rotation. But they don't seem to be interested in fixing anything as it continues to line their pockets for the time being, so until then I will continue to promote the mass exodus to alternate platforms hoping that the drop in revenue might actually spark some change.

usable_records.text: Let Us More Directly Influence Daylists As part of a study at my university, I've been recording some of the recommendations and topics of my various daylists throughout the week. One thing I've noticed is that my daylist has almost nothing in common with my current listening habits. It's all music I listened to months ago, and despite using the app fairly frequently, my daylist algorithm is ignoring my new tastes. Having the ability to more directly influence or provide feedback to our daylists, even if it's just simple "Good playlist" or "Bad Playlist" feedback options, would be very useful for allowing users to tune their experiences to match our current tastes, while still preserving the convenience and novelty of our daylists. If I can tell the DJ to switch it up, I should be able to do this as well. It can't be harder than implementing AI, song mixing, and everything else in here. Make it happen please.
usable_records.author: BlueSpook

usable_records.text: Add option to avoid AI-generated / synthetic artists Hi Spotify team, I’d really appreciate it if you would add a setting that lets me avoid AI-generated or synthetic artists. I don’t have anything against AI in general, but when I listen to music, I want real musicians, real voices and real creativity. Right now it’s impossible to tell what’s human and what isn’t, and some AI projects show up in my recommendations next to real bands. That feels misleading, and it affects how I use the platform. What I’m asking for is pretty simple: a toggle to filter out AI-generated artists clear labeling when a track or artist is AI-driven better verification so synthetic projects don’t slip into the catalogue unnoticed I think a lot of listeners feel the same way. Transparency would make the experience better for everyone and help support real musicians. Thanks for taking it seriously.

usable_records.text: How satisfied are you with Spotify right now? Your experiences? Hey everyone, I’m collecting real user experiences for a university project and would love to hear your opinions on Spotify — good or bad. Here are a few points I’m especially interested in: 1. General usage: Is Spotify stable for you? Any bugs, slow loading, or interface issues? 2. Recommendations: Do the suggestions match your taste, or do playlists feel repetitive? 3. Pricing & subscription: How do you feel about the current pricing? Are you using Premium or Free — and what do you like or dislike? 4. Content (music/podcasts): Are any artists/tracks missing? Are you satisfied with the podcast and audiobook offering? 5. Audio quality & settings: How’s the sound quality for you? Do you use EQ settings or notice any limitations? 6. Device compatibility: Any problems with smart speakers, car systems, or synchronization? Thanks a lot for sharing your experience — every insight helps! 🙌
usable_records.author: JoeShmoe21

usable_records.text: Re: [Web API] Creating Playlist as Public Thank you @Ximzend for your reply on this issue.I am however still confused about it because if the "public: false" attribute doesn't actually change the playlist to truly private (publicly inaccessible even with the link), then it just making it publicly hidden / indiscoverable (not appearing on the user profile, recommendations or search results). It's actually a different state for a public playlist, "hidden". However the real problem is that the API doesn't seem to expose that state in GET /playlists as far as I can see - when calling POST /users/{user_id}/playlist with "public: false" and then calling GET /playlists/{playlist_id} the returned flag would still be "public: true", but that just tells us the playlist is not private - it doesn't actually tell us whether it is truly public or hidden. So how can we know that state from the API? Is there another flag for it?
usable_records.author: yuvalr80

usable_records.text: Re: Spotify doesn't download all songs in the offline playlists Yes, exactly! Thanks, @onlytocomplain ! It appears that the songs are interlinked for stream count reasons, as on Spotify this is tracked per-song and everything is aggregated. So due to this interlinking, a song with the same content can appear as a "previous version" of itself, but is ultimately one and the same and shouldn't influence the listening experience. Some artists may do this to expand their reach to new audiences, like having the "previous version" show up only in personalized recommendations (such as your daylist, for example). What most likely has happened here is that the "current version" appears in some of your other playlists which you have downloaded, but due to the interlinking, the "previous version" also shows as downloaded. We can see how this may look confusing, so your feedback on the matter is greatly appreciated - thanks for sharing it here. Cheers 🤘
usable_records.author: Yordan

usable_records.text: Suggestion for recommending producers based on musical style, in a simple and quick way. Hello everyone, how are you? My idea is the following: when I access a producer’s profile, I would like to know that producer’s specific musical style and which styles they identify with. This would allow me to find new producers, tracks, and related content in an easy, fast, and intuitive way. The idea is that on the shared screen, below the producer’s name (or in another visible place), the musical style could be displayed. For example: Main Genre: Trance Other genres they identify with: Psytrance, Progressive House, House. This way, by clicking on the genres, I would be taken to other producers—well-known or not—who play the same style, thus contributing both to established producers and to those who are not yet very famous. I hope this can be implemented! Thank you all!
usable_records.author: jonnetg

usable_records.text: Re: Removing Podcast Recommendations After about half a year of what feels like abuse, these Spotify podcast recommendations keep appearing in my feed and there is still no way to make them go away. If I cannot curate my own taste in podcasts and music, what is the point? I don’t need Spotify to tell me what I like and don’t like. The “Not Interested” button has done nothing after months of pressing it (Spotify will keep recommending it anyway). I’m so tired of my feed that I literally will be terminating my account and creating a new one. This time I won’t make the mistake of accidentally pressing a podcast/video/music that I don’t like and see if my feed gets better. If not, I might try Apply music for the next few years before I switch to Android.

usable_records.text: Re: Bizarre album listed under wrong artist "It is honestly exhausting to see yet another 'fake' album being uploaded under a legendary name like Aztec Camera . A band that hasn't been active since the 90s suddenly 'releasing' Christian-themed content? It’s clearly an imposter or a metadata error, and it’s a slap in the face to fans. Spotify needs to implement a true artist/band certification service for recommendations immediately. At this point, we urgently need a profile setting that allows users to filter out unverified releases or flag blatant 'AI slop' and imposters. The platform is becoming cluttered with low-quality, fraudulent content. If Spotify doesn't find a way to protect the integrity of real artists' discographies soon, the user experience will be permanently ruined. Please fix the Aztec Camera profile and give us better tools to control what appears in our feeds."

usable_records.text: Feature Request: A Dedicated Listen Later List for Music Discovery Currently, when we stumble upon an interesting track, album, or EP but don't have the time to listen right away, our options are limited . We either have to add it to our "Liked Songs" (which messes up our personal algorithms and recommendations) or create a temporary playlist (which clutters our meticulously organized library). I suggest implementing a simple "Listen Later" feature—perhaps accessible via the three-dot menu—that saves music to a specific, easily accessible queue or folder . Just like the "Watch Later" feature on video platforms, this would allow users to bookmark new discoveries instantly without disrupting their established music collections . This small UI/UX improvement would significantly boost user engagement, encourage users to explore new artists without hesitation, and keep the main library perfectly organized . Thank you for considering this enhancement!
usable_records.author: kxyra

usable_records.text: Re: Recommended Songs - Not appearing in listening history Hey @MadisonnRoxx , Thank you for reaching out to the Community and welcome. That's an odd case since the recommended songs should be added to the listening history as usual if you play them. So to make sure we're on the same page, would you mind confirming if you've already performed a clean reinstall of the app? If not we'd recommend following the steps mentioned in this article to see if it makes any difference. If the issue persists, it's possible that the behavior you mentioned could be related with this thread , which is currently under investigation by internal teams since it's affecting the way that the app "recognizes" the tracks played from outside a playlist (such as the queue or recommendations). Hope this helps. If anything else comes up, don't hesitate to ask.
usable_records.author: OscarDC

usable_records.text: Re: Issues with using "Search" - keyboard does not appear automatically This is how it works today. To open keyboard when hitting the search button: 1. Tap search -> moves to page for search 2. Tap search field at top of search page. Old searches and recommendations can be viewed here-> field transforms to a writeable field in grey 3. Tap the grey field to type in it -> keyboard upens up. This is ridiculously complicated. It should open the keyboard once you tap the first search button. But it could allow you to scroll through past searches and recommendations as you can. This is however a problem that is found in Google Play Store as well so I understand that this may be a Google problem as well. But in play store it is only two taps to open the keyboard.
usable_records.author: Orschild

usable_records.text: Re: Smart shuffle on desktop Hi @j_pope , Thanks for reaching out. The process to using Smart Shuffle on desktop is slightly different now, but still functions the same. All you have to do is open a playlist and tap the shuffle icon twice, either at the top of the playlist before starting it or in the Now Playing field at the bottom. When it turns on, you should see a notification that recommendations have been added to the queue. Does this work for you? If the option isn't there, make sure it's enabled from the settings as shown at the end of this article . If it still doesn't work and you continue to face issues with it, let us know in more detail what the exact behaviour is and we'll be glad to help you. Hope this helps.
usable_records.author: Joan

usable_records.text: Re: Trouble Resizing New Android Widget Hi there @TOG_2 , Thanks for reaching out. We'll be glad to help you with the Android widget. The default size of the widget is 4x2, but it can be resized to anything from 1x1 to 4x4. Be advised though that this does vary depending on the device. Make sure you're using the new widget with all the visible playlist recommendations and not the old one with just the player. The old widget is deprecated, but it's possible you may still see it and that one lacks the resizing options of the new widget. If the resizing of the new widget still doesn't work, send us some screenshots and let us know in greater detail what the behaviour is so we can look into it and assist you further. Hope this helps.
usable_records.author: Joan

usable_records.text: Re: Recommended songs Hey there @Xyzman123 , Thanks for getting back to us. Just to confirm, have you tried the suggestions @Dessi mentioned previously? It'd be helpful if you could give those a steps so we can narrow down what might be causing the issue. Also, make sure to check if the same happens on different devices or only on your phone. On another note, we recommend checking if the same happens with a different internet connection. If you've been using Wi-Fi, now you can try with mobile data to see if that makes the difference. Lastly, sending us the exact make/model, operating system and Spotify version of your affected devices would come in handy. You can find the Spotify version of your devices by following these steps. We'll be on the lookout for your reply.

usable_records.text: Re: Ai generated content should be labeled. AI Generated Music should definitely not be a recommendation if it's not something you casually listen to. The problem is that Spotify doesn't categorize AI music separately form human made music. That means if you block AI music, it actively messes with your algorithm that includes human made music of the same genre. So if I block an AI generated song in the genre of 60s surf rock, I'll less likely be shown 60s surf rock (which I enjoy). That is a severe, nearly critical, impact to user experience. If Spotify keeps AI music on their platform, it not only needs to be tagged as such, but categorized differently such that your listening algorithm doesn't affect human-made music. Furthermore, for ease, a setting to disable AI generated song recommendations.
usable_records.author: ZacL

usable_records.text: Auto-Remove or Prompt to Remove Songs You Repeatedly Skip I have huge playlists and it’s hard to clean them up manually. My idea is that Spotify should notice when you keep skipping the same song and either remove it automatically or just ask if you want to. You guys already track skips for recommendations so the data is already there. A few things to consider though — skipping in the last few seconds shouldn’t count because that’s basically a full listen. And it should take a few skips before anything happens, not just one, because sometimes you skip a song just because you’re not in the mood even though you still like it. If those edge cases are handled right this could make playlist cleanup basically automatic just from listening normally.
usable_records.author: SawyerE

usable_records.text: Re: i have a few problems here lol Hey there @op1umofthepeople , Thanks for reaching out on the Community! Hope you don't mind us moving your post from the Ideas section, as it fits better here 🙂 Your Spotify account will not automatically be removed even if you're not using for a bit, no need to worry. However, certain features such as recommendations, Made for You playlists, and Recently played might not be up-to-date if you're not actively using the app. While it's not possible to restore previously recently played tracks in the app, Recently played should update with time and reflect your current listening habits. Feel free to have a look at this article for more details on Recents. We hope this clears things up! If anything else appears, we're just a post away.

usable_records.text: Re: Duo members share one Apple iPad. Hey @Trentify , thanks for reaching out to the Community! It’s not possible to switch libraries between accounts without logging out and logging in to another account. Premium Duo works by having two members under the same plan so only the plan’s manager is billed. This means that both accounts are still separate and have their own libraries and content recommendations. However, there is a feature that may be rolled out to iPad in the future which allows you to switch accounts on the same device. More info on that here . There’s more info on how Premium Duo works here . Hope this helps!
usable_records.author: Billy-J

metadata.title: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs
metadata.search_snippet: @mimiczone If you like AI, go for it. Many of us just don't like it. As I mentioned earlier, it's quite easy to control your Spotify recommendations. Spotify was a human only club until AI entered the picture. Most of us, I think, still like that formula. But that ship has sailed never to return. I just think many of us want an option to filter out AI music just as we can explicit material. There's certainly nothing wrong with that. Otherwise, why are so many subscribers commenting in this discussion?

usable_records.text: You recommend playlist for concentration, but then.. I'll start by saying that I'm not using the premium plan yet... but I'd still like to point out the inconsistency. I've been receiving frequent suggestions from Spotify for "Playlist for concentration or to listen to during the workday helping to focus on." But while listening to these Playlists, the frequency of interruptions due to ads is significant. I don't expect it to be removed from the free plan, but at least the number of interruptions while listening to concentration playlists shall be reduced... otherwise, how can you concentrate?

usable_records.text: Re: Spotify - The Worst App in the World EVER I listen to many different genres, from punk, disco, progressive rock, pop, jazz and classical. This year, I am only getting jazz tracks, and nothing vocal at all, mainly drum, bass, piano trios... The recommendation algo is much too narrow. I would like to have the ability to widen it back to what is was before. e.g. I haven't had any world music tracks for an age. I hate the recommendations now, just so boring. Give me a button to widen/ explore in my taste?

usable_records.text: Suggest songs by lyrics / themes Spotify needs to ask us what variable we would like song recommendations to be based on. As simple as: would you like Similar artists? Similar beats/genre? Similar lyrics?! For example, when I have a playlist comprises solely of songs about being in the military and Spotify offers me similar bands or beats, I can't use a single one of them. It shouldn't be that hard to make an algorithm based on song lyric theme, but you have to ask us to know that that's what we want.

usable_records.text: Content preference to disable AI generated music In the same way Spotify gives me the choice to listen to “explicit” music or not, please give me the choice to listen to “AI generated” music or not. For example, souloverai.com independently and algorithmically tags music as gen AI. For now, I have to manually filter and block 1/5 of the Spotify recommendations on my favorite genre. This has become exhausting and disheartening for someone wanting to discover and support professional, human musicians.

usable_records.text: Re: [Your Library] Search for individual songs within all Playlists This is a major usability problem for anyone with large music libraries. Searching Your Library should return individual tracks across all playlists, not just playlists, albums, and artists. It makes no sense that Spotify can identify songs for recommendations and playlists internally, yet users cannot globally search their own saved tracks by title.

usable_records.text: Spotify AI dj settings page 1. DJ AI Settings Overview: Users should have the ability to customize the DJ AI to play specific genres, moods, or artists. Implementation: Genres and Moods: Users can select from a predefined list of genres or moods. Artist and Song Suggestions: Users can specify favorite artists, bands, albums, or songs. Playlist Preferences: Users can create playlists and specify their preferences for the DJ AI to follow. 2. AI/Prompted Playlist Settings Overview: Users can define specific prompts or themes for AI-generated playlists. Implementation: Prompt Input: Users can input a text prompt that the AI will use to generate the playlist. Theme Selection: Users can choose from predefined themes or create custom ones. Playlist Options: Users can specify the length of the playlist or the number of songs. 3. Mixed Playlists Settings Overview: Users can customize the AI to mix playlists based on specific criteria. Implementation: Criteria Selection: Users can choose from different criteria such as song popularity, genre diversity, or tempo. Mixing Preferences: Users can specify the mixing style (e.g., smooth, fast-paced). Playlist Customization: Users can adjust the number of tracks or the time between songs. 4. AI Artists/Bands/Albums/Songs Settings Overview: Users can manage and customize AI recommendations for artists, bands, albums, and songs. Implementation: Artist and Band Suggestions: Users can input their favorite artists or bands. Album and Song Recommendations: Users can specify genres or themes for album and song suggestions. Personalized Recommendations: Users can receive personalized recommendations based on their listening history. 5. Control Over AI Playback Overview: Users should have the ability to control the playback of AI-generated content. Implementation: Pause and Play: Users can pause and play AI-generated content. Skip Tracks: Users can skip tracks if they are not enjoyable. Volume Control: Users can adjust the volume of AI-generated content. 6. Feedback and Improvement Overview: Users should have the option to provide feedback on the AI's performance. Implementation: Rating System: Users can rate the AI's performance on a scale of 1 to 5. Comments Section: Users can leave comments on their experience with the AI. Suggestions: Users can provide suggestions for improvement. By implementing these settings, users will have a comprehensive and customizable experience with the AI features in Spotify, allowing them to tailor the music recommendations to their preferences and enjoy a personalized listening experience. 👤 You Now add custom voice section, such as different english accents, such as American, Australian, British, etc, and maybe even the ability to use your own voice 🤖 Assistant Here is an updated version of the Spotify AI settings page with an added custom voice section: # Spotify AI Settings Page AI Chat Conversation 👤 You help me complete this feature request and elaborate on each point 🤖 Assistant To complete this feature request, we can design a comprehensive AI settings page for Spotify that includes detailed controls for each AI functionality. Playlist Options: Users can specify the length of the playlist or the number of songs. 3. Mixed Playlists Settings Overview: Users can customize the AI to mix playlists based on specific criteria. Implementation: Criteria Selection: Users can choose from different criteria such as song popularity, genre diversity, or tempo. Mixing Preferences: Users can specify the mixing style (e.g., smooth, fast-paced). Playlist Customization: Users can adjust the number of tracks or the time between songs. 4. AI Artists/Bands/Albums/Songs Settings Overview: Users can manage and customize AI recommendations for artists, bands, albums, and songs. Implementation: Artist and Band Suggestions: Users can input their favorite artists or bands. Album and Song Recommendations: Users can specify genres or themes for album and song suggestions. Personalized Recommendations: Users can receive personalized recommendations based on their listening history. 5. Control Over AI Playback Overview: Users should have the ability to control the playback of AI-generated content. Implementation: Pause and Play: Users can pause and play AI-generated content. Skip Tracks: Users can skip tracks if they are not enjoyable. Volume Control: Users can adjust the volume of AI-generated content. 6. usable_records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs There’s an article in the Guardian today 14 Nov “AI slop tops Billboard and Spotify charts as synthetic music spreads”. Not sure if I can share the link here as it's a newspaper, but it discusses how a couple of AI songs have managed to top Spotify's Viral 50 chart, and how this slop is saturating the streaming services. There’s a link to the statement as posted by @Vasil at the top of this discussion, and there’s also a link to a study from 12 Nov by Deezer, looking at whether most listeners can identify AI tracks, and the need for transparency; Deezer Survey The Deezer study was conducted with 9000 people across 8 countries, it makes interesting reading. Deezer are the only streaming company to at least tag AI at this time, and appear to be proactive in trying to find a solution how to deal and/or live with this. Spotify’s statement of introducing a spam filter this autumn, and “rolling the system out conservatively over the coming months” sounds similar to the lossless announcement from several years ago, how long did we end up waiting for that? Spotify’s statement does not say we will see which songs are AI-generated in playlists, recommendations, or search results. The only visible identification of AI use will come from new AI disclosure tags that appear inside the track’s detailed credits, these won’t be prominently displayed in the playlists, and will only show up if labels, distributors, or rights holders submit that information through the new industry-standard system. There is no suggestion Spotify will prevent AI content from appearing in users recommendations, only spam and abusive mass-generated content, not AI music in general. Spotify is the biggest and most established music-streaming service in the world. But anyone who cares about the state of the music industry, the shrinking payouts to artists, the inferior audio quality compared to competitors, the growing deluge of AI slop creeping into their recommendations, (and don’t get me started on the ICE ad’s, AI Drone warfare and podcasters payouts), will eventually look elsewhere. I already have. This discussion, as of today, only has just over 3,160 votes, which suggests the issue isn’t gaining much traction outside this community, and with more than 280 million premium subscribers, Spotify probably won’t notice the small minority who leave. But many of us have been loyal for years, tolerating the absurdly long wait for lossless streaming while other platforms have offered it for ages, and overlooking Spotify’s low artist payments because the app was always smooth, played nicely with external speakers, and the recommendations used to be genuinely great (along with AI content, my Discover Weekly has absolutely deteriorated, and has been dreadful ever since Spotify introduced the genre tags. I know I have an eclectic taste in music, but even by my own standards the stuff being recommended to me lately is bizarre). Now, though, the red flags are piling up. Spotify feels like it has lost its moral compass, and the flood of AI generated slop appearing in our playlists, with no way to report it, filter it out, or opt out, feels like the final nail in the coffin. When one of the smaller streaming services has already taken the innovative of tagging AI content and even surveying their users about these concerns, it’s hard not to conclude that Spotify has completely lost touch with its audience. We have a choice. I’ve already made mine.
usable_records.author: AntGil

usable_records.text: Request for Extended API Access for AI-Based Spotify Thesis Project Hello, I am currently developing an AI-powered music assistant as part of my university thesis project, focused on enhancing the way users interact with Spotify through intelligent analysis, recommendations, and conversational interaction. At this stage, my system is already functional and operates using the data currently available through Spotify’s API (primarily liked/saved tracks). Based on this data, I have implemented the following capabilities: Storage and structuring of liked tracks, including artist, album, release year, and metadata Automatic genre inference (especially for Drum & Bass subgenres such as neurofunk, jungle, liquid, techstep, etc.) Mood detection based on audio features (energy, valence, danceability, acousticness, etc.) Classification of tracks into mood categories such as: aggressive, melodic, atmospheric, energetic, uplifting, dark, etc. Sorting and organizing tracks by genre, mood, and historical period Identification of most frequent (top) artists based on listening data Basic recommendation engine based on: genre (e.g. "recommend neurofunk tracks") mood (e.g. "I want something aggressive") combined mood + genre queries Additionally, I have implemented a natural-language query system that supports prompts such as: "show all my Spotify tracks sorted by genre" "sort all tracks in my Spotify liked songs by genre" "recommend tracks" "suggest jungle tracks" "recommend me drum and bass classic" "recommend me drum and bass only" "I want something aggressive" "I want something melodic" "I'm in the mood for dark Drum" "suggest something atmospheric" "recommend emotional liquid" "I want energetic jungle" "which artist appears most in my liked songs" "what Drum and Bass genres exist" "when did jungle appear" The system also supports historical genre analysis and era detection, allowing users to explore the origins and evolution of specific styles. The next stage of my project (currently conceptual and partially designed) includes: A voice-based AI assistant (similar to Alexa), for example: "Hey Spotify, recommend me tracks for chilling with friends at home." "Hey Spotify, give me music for a late-night party." "Hey Spotify, what did I listen to most this week?" Advanced mood and atmosphere detection based on listening patterns and context Weekly and monthly listening recaps (beyond the current annual Spotify recap) Discovery of new artists based on deep preference analysis Context-aware playlist generation (e.g. social events, studying, nightlife, etc.) More advanced recommendation logic using full catalog data Potential future extension: intelligent mixing / track sequencing (for advanced usage) However, in order to fully realize these ideas, I would need broader access to Spotify data. At the moment, my system is limited to what Spotify currently allows through the API (mainly liked songs). While this is sufficient for building a prototype, it significantly restricts the ability to: recommend new tracks outside of the liked library analyze the full spectrum of available genres and artists build more advanced AI-driven discovery features implement deeper personalization and context-aware recommendations For this reason, I would like to ask: Is it possible to obtain extended access to Spotify’s data for research or academic purposes? Are there programs, partnerships, or special permissions for thesis-level or experimental AI projects? What would be the appropriate path to request broader metadata or catalog-level access? I believe this project aligns with the future direction of music platforms toward more intelligent, conversational, and personalized interaction with music. Thank you very much for your time and consideration. I would be happy to provide additional details, documentation, or a demonstration of the system if needed. Kind regards, Hristina
usable_records.author: HrissTsvetanova

usable_records.text: Enable Hi-Res Losless / Dolby Atmos Streaming and Downloading With the launch of Spotify Lossless in September 2025, Premium subscribers finally got FLAC streaming up to 24-bit/44.1 kHz — a great first step. However, Spotify still trails the competition in two key areas: true Hi-Res audio and immersive/spatial formats. The request: Hi-Res Lossless streaming beyond the current 24-bit/44.1 kHz ceiling, ideally up to 24-bit/192 kHz where the source masters allow it. Dolby Atmos / spatial audio support for tracks that have a dedicated Atmos mix. Offline downloads in both formats , so the full quality is also available without a connection — essential for travel, areas with poor coverage, and professional use cases (DJs, audio engineers, reviewers). Why Apple Music is the benchmark: Apple Music has offered Lossless (up to 24-bit/48 kHz), Hi-Res Lossless (up to 24-bit/192 kHz) and Dolby Atmos since May 2021 — at no extra cost, including downloads. Amazon Music Unlimited and Tidal offer comparable tiers. The catalog of Atmos mixes and Hi-Res masters already exists at the labels; Spotify is currently the only major platform not delivering it to listeners. Why this matters: Many subscribers currently pay for a second service (Apple Music or Tidal) purely for audio quality, while preferring Spotify's recommendations, playlists and ecosystem. Closing this gap removes the last reason to maintain two subscriptions. Hi-Res-capable hardware (DACs, LDAC/aptX devices, wired IEMs, home audio systems) has become mainstream and affordable. The audience for this feature is no longer a niche. Spotify Connect combined with Hi-Res and Atmos would be a genuinely unique selling point: no competitor matches Spotify's device integration. Suggested implementation: Add "Hi-Res Lossless" and "Dolby Atmos" as selectable quality options in the existing audio quality settings, with clear badges on eligible tracks (similar to Apple Music's labeling). Make both available for downloads, with a visible storage/bandwidth warning given the larger file sizes. If cost is a concern, even a paid add-on tier would be preferable to no option at all — though competitors have shown it can be included in the standard subscription. Spotify Lossless proved the infrastructure for high-quality delivery exists. Extending it to Hi-Res and Atmos is the logical next step to make Spotify the complete platform for both casual listeners and audiophiles.
usable_records.author: ChrisLupus

usable_records.text: Discography listening experience feels undermined by current playback behavior Hello Spotify Team, I would like to share feedback regarding the current artist and album listening experience in the desktop application. For years, many users requested better discography browsing, especially the ability to view and explore releases in chronological order. Spotify's community forums contain years of feedback asking for proper oldest-to-newest sorting, and more recently improvements were introduced to make discography navigation much better. As someone who enjoys discovering artists through their complete body of work, this was exactly the kind of improvement I had been waiting for. Unfortunately, the value of these changes now feels reduced because the playback experience increasingly pushes listeners away from album-focused listening. I often want to start with an artist's earliest album and continue through their catalog, album by album, experiencing their evolution over time. The recent addition of chronological sorting finally made that possible from a navigation perspective. However, when playback behavior starts steering users toward autoplay, artist radio, recommendations, or algorithm-generated content, it works against the purpose of exploring a discography in the first place. If I open an artist page, sort releases from oldest to newest, and start listening to albums, my intention is very clear: I want to listen to that artist's albums. Not radio. Not recommendations. Not a personalized stream. If I want artist radio, I will deliberately start artist radio. What makes this especially frustrating is that Spotify has already invested effort into improving discography organization and chronological browsing. Those were positive changes that acknowledged how many listeners still care about albums as complete works. The playback experience should reinforce that goal rather than interrupt it. I would love to see Spotify continue improving album-focused listening by: Supporting seamless album-to-album discography playback. Respecting chronological listening workflows. Keeping album listening clearly separated from recommendation-driven listening. Bringing advanced discography features to mobile apps rather than simplifying desktop behavior toward a more radio-focused experience. Albums are one of the foundations of music culture. Many listeners still enjoy experiencing music the way artists released it, one record at a time. Thank you for your consideration. Plan Premium Country PL Operating System (iWindows 11) My Question or Issue
usable_records.author: mikcio2

metadata.title: Discography listening experience feels undermined by current playback behavior
metadata.search_snippet: Hello Spotify Team, I would like to share feedback regarding the current artist and album listening experience in the desktop application. For years, many users requested better discography browsing, especially the ability to view and explore releases in chronological order. Spotify's community forums contain years of feedback asking for proper oldest-to-newest sorting, and more recently improvements were introduced to make discography navigation much better. As someone who enjoys discovering artists through their complete body of work, this was exactly the kind of improvement I had been waiting for. Unfortunately, the value of these changes now feels reduced because the playback experience increasingly pushes listeners away from album-focused listening. I often want to start with an artist's earliest album and continue through their catalog, album by album, experiencing their evolution over time. The recent addition of chronological sorting finally made that possible from a navigation perspective. However, when playback behavior starts steering users toward autoplay, artist radio, recommendations, or algorithm-generated content, it works against the purpose of exploring a discography in the first place. If I open an artist page, sort releases from oldest to newest, and start listening to albums, my intention is very clear: I want to listen to that artist's albums. Not radio. Not recommendations. Not a personalized stream. If I want artist radio, I will deliberately start artist radio. What makes this especially frustrating is that Spotify has already invested effort into improving discography organization and chronological browsing. Those were positive changes that acknowledged how many listeners still care about albums as complete works. The playback experience should reinforce that goal rather than interrupt it. I would love to see Spotify continue improving album-focused listening by: Supporting seamless album-to-album discography playback. Respecting chronological listening workflows. Keeping album listening clearly separated from recommendation-driven listening. Bringing advanced discography features to mobile apps rather than simplifying desktop behavior toward a more radio-focused experience. Albums are one of the foundations of music culture. Many listeners still enjoy experiencing music the way artists released it, one record at a time. Thank you for your consideration. Plan Premium Country PL Operating System (iWindows 11) My Question or Issue

usable_records.text: Re: Ai generated content should be labeled. I strongly support the idea that AI-generated content on Spotify should be clearly labeled, and I want to explain why this matters so much to me as a listener who values human-made art. I want to **exclusively** listen to music created and performed by humans, including all vocals. When AI-generated tracks or AI-assisted vocals appear in my recommendations or playlists without any clear labeling, I feel like my time is being stolen. I did not choose this, and I don’t want to be tricked into listening to something that isn’t genuinely human-made. More and more big artists and labels are incorporating AI vocals and AI elements into their releases, and I personally dislike this trend a lot. It actively decreases my satisfaction with Spotify as a platform, because I can no longer trust that what I’m hearing is actually a human performance. I’m not saying this music shouldn’t exist on the platform at all, but I should be able to opt out of it completely. What I’m asking for is simple and fair: - Artists and labels must clearly indicate if a track contains any AI-generated or AI-assisted elements (especially vocals). - Spotify should display this clearly and offer a filter or setting to hide AI-generated or AI-assisted content from my recommendations, playlists, and radio. - This should apply across the whole platform: editorial playlists, algorithmic playlists, radios, and personalized mixes. I pay for Spotify because I want to listen to art created by human beings, with human intent, emotion, and performance. That’s the experience I value and choose to support financially. If I can’t reliably avoid AI-generated content here, I will seriously consider leaving the platform and looking for a service that respects this preference and gives me full control over whether I engage with AI-generated music or not. Fun fact: I did however use AI to write this comment 😛
usable_records.author: hiiambandit

usable_records.text: AI music transparency labels and optional controls As a Spotify Premium subscriber of more than 10 years, I would like to suggest greater transparency around AI-generated music on the platform. To be clear, I am not asking Spotify to remove or ban AI-generated music. Listeners should be free to enjoy whatever music they like, and artists should be free to experiment with new technologies. My request is simply that users have access to information that helps them make informed choices. Proposed features: • Add a small badge or disclosure icon on tracks, albums, or artist profiles when music is substantially generated by AI. • Allow users to view additional information about how a song was created. • Add an optional setting that allows listeners to exclude AI-generated music from influencing recommendations, listening statistics, Spotify Wrapped, and taste profiles. Why this matters: Recently, I discovered a song that I genuinely enjoyed, but I realized there was no easy way to determine whether it had been primarily created by human artists or generated through AI systems. As AI-generated content becomes more common, I believe many listeners will want greater transparency. This proposal is not about restricting access to AI-generated music. It is about providing information and giving users more control over their listening experience. Spotify has already demonstrated leadership in AI-powered features such as DJ and personalized recommendations. Implementing clear disclosure standards for AI-generated music would be another opportunity for Spotify to lead the industry in transparency and consumer choice. People do not need to agree on the role AI should play in music, but they should have access to information that allows them to make their own decisions. If this is something you would like to see, please vote and comment so Spotify can better understand community interest.
usable_records.author: Stefaniepantz

usable_records.text: AI Chat-Based Playlist Generator (Prompt-to-Playlist Feature) I would love to see a new feature in Spotify that allows users to generate playlists or radio stations using natural language through an AI-powered chat interface. Instead of relying only on existing playlists or passive algorithmic recommendations, users could simply describe what they want to listen to, and Spotify would generate a personalized, dynamic playlist in real time. The idea is to introduce a new “AI Mode” where users type anything—from a mood to a very specific concept—and the system interprets it to build a playlist that adapts continuously based on user interaction (likes, skips, or prompt refinements). For example, a user could write “songs that could be World Cup 2026 anthems, but are not official World Cup songs,” and the system would understand elements like high energy, stadium vibe, and global appeal, while excluding existing official tracks, instantly generating a playlist that fits that concept. This would enable true intent-based music discovery, allowing users to explore highly specific or creative ideas in a way that feels active and engaging rather than passive, while also increasing time spent on the platform and differentiating Spotify’s experience. Additional features could include refining prompts (e.g., “make it more electronic” or “more Latin”), saving generated playlists, or integrating this experience with existing features like Radio or AI DJ. Overall, this would shift Spotify from simply recommending music to truly understanding what users want to feel or explore, making discovery more personalized, interactive, and creative. And if by any chance someone from the team finds this interesting, valenvota23@(gmail. com) (It gets censored, thats way is written weirdly) — I’m currently looking for an internship and always happy to chat (had to shoot my shot 😄 ).
usable_records.author: valenvota
