usable_records.text: Allow playlist creators to insert non-playable text headers (section separators) inside playlists. Description Long-form and chronological playlists lose clarity because Spotify playlists are currently a flat list of tracks with no structure. This is especially noticeable for: Decade or year-by-year music history playlists Educational playlists (music, language, classroom use) Fitness playlists (warm-up / intervals / cooldown) DJ or long-form curated sets There is currently no way to indicate sections, years, or phases inside a playlist without disrupting playback. Proposed Feature Allow playlist creators to insert non-playable text headers (section separators) directly into playlists. Key characteristics: Headers are not tracks and do not play They do not affect shuffle, autoplay, or recommendations Visible inline within the playlist track list Optional and controlled by the playlist owner Examples: — 1994 — Warm-Up Peak Set Cooldown Side A / Side B Why This Matters This improves playlist usability, navigation, and comprehension without changing Spotify’s playback model. It benefits many user groups: Music history and archival listeners Educators and students Fitness instructors and athletes DJs and playlist curators Anyone managing long playlists This feature encourages higher-quality curation and makes long playlists easier to follow and return to. Minimal Implementation Suggestion To keep scope small: Allow insertion of a non-playable text item Character limit (e.g., 50 characters) Appears only in playlist views Ignored by playback, shuffle, and recommendations No changes needed to audio playback or discovery systems. Example Use Case A playlist titled “Rock #1s for the 1990s” could appear as: — 1991 — Track A Track B — 1992 — Track C Track D This adds clarity without affecting how the playlist plays.
usable_records.author: jon_hughes

usable_records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs I did it. After 10 years... I canceled Spotify premium and am fully acclimated to using Symfonium with my local files + Deezer and Bandcamp for recommendations. (FYI my 3,000 mp3 files are only 12GB, so your library might not take up as much space as you think) I made a comment here early this year voicing my grievances and sadly as expected nothing has really gotten better. Spotify has been paying con men for their laundered goods they've made from unwilling artists, happily recommended it everywhere on the platform, and then ignored users who want to know what they're listening to - the recent(ish) news is too little too late (if not a simple legal decision to cover themselves). I feel I've been lied to for too long. I've finally got everything in order and can move on. It's time for me to say goodbye forever. Streaming could have been great. Could've continued to be great - in it's own way (convenience). But you've gone too far down the path of "enshitification" and I'm not paying for it any longer. And I'm not even staying so you can make ad revenue off me. "Cry harder. You're not making a difference. No one cares" Bro, I'M sick of seeing this. I want to feel good about my music again. You can go wallow in your endless pit of artificially intelligent meaninglessness. I'm leaving. I'm done and gone. And maybe buying a Deezer subscription just to spite Spotify and support their competitor.
usable_records.author: OneAlbatross

usable_records.text: Please Add a “Music-Only Mode” Hi Spotify Team and Community, I’ve been a Premium subscriber for over 10 years. Spotify has been my primary music platform throughout that entire time, and I’ve recommended it to countless friends and colleagues. Recently, however, I’ve been increasingly frustrated with the growing emphasis on video content in the Home feed. I want to be clear: I am not against video as a feature. I understand that Spotify is expanding and evolving as a platform. What concerns me is the lack of user control. As a paying Premium subscriber, I would like the option to use Spotify strictly as a music platform — without video recommendations dominating the Home experience. Right now, there is no way to disable or opt out of this content globally. For users like me, who primarily use Spotify for music discovery, playlists, and daily listening, the shift toward video feels intrusive and misaligned with why we subscribed in the first place. This isn’t just a minor UI preference. It affects the overall experience of the product. If a simple “Music-Only Mode” toggle existed, this wouldn’t be an issue. Autonomy is what’s missing. I’m genuinely considering canceling my subscription over this — not because I dislike Spotify, but because the experience no longer reflects how I want to use the platform. I hope this feedback is taken seriously. Many long-time users value Spotify for its music-first identity. Please give us the choice. Thank you.
usable_records.author: Pasteleiro2D

usable_records.text: Optional Dynamic Range filter for recommendations I would like to propose a small, optional feature that could meaningfully improve listening quality for users who care about musical dynamics — without changing audio playback or affecting the default experience. Idea Add an optional “Dynamic Range filter” that allows users to exclude tracks with extremely low internal dynamic range from recommendations and autoplay. Important design principles: The filter would be OFF by default. It would only affect content selection (recommendations, radio, autoplay), not playback processing. No compression or DSP would be applied. The original audio would remain untouched. A simple implementation could use an existing loudness-based metric such as EBU R128 Loudness Range (LRA), calculated offline per track. Example user setting: Enable Dynamic Range filter Minimum dynamic range (LRA): slider Apply only to selected genres (e.g. classical, jazz, acoustic, etc.) Why this is valuable: It allows listeners who prefer natural dynamics to avoid overly dense and heavily limited masters. It avoids any artistic interference with the audio signal. It introduces a quality-oriented selection criterion, similar in spirit to how loudness normalization removed the incentive for excessively loud mastering. This would not be a “quality judgement” feature, but a personal listening preference tool — much like genre or mood filtering. From a system perspective, it could also provide useful feedback to content producers, as excessively low-dynamic masters would be less likely to appear in certain listener profiles. Thank you for considering this idea. Best regards, Tõnu Valk
usable_records.author: ebepost

usable_records.text: Option to automatically trigger "Private Session" for specific playlists Hi everyone, I would like to suggest a feature to improve how Spotify handles listening privacy and user statistics. Currently, "Private Session" is a global toggle. It would be extremely helpful to have a setting within individual playlists (such as sleep, meditation, or focus/study playlists) to automatically launch them in Private Session mode . Why this feature is needed: Clean Data & Stats: Many users track their listening habits via services like Last.fm or look forward to their "Spotify Wrapped." Sleep or ambient playlists often "pollute" these stats if we forget to manually toggle the Private Session. User Convenience: Manually switching Private Session on and off every time is tedious. Users often forget to enable it before sleep, or worse, forget to disable it the next day, losing hours of tracked "active" listening. Better Recommendations: This would allow for a clear separation between "utility listening" (background noise, white noise, concentration) and "active music discovery," keeping our personalized recommendations and "Daily Mixes" accurate. A simple toggle like "Always play this playlist in Private Session" within the playlist settings would be a game-changer for many of us. Thanks for considering this! PS : I'm french so i used Gemini tu write a message that is precise in English. That is why the message is in this format in order to be the more comprehensive possible.
usable_records.author: rc_luigi

usable_records.text: Full Account Reset Option (Clean Slate Mode) Idea Description: I’d like to suggest a feature that allows users to completely reset their Spotify account to a “brand new” state. Right now, recommendations, liked songs, playlists, and listening history all accumulate over time. This becomes problematic when: An account has been shared with others who have very different music tastes A user wants to completely restart their music journey The account’s data no longer reflects the user’s preferences at all In these cases, there is currently no way to fully start fresh without creating a new account. Proposed Solution: Introduce a “Clean Slate” or “Full Account Reset” option in account settings that: Deletes all listening history Removes all liked songs Deletes or clears all playlists Resets recommendations (Discover Weekly, Daily Mixes, Release Radar, etc.) Restores the account to a true “new user” state Optional Safeguards: A clear warning and confirmation step before reset Option to export or back up playlists before deletion Benefits: Gives users full control over their account and data Eliminates the need to create a new account just to start fresh Helps users who previously shared accounts Improves recommendation accuracy from a clean starting point This feature would provide a simple, user-friendly way to completely restart the Spotify experience without losing the account itself. Thanks for considering this idea!
usable_records.author: rj_devil_killa_91

usable_records.text: Add an option to pause briefly before Autoplay starts after an album When Autoplay is enabled, Spotify automatically starts playing recommended songs after the last track of an album. This is useful, but when listening to a full album, it can be hard to tell by ear when the album itself has ended and when Autoplay has started. This is especially noticeable when listening while working, commuting, or using a Bluetooth speaker or smart speaker. In those situations, I often listen without looking at the screen, so I may not realize that the album has ended and that Spotify has already moved on to recommended tracks. My idea is to add an optional setting that inserts a short pause before Autoplay starts after an album finishes. For example, after the final track of an album ends, Spotify could wait a few seconds before starting the first Autoplay recommendation. This would make it much easier to notice that the album has finished, without having to turn Autoplay off completely. I do not want to disable Autoplay, because it is useful for discovering new music and keeping playback going. I would just like a clearer audible separation between the album and the Autoplay recommendations. This would improve the experience for users who enjoy listening to full albums, while still keeping Autoplay available for continuous listening.
usable_records.author: guijty15699

usable_records.text: Re: Unwanted items in library Hey @Momma_Jo , Thanks for your reply. Currently, it's not possible to remove items from the Home page. As @Yordan mentioned previously, only certain recommendations have the " Not interested " option, which, if selected, will hide the content. We appreciate you taking the time to share your feedback with us, as it helps improve the overall user experience. Also, we recommend you giving a +Vote to this live idea , if this is something you'd like to see in the future. The Idea Exchange is a great way for us to see what our users are interested in and how many people like a certain idea. Check out this FAQ to learn more about it. On another note, you mentioned that you tried deleting 2 of your playlists, but weren't able to. Could you let us know if you tried to delete those from Your Library or from the Recently played on your Home page? If you tried to delete them from the Recently played section on the Home page, we can confirm that it's not possible, but you should be able to delete them from Your Library by following these steps. Hope this helps. If you have any questions or need a hand with anything else, the Community is here for you.
usable_records.author: Eni

metadata.title: Re: Unwanted items in library
metadata.search_snippet: Hey @Momma_Jo , Thanks for your reply. Currently, it's not possible to remove items from the Home page. As @Yordan mentioned previously, only certain recommendations have the " Not interested " option, which, if selected, will hide the content. We appreciate you taking the time to share your feedback with us, as it helps improve the overall user experience. Also, we recommend you giving a +Vote to this live idea , if this is something you'd like to see in the future. The Idea Exchange is a great way for us to see what our users are interested in and how many people like a certain idea. Check out this FAQ to learn more about it. On another note, you mentioned that you tried deleting 2 of your playlists, but weren't able to. Could you let us know if you tried to delete those from Your Library or from the Recently played on your Home page? If you tried to delete them from the Recently played section on the Home page, we can confirm that it's not possible, but you should be able to delete them from Your Library by following these steps. Hope this helps. If you have any questions or need a hand with anything else, the Community is here for you.

usable_records.text: Feature idea: Talk radio + replace music breaks with my Spotify playlist Hi Spotify Product Team, I’d like to share a feature idea that I think many “talk radio” listeners would love. I often listen to live radio stations for the spoken segments (for example P4 Göteborg), but I don’t always enjoy the music the station plays between talk segments. A great Spotify feature would be a “Talk Radio Mode” for live radio streams: Let the user listen to a live radio station as usual for talk/news/interviews. When the station switches to music, Spotify automatically fades out the radio and plays tracks from a Spotify playlist the user selects. When the station returns to talk, Spotify switches back to the live radio stream. Ideally, the transition would be seamless (quick fade/crossfade), and the user could choose the playlist (or “DJ”/recommendations) for the music breaks. I’m not sure what technical constraints exist around detecting talk vs. music in a live stream, but if it’s feasible, I think it would be a really compelling way to combine live radio content with Spotify’s personalized music experience. Thanks for considering the idea, and I’d be happy to clarify the user experience if helpful. Best regards, Robert Wiesel
usable_records.author: Wieselan

usable_records.text: Add an Incognito Mode for Listening That Does Not Affect Taste Profile Spotify should add an Incognito Mode for listening. Sometimes users want to play music that does not reflect their actual taste. This can happen when we are playing music for friends, working with children, testing songs, listening to sleep sounds, checking out a viral track, or playing music for a specific event. Those listens should not reshape our recommendations, Discover Weekly, Daily Mixes, Spotify Wrapped, or overall taste profile. Spotify already gives users some control with Private Session and the option to exclude certain tracks or playlists from the taste profile. However, users still need a simpler session-based option. The feature could work like this: Turn on Incognito Mode Listen normally Spotify still plays music as usual Those streams do not affect the user’s taste profile Similar songs are not pushed into recommendations afterward The mode automatically turns off after a set time, like Private Session This would give users more control over personalization without removing personalization completely. It would also make Spotify feel more accurate because recommendations would reflect what users genuinely enjoy, not every temporary, social, or situational listening session. Please consider adding a true Incognito Mode for Spotify listening.
usable_records.author: riccixrose

usable_records.text: What exactly does the x button do when you have a recommended song Plan Premium Country Sweden Device iPhone Operating System iOS My Question or Issue let me set the stage: I search up a random song, not from any playlist or nothing, I have the ”recommend songs” setting on so it will autoplay a song once the song you search up and play is over. It immediately starts another recommended song, and like always, recommended songs have that ”X” or ”+” buttons. + to add to liked songs, but pressing the ”x” gives a popup where it says that it will hide this song from this playlist. WHAT PLAYLIST?? it also has another add-on option to not recommend this song for 30 days which is self explanatory. But what playlist are they talking about? And what is it hiding? If I go to the artist page, the song is still there, if I search for the song, it comes up. So it’s not hidden. what if I wanna undo this hidden thing, whatever that is, later on? perhaps this is a weird question but I would really like to know cause I wanna know how I influence my algorithms.

usable_records.text: Re: Discovery weekly hasn't worked for months Hey there @Phatency , Thanks for reaching out on the Community! And kudos for including some details on what's happening from your end - we appreciate it. Just to confirm, do you notice any difference if you connect your device(s) to another internet connection? For example, if you're currently on WiFi, you can try mobile data to check if you're able to select a genre. If you're using any VPN, make sure to switch it off, as it could interfere with the service. As the Discover Weekly playlist is dynamic and reflects your latest listening habits, it might not always have enough content to go off to create the perfect mix for you. We'd recommend to keep listening to the music you love and giving us feedback (such as liking or hiding songs in the list), and with a bit of time, your Discover Weekly should update and better reflect your taste. Keep in mind that the more you listen to tracks outside of a Private Session , or by adding the tracks you love to Liked Songs and other playlists, the better our recommendations would be. Cheers!
usable_records.author: Maria

usable_records.text: Help the new ai DJ Hi Spotify team, I am a big fan of the new AI DJ feature, and I would love to suggest a few enhancements to make the experience even more personalized: 1) Contextual Interaction: I would love the ability to communicate directly with the AI DJ. It would be great to inform the DJ about the activities I am about to start (e.g., working out, studying, hosting a dinner). Allowing the AI to understand the context of my current situation would help it curate the perfect soundtrack for that specific moment. 2)Granular Feedback System: Currently, it is hard to steer the DJ’s selections. I would like to be able to tell the DJ exactly why I like or dislike a track. By providing specific feedback—such as "too loud," "too much bass," or "too many lyrics"—the AI could learn my preferences on a deeper level and refine its future recommendations to better suit my taste. I believe these additions would significantly deepen the connection between the user and the AI DJ. Thank you for your time and for the great work on this feature!
usable_records.author: rtsrooia

usable_records.text: Regional Focus: Allow users to select preferred locales/regions for algorithm recommendations Current Behaviour: Right now, Spotify's automated discovery algorithms (Discover Weekly, Release Radar, Smart Shuffle, and Autoplay) default heavily toward mainstream global hits, which are overwhelmingly dominated by American artists. Even if a user’s personal listening history consists primarily of UK, European, or other regional music, the algorithm frequently overrides these habits to push US-centric music. There is currently no setting to specify which geographic music scenes you want to discover or prioritise. My Suggestion for Improvement: I propose adding a "Preferred Regions/Locales" setting within the app preferences, or a localized filter toggle directly on automated playlists. This feature would allow users to select specific regions (e.g., "UK & Ireland", "Europe", "Nordic", etc.) that they want the algorithm to actively prioritize. Selecting a region would adjust the recommendation weights, preventing the feed from constantly defaulting to American charts. As a global platform, Spotify should allow listeners to explicitly support and discover talent from their own regions or specific cultures they are interested in, rather than pushing a one-size-fits-all, US-heavy music feed.
usable_records.author: opalfruit1979

usable_records.text: Option to Reset Listening History & Recommendations Idea Description: I’d like to suggest a feature that allows users to fully reset their listening history and music recommendations on Spotify. Currently, recommendations such as Discover Weekly, Release Radar, and Home feed suggestions are heavily influenced by past listening activity. This becomes an issue in cases where: An account is shared between multiple users with very different music tastes A user’s preferences significantly change over time The account has been used temporarily by someone else In such situations, recommendations become inaccurate and no longer reflect the primary user’s preferences. Proposed Solution: Introduce a “Reset Listening History” or “Start Fresh” option in account settings that: Clears all listening history Resets algorithmic recommendations (Discover Weekly, Daily Mixes, etc.) Optionally allows users to retain saved songs and playlists Benefits: Gives users control over their recommendation system Improves personalization accuracy Helps users who share accounts or undergo taste changes Enhances overall user satisfaction Additional Suggestion (Optional): Spotify could also consider introducing separate user profiles under one Premium account, similar to other streaming platforms, to avoid this issue altogether. Thanks for considering this feature!
usable_records.author: rj_devil_killa_91

usable_records.text: Re: [Amazon Alexa] Swap Spotify accounts without changing Alexa account I’m reporting a long-standing integration problem between Spotify and Amazon Alexa that affects multi-user households. Spotify allows multiple profiles under Family/Duo plans, but Alexa can only link one single Spotify account per Amazon account/device. There is no support for switching Spotify profiles based on voice profiles, Amazon Household members, or speaker recognition. As a result: All users share one Spotify profile on Alexa Listening history and recommendations are mixed Family plans lose their core benefit on voice assistants This is not an Alexa limitation. Apple Music and Amazon Music both support per-person profile mapping on Alexa. Spotify does not expose profile context via the Alexa integration. This is a design/architecture gap, not a configuration issue, and it has been raised by users for years without resolution. Please escalate this as a feature/architecture issue: Expose Spotify Family/Duo profiles to Alexa and allow profile selection via voice or account context. This is critical for families and a major reason users consider switching away from Spotify.
usable_records.author: Fab_Joe91

usable_records.text: Re: Release Radar messed up Hi there @Hoofmark , Thanks for reaching out! Ultimately, just like other auto-generated playlists, the Release Radar is based on your past music habits. While yes, it includes music from artists you follow, new releases from similar artists are also added in. The more you listen to your favorite content, the better recommendations you'll get. You can save your favorite content by clicking on the plus (+) icon and this will help the suggested playlists/albums to be updated so it can include more personalized results. Keep in mind that the content shown in the Discover section can vary, and it depends on different factors like your recently played content, listening habits and content that other users with similar taste listen to. We also suggest activating a Private Session when you want to listen to the relaxing frequencies you've mentioned. That way the content you'd listened to at that moment wouldn't feed your algorithm. You can find more info on how to activate a Private Session here . If you have any other doubts, just let us know!
usable_records.author: Novy

usable_records.text: Re: Unwanted items in library Hi, @Momma_Jo , The screenshot you've attached actually showcases the Home page, not Your Library. The 8 cards shown at the top are shortcuts to content you've recently interacted with, while the remaining sections on the Home page are usually various recommendations from Spotify which you might like. They aren't necessarily part of Your Library, unless you've manually saved them via the (+) button. This dynamic content is subject to change and frequently updates based on your taste profile . Your Library can be accessed from its dedicated button located on the bottom and it's where you'll find your playlists, saved albums and the artists/shows you follow. 💡 Tip: You can hide certain episode- and playlist suggestions from the Home page by tapping the three dots menu and selecting ' Not interested '. Feel free to give it a try, if you haven't already - just make sure you're running the latest app updates . Podcast suggestion 'Not interested ' button Playlist suggestion 'Not interested ' button Hope this clears things up. The Community is here if there's anything else you need assistance with!
usable_records.author: Yordan

usable_records.text: Ban certain podcasts from recommendation and autoplay As has for example been voiced as a concern by various users in the following board: https://community.spotify.com/t5/Other-Podcasts-Partners-etc/How-do-I-stop-a-podcast-I-don-t-like-from-autoplaying/td-p/5571492 It can be unpleasant to have Spotify's algorithm repeatedly choose to autoplay episodes of a podcast that the user has decided is not in their interest. Even giving a podcast a bad rating does not affect this recommendation and autoplay behaviour and I would propose alongside other users that were bothered by the same issue that an option is implemented in regards to removing certain podcasts from an eligible list for the autoplay algorithm to pick from. This does not have to result in any punishment of the podcast itself, but it should be possible for a user to remove content that they are consciously choosing not to be interested in to appear in proposals and autoplay. Thank you very much for considering this request and patching the app on all OS.

usable_records.text: ability for users to adjust the AI recommendation algorithm At the moment, recommendations are generated automatically, but it would be very useful to give users some control over how those recommendations are shaped. For example, users could choose whether they want suggested tracks to be based more on their listening history, favorite genres, mood, recently played songs, discovery of new artists, or similarity to a specific track or playlist. At the moment, recommendations are generated automatically, but they are often not accurate enough. For example, when selecting “Go to Radio” from a specific song, the tracks in the generated radio often seem to match mainly the song’s release period or year, rather than its actual sound, style, mood, or genre. This can make the listening experience feel less relevant and less personalized than expected. It would be very useful to give users more control over how recommendations are generated. For example, users could choose whether suggested tracks should be based more on sonic similarity, genre, mood, favorite artists, listening history, recently played songs, or music discovery
usable_records.author: mpiras-it

usable_records.text: Restore a permanent Recents row on the desktop Home page The desktop app used to provide quick and reliable access to recently played albums and playlists through a dedicated Recents section on the Home page. In recent versions, the Recents row appears inconsistently and often disappears altogether, making it much harder to return to content that users have recently been listening to. I would like Spotify to restore a permanent, always-visible Recents row on the desktop Home page, similar to the experience offered in the mobile app. This section should remain available regardless of what recommendations, personalised playlists, podcasts, audiobooks or other promotional content Spotify chooses to display elsewhere on the Home page. Many users use the Home page primarily as a starting point for returning to albums and playlists they already know and listen to regularly. Having to search through activity feeds or recommendation modules to find recently used content adds unnecessary friction to a task that should be immediate. Please restore a dedicated, permanently visible Recents row on the desktop Home page.
usable_records.author: hfsbro

usable_records.text: Re: Autoplay activated for Apple TV. Spotify Connect problematic functioning. Hey folks, We appreciate your patience while the team looked into this. The Apple TV app doesn't have a lot of settings at the moment and they're limited, but as we're always looking for ways to improve and offer better functions, there's a possibility that more will be added in the future, so that you'll be able to change the settings directly from there. We really appreciate your feedback on the matter and we've made sure to pass your comments on to the relevant team. @-Karma- the songs that you see happen to be recommended tracks from Spotify which share the same icon as Smart Shuffle recommendations, and this is causing a bit of a confusion since Smart Shuffle is not yet supported on Apple TV. We wanted to share this detail to hopefully clear things up a bit. Once again, thank you both for posting in the forum and should you need help with anything else, we're just a post away. Cheers!

usable_records.text: Channel/Radio Session options I recently came over to Spotify from Pandora sort of reluctantly. I had been with Pandora for 10 years, and I had plenty of reasons I chose to stay that long, but now I had to switch and I have some recommendations of features I really liked on Pandora. When I would listen to the radio function of an artist (my most preffered way of listening to music) I was able to select the mood of the session on the radio. It had roughly 8 functions ranging from Artist only to Relax or Energize and the function worked extremely well. It would play music in that genre or similar to that artist but it could be tailored to how you were feeling. It helped me find new music with Discover and it helped me find music in my favorite genre or by my favorite artists that was maybe more calm and relaxing for when I wanted to study or wind down for the day.
usable_records.author: rgb101125

usable_records.text: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs Please give us the option to exclude AI-generated tracks from: • Taste profile and personalized recommendations • Made For You playlists and Smart Playlists • Radio and autoplay • Release Radar and Discover Weekly I’m not against AI music in principle. But I want to be the one who decides whether I listen to it. Right now, algorithmically generated, factory-produced tracks are quietly mixed into my playlists without any way for me to opt out — and that undermines the trust I place in Spotify’s recommendations. The new AI disclosure in Song Credits is a step in the right direction, but transparency alone isn’t enough. Labeling without a filter option is like putting an allergen on a menu and then telling the kitchen they don’t have to accommodate dietary restrictions. Please make this a user setting — not a workaround, not a maybe, but an actual toggle. The choice of what I listen to should belong to me.
usable_records.author: Herg

metadata.title: Re: Mark / Disable AI Generated Songs
metadata.search_snippet: Please give us the option to exclude AI-generated tracks from: • Taste profile and personalized recommendations • Made For You playlists and Smart Playlists • Radio and autoplay • Release Radar and Discover Weekly I’m not against AI music in principle. But I want to be the one who decides whether I listen to it. Right now, algorithmically generated, factory-produced tracks are quietly mixed into my playlists without any way for me to opt out — and that undermines the trust I place in Spotify’s recommendations. The new AI disclosure in Song Credits is a step in the right direction, but transparency alone isn’t enough. Labeling without a filter option is like putting an allergen on a menu and then telling the kitchen they don’t have to accommodate dietary restrictions. Please make this a user setting — not a workaround, not a maybe, but an actual toggle. The choice of what I listen to should belong to me.

usable_records.text: Incognito Mode for Listening (Exclude from Recommendations Algorithm) You know how sometimes you just want to listen to something once a random genre, a throwback, or even something you don’t normally like without messing up your recommendations? Right now, even a short listening session can influence: DJ suggestions Daily Mixes Release Radar / Discover Weekly Home recommendations Proposed solution: Add an Incognito Mode (similar to private browsing in browsers like Microsoft Edge): When enabled: Listening activity is not used for personalization Songs are not saved to taste profile DJ and algorithm ignore that session Use cases: Exploring new genres without “polluting” your algorithm Playing music for other people (friends, family, kids) One-time moods that don’t represent your taste Testing playlists or songs Why this matters: Spotify’s strength is personalization, but right now there’s no way to protect it . One session can skew recommendations for days. An Incognito Mode would give users control over their algorithm , not just their library.
usable_records.author: Richinpr

usable_records.text: DJ X History & Replay Spotify’s AI DJ is great at creating personalized listening sessions, but currently there is no way to view or revisit previous DJ segments. I would like Spotify to add a DJ History page where users can see previously played DJ sessions and segments. Features could include: • View previously played DJ segments • Replay an entire DJ segment • See which songs were played and which were skipped • Save a full segment to a playlist • Add all songs from a segment to Liked Songs • Rate a segment with a thumbs up or thumbs down to help improve future recommendations Sometimes the AI DJ creates an amazing mix of songs, but if you leave the session or forget a song, there is no easy way to find it again. A DJ History feature would make it easier to rediscover music, revisit favorite sessions, and provide better feedback to improve recommendations. Thank you for considering this idea.
usable_records.author: Mrblueshorts

usable_records.text: Simplify the interface to improve the user experience I would like to share some feedback regarding the Spotify interface, which I use regularly. For some time now, I have noticed a growing sense of visual clutter within the application. Between recommendations, sponsored playlists, podcasts, and other promoted content, navigation sometimes feels less fluid and harder to read. By comparison, some competing platforms offer a more streamlined interface, which improves overall usability and makes it easier to quickly access desired content. In my daily use, this results in: difficulty quickly finding my music or playlists a feeling of overcrowding on the home screen a loss of simplicity compared to what originally made Spotify strong I understand the value of offering recommendations and new content, but an option to simplify or further customize the interface (such as a “minimalist” mode) would be greatly appreciated. Thank you for your attention and for the continuous improvements made to the application.
usable_records.author: sebastien_45
